title: 3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point Clouds

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.
reviews.main_review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.

Main Review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces V2B, a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for 3D single-object tracking in sparse LiDAR point clouds. The core contributions are (1) a shape-aware feature learning framework that fuses global-shape and local-geometric information, and (2) a proposal-free BEV-based localization head. Experiments on the KITTI dataset demonstrate state-of-the-art Success and Precision scores compared to prior trackers.

Strengths  
1. The problem of robust 3D tracking in sparse point clouds is important, and the authors target it directly.  
2. The manuscript is clearly written and well organized, with a thorough description of the network architecture and training procedure.  
3. Motivation for the dual-branch design in both template embedding and shape-aware modules is sound, emphasizing complementary global and local cues.  
4. Quantitative results on KITTI show that V2B outperforms competing methods (e.g., SC3D, P2B) in overall Success and Precision.  
5. The paper includes an ablation study that examines the impact of individual components (e.g., shape-aware module, BEV localization), demonstrating their contributions.

Weaknesses  
1. The claim that V2B better handles sparse subsets of point clouds is not supported by the data in Table 1 vs. Table 2: baselines such as SC3D and P2B actually improve on sparse sequences, whereas V2B’s performance drops.  
2. Although the network uses both global and local branches in the template embedding and the shape-aware feature module, there is no targeted ablation isolating these branches to justify their necessity.  
3. The ablation study is restricted to the car category; it remains unclear if the same trends hold for pedestrian, cyclist, and other classes.  
4. A few phrases (e.g., “stable does not mean accurate” on lines 378–380) are awkward or potentially misleading and should be clarified.  
5. The paper does not discuss limitations in scenarios where a BEV representation may be insufficient, such as indoor or highly cluttered environments.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Provide focused experiments on sparse point-cloud subsets to verify that the shape-aware learning module truly mitigates sparsity issues.  
2. Conduct additional ablations in both the template embedding and shape-aware modules to tease apart the contributions of the global-shape and local-geometric branches.  
3. Extend the ablation studies beyond the car category, covering all four object classes to ensure generality.  
4. Rephrase or elaborate on the claim “stable does not mean accurate” to clarify what is meant by stability versus accuracy.  
5. Include a brief discussion on environments where a BEV-based approach may struggle (e.g., indoor scenes, vertical structures).

Overall Assessment  
V2B is a well-motivated, technically solid contribution that achieves strong empirical performance in 3D single-object tracking. The main architectural innovations are clearly described, and the reported gains over prior art are convincing in the standard KITTI benchmarks. Addressing the suggested experiments and clarifications would further strengthen the validation of key claims. I recommend accepting this paper.  

Rating: 7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.
reviews.main_review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.

Main Review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper introduces a 3D single-object tracker built around two core contributions: (1) a Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning module that fuses template and search volumes using both global and local correlation maps, and (2) a Voxel-to-BEV (bird’s-eye-view) localization pipeline inspired by 3D detection frameworks. The authors demonstrate that their approach yields higher tracking accuracy and lower inference cost compared to recent state-of-the-art methods on standard LiDAR benchmarks.

Strengths  
1. Empirical Gains: Across multiple benchmarks, the proposed method outperforms strong recent trackers by a noticeable margin in both precision and success metrics.  
2. Solid Baseline Comparison: The paper includes a well‐designed baseline and clearly shows how each proposed module contributes to performance gains.  
3. Computational Analysis: A comprehensive evaluation of runtime and resource usage supports claims of practical efficiency.  
4. Ablation Study (Partial): Initial experiments isolate the impact of the shape-aware feature learning and Voxel-to-BEV localization blocks, confirming that both are beneficial.  
5. Feature Expressiveness: Incorporating a training-only point-cloud reconstruction task encourages richer template representations, which appears to strengthen matching accuracy.

Weaknesses  
1. Writing Quality: The manuscript contains numerous grammatical mistakes and several unpolished sentences that hinder clarity.  
2. Template Feature Embedding Complexity: The dual-branch global/local correlation architecture is intricate, yet the paper stops short of ablations that distinguish the contributions of each branch.  
3. Questionable Novelty of Voxel-to-BEV: The localization module closely mirrors existing 3D detection encoders (e.g., PointPillar, SECOND), but the paper does not acknowledge or compare against these works.  
4. Missing Citations: Seminal 3D detection pipelines such as PointPillar and SECOND are not referenced, making it hard to assess the originality of the localization stage.  
5. Lack of Limitations Discussion: The manuscript omits any systematic analysis of failure cases—particularly in re-identification scenarios and tracklet initialization—and does not address potential societal implications (e.g., privacy risks when re-identifying objects).  

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Manuscript Polishing: Thoroughly proofread and rework sections with grammatical errors or awkward phrasing to improve readability.  
2. Detailed Ablations: Add focused experiments that:  
   – Compare global vs. local correlation branches in the Template Feature Embedding network.  
   – Evaluate different distance metrics (cosine, dot-product, etc.) for feature matching.  
3. Related-Work Discussion: Cite and discuss PointPillar, SECOND, and similar 3D detection frameworks to clarify the novelty (or adaptation) of the Voxel-to-BEV module.  
4. Limitations & Failure Modes: Introduce a dedicated section analyzing cases where the tracker fails (e.g., long occlusions, subtle shape changes), and discuss issues around tracklet initialization and re-identification.  
5. Societal Impact Statement: Briefly outline potential ethical or privacy concerns arising from robust 3D tracking in real-world deployments.

Conclusion  
Overall, this work delivers meaningful improvements in 3D tracking accuracy and efficiency through a well-motivated combination of feature learning and voxel-based localization. However, the paper would benefit from clearer writing, deeper ablation studies, proper attribution to prior detection methods, and an explicit discussion of limitations and societal impact. I recommend acceptance with minor-to-moderate revisions to address the points above.

Rating: 6 – Marginally above the acceptance threshold

Summary:
This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.

Main Review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

reviews.summary: This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

Summary:
This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.

Main Review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

reviews.text:
Review for “Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracking on Sparse LiDAR”

Summary  
This paper proposes a Siamese voxel-to-bird’s-eye-view (BEV) network for 3D object tracking in sparse LiDAR point clouds. By learning target and search-region embeddings in a shared BEV space, the method achieves substantial improvements over prior work on the KITTI tracking benchmark, especially for cars and pedestrians. The authors demonstrate clear performance gains, offer detailed visualizations, and plan to release code to facilitate reproducibility.

Strengths  
1. Task relevance and architecture: Tracking sparse 3D point clouds for autonomous driving is a challenging problem, and the Siamese voxel-to-BEV design is logically motivated to leverage spatial structure in LiDAR data.  
2. Strong quantitative results: On KITTI, the method outperforms existing trackers by a significant margin in overall Success and Precision metrics, showing that the approach is effective in practice.  
3. Clarity of presentation: The paper is well organized, with helpful figures illustrating the tracking pipeline and representative outputs.  
4. Commitment to open science: Promising to release code, annotations, and training scripts will greatly benefit future research in this area.

Weaknesses and Concerns  
1. Category sensitivity: Performance on the cyclist class lags behind other categories, with noticeably lower Success and Precision scores. This suggests that the model may be sensitive to limited training samples for rarer classes. It remains unclear whether other sparse categories would suffer similarly under data scarcity.  
2. Generalization: Experiments are limited solely to KITTI and rely on manually tuned hyperparameters (loss coefficients λ1, λ2, λ3, etc.). There is no evidence that the approach would transfer to other datasets or real-world deployments without extensive retuning.  
3. Visualization details: Figure 3 uses a color scheme to distinguish track histories and predictions, but no legend or explanation for the color mappings is provided, making interpretation difficult.  
4. Modality limitations: The paper focuses exclusively on LiDAR input and does not discuss how to incorporate complementary RGB data, which could be valuable for safety-critical scenarios.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Include video demonstrations or animated overlays of tracking outputs to supplement the static BEV figures and better illustrate temporal consistency.  
• Add a brief discussion of potential extensions to multi-modal fusion (RGB + LiDAR), outlining how the Siamese BEV backbone could accommodate image features.  
• Revise Figure 3 to include a clear legend or caption explaining the color coding of ground truth, search regions, and tracked trajectories.  
• Provide practical guidance for adapting the method to new environments, such as recommended ranges for loss-function weights and hyperparameter tuning strategies.

Conclusion  
Overall, this work offers a well-conceived and experimentally validated approach to 3D object tracking using sparse LiDAR. The solid performance gains on KITTI, combined with clear exposition and planned code release, make it a valuable contribution. However, attention to category imbalance, visualization clarity, and generalization will strengthen the paper. I therefore recommend acceptance at a marginal level (Rating: 6 – marginally above the acceptance threshold), subject to minor revisions addressing the points above.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.

Main Review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds. Specifically, it adopts a tracking by detection framework to find the target in the search area, implemented with a siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. To improve feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embeded to the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. In terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal based method, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense BEV voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI dataset with custom split shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods.
reviews.main_review:
Following aspects of the submission can be improved.

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.
The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements.
Another claimed novelty of shape-awre feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty.
Overall, I would suggest authors provide a clear comparison with previous methods that are technically related, and clarify what's inherited from them, and what's changed to better solve an existing problem.

2. Needs better presentation of the method section.
It's hard to follow the method section as tons of technical details are thrown to the readers without providing enough context and motivation of why the architecture is designed so. I would strongly suggest the authors to re-write the method section in the revised submission.

3. More results in the experiments.
There can be more results in the experiments to help readers understand how the model is working. For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist because the proposed method performs much worse in this category compared with SC3D. Also, in Table 3 the speed comparison, the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, however, Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, any reason why this huge gap?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I didn't see specific discussions about the limitations and potential negative societal impact in the submission, please add these in the revised submission.

Summary:
This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds. Specifically, it adopts a tracking by detection framework to find the target in the search area, implemented with a siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. To improve feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embeded to the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. In terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal based method, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense BEV voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI dataset with custom split shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods.

Main Review:
Following aspects of the submission can be improved.

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.
The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements.
Another claimed novelty of shape-awre feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty.
Overall, I would suggest authors provide a clear comparison with previous methods that are technically related, and clarify what's inherited from them, and what's changed to better solve an existing problem.

2. Needs better presentation of the method section.

3. More results in the experiments.

For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist because the proposed method performs much worse in this category compared with SC3D. Also, in Table 3 the speed comparison, the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, however, Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, any reason why this huge gap?

Limitations And Societal Impact:

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces a Siamese tracking‐by‐detection pipeline for 3D LiDAR object tracking. The authors embed a template feature into a dense Bird’s Eye View (BEV) detection head and impose a shape completion regularization loss to improve feature discriminativeness. On the KITTI tracking benchmark, the proposed method outperforms prior state‐of‐the‐art techniques, especially on objects with sparse LiDAR returns.

Strengths  
• Significant empirical gains over previous methods on the KITTI tracking benchmark, demonstrating the effectiveness of the dense BEV detection strategy for sparse‐point objects.  
• Clever combination of Siamese template embedding with a proposal‐free, dense detection head that simplifies the tracking pipeline.  
• Incorporation of a shape completion regularization loss (inspired by SC3D) that enhances the distinctiveness of object features.

Weaknesses  
• The overall framework closely mirrors P2B’s two‐step approach and SC3D’s shape completion regularization, yet the paper does not clearly delineate which components are novel versus inherited.  
• The Method section is difficult to follow: it dives into low‐level technical specifics without first presenting an overarching motivation or architectural overview.  
• Experimental evaluation is incomplete: there are no ablations isolating the introduced shape completion variants, no qualitative tracking examples (particularly for the cyclist category), and no analysis of anomalous performance drops on cyclists.  
• Reported runtime (17 FPS on a TITAN RTX) contradicts P2B’s 40 FPS on a 1080Ti GPU, with no discussion of hardware or implementation differences.  
• The manuscript lacks any discussion of limitations or potential negative societal impacts of the proposed approach.

Detailed Comments and Suggestions  
1. Novelty and Comparison to Prior Art  
   – Quantitatively and qualitatively compare to P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. Clearly state which design choices (e.g., dense BEV head, template embedding strategy) are inherited and which are your original contributions.  
   – In particular, clarify how your detection head differs from P2B’s proposal‐free BEV detector, and what modifications to SC3D’s completion loss were made.  

2. Method Presentation  
   – Begin the Method section with a high‐level overview of the two‐step pipeline. Explain the rationale for using a dense detection head versus proposal‐based detection before detailing network layers and loss functions.  
   – Provide a schematic diagram or flowchart to guide the reader through the main architectural components and their interactions.  

3. Ablations and Qualitative Results  
   – Perform ablation studies isolating the impact of your modified shape completion loss (lines 187–190) versus the original SC3D version. This will justify the added technical complexity.  
   – Include qualitative tracking results for all four object categories in KITTI (car, pedestrian, cyclist, van). In particular, analyze why cyclist performance falls behind SC3D and discuss potential remedies.  
   – Supplement Table 5 (proposal‐based vs. dense detection) with qualitative examples showing where dense detection yields better localization.  

4. Runtime Analysis  
   – Report a detailed runtime profile, specifying batch sizes, pre‐ and post‐processing costs, and hardware configurations. Explain the discrepancy between your 17 FPS on TITAN RTX and P2B’s 40 FPS on 1080Ti to ensure a fair comparison.  

5. Broader Impact and Limitations  
   – Add a dedicated section discussing limitations (e.g., sensitivity to point‐cloud density, failure modes in crowded scenes) and possible negative societal impacts (e.g., safety considerations in autonomous driving deployment).  

Recommendation  
Rating: 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold.  
I find the proposed combination of Siamese template embedding, shape completion regularization, and dense BEV detection to be a promising direction that yields state‐of‐the‐art performance on KITTI. However, to fully validate the contributions and ensure clarity, the authors should strengthen comparisons to prior work, offer targeted ablations and qualitative analyses, clarify runtime discrepancies, and discuss limitations and societal impact. With these revisions addressed, the paper would merit acceptance.

reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper presents a Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for 3D object tracking in point clouds. The core idea is to combine (1) a global and local template matching strategy in a Siamese framework, (2) a shape-completion network to enrich sparse point-cloud targets, and (3) a voxel-to-BEV localization module that performs center regression without relying on proposals. Experiments show promising quantitative results, but the overall novelty is somewhat limited since the approach largely integrates established components (template matching akin to SiamRPN [1], shape completion, and BEV localization similar to LaserNet [2]).

Strengths  
• Empirical Performance: The method yields competitive tracking accuracy on standard benchmarks, demonstrating that the combination of global/local matching, shape completion, and BEV regression can be effective in practice.

Weaknesses  
• Limited Novelty: Each key component—Siamese template matching, shape completion, and BEV-based localization—closely resembles prior work (in particular, SiamRPN for template matching and LaserNet for BEV regression). As a result, the paper’s main contribution lies in the architectural assembly rather than in fundamentally new algorithms.  
• Missing Ablation Studies: The manuscript does not include ablation experiments isolating the contributions of the global versus local template matching branches. Without these, it is difficult to assess which component drives the performance gains.

Detailed Comments and Suggestions  
1. Novelty Clarification  
   The paper would benefit from a clearer articulation of what aspects are novel. For instance, is the specific integration of global and local matching into a unified Siamese framework a novel design, or is it a straightforward extension of existing work? Clarifying this will help readers appreciate the paper’s unique angle.  

2. Ablation Analysis  
   I strongly recommend conducting ablation studies that disable or vary the global and local template matching modules independently. Reporting performance when only one branch is active would quantify each module’s contribution and justify the architectural choices.  

3. Technical Details  
   - Please provide more insight into how the shape-completion network is trained and how its output interfaces with the BEV localization network.  
   - Elaborate on any implementation tricks or training schedules that were critical to achieving the reported results.  
   - A brief discussion comparing runtime and computational cost with baseline methods (e.g., SiamRPN, LaserNet) would also strengthen the empirical evaluation.

Conclusion  
The proposed Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker demonstrates promising results by combining global and local template matching, shape completion, and BEV-based center regression. However, the limited novelty—stemming from the reuse of known components—and the absence of ablation studies leave open questions about the true impact of each module. With additional experiments clarifying these points, the paper would make a clearer contribution.  

Recommendation: 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold (weak accept).  

References  
[1] SiamRPN: “High-Speed Tracking with Fully Convolutional Siamese Networks,” CVPR 2018.  
[2] LaserNet: “LaserNet: An Efficient Probabilistic 3D Object Detector for Autonomous Driving,” CVPR 2019.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines
reviews.main_review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines

Main Review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.
reviews.main_review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.

Main Review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.

4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a Siamese voxel-to-bEV framework for localizing sparse 3D point-cloud objects by decomposing the 3D center estimation task into a 2D bird’s-eye view (BEV) localization plus a separate z-axis regression. The technical write-up is clear and the idea of mapping voxel features to a BEV plane is well explained. However, despite its novel framing, the submission falls short of a complete multi-object tracking (MOT) solution, lacks sufficient comparison to prior art, and provides only limited empirical validation. In its current form, I recommend rejection (Rating: 4 – Ok but not good enough).

Strengths  
• The manuscript is very well structured, and the technical sections are easy to follow.  
• The core Siamese voxel-to-BEV architecture is described clearly, including network layers and loss terms.  
• Decomposing 3D center estimation into a 2D BEV prediction plus a z-axis regressor is an innovative modeling choice that could offer computational advantages.

Weaknesses  
• Tracking scope is ill-defined. It is unclear whether the method is meant for single-object or multi-object scenarios, and there is no discussion of how to handle track initiation, termination, or occlusions.  
• The pipeline reads more like a 3D object detector with a re-identification head than a full MOT system. Essential modules for maintaining track identity (birth/death of tracks, occlusion management) are missing.  
• The related-work section is too narrow, omitting key 2D and 3D MOT benchmarks such as MOTChallenge, JRMOT, EagerMOT, and VoteNet-based trackers.  
• No new theoretical contribution is offered; the model reuses existing network backbones and attention modules with only minor adaptations.  
• Experiments are limited to KITTI with a custom “Success/Precision” metric rather than standard MOT metrics like MOTA or HOTA, making it hard to compare fairly to the literature.  
• The method’s applicability to non-rigid objects (e.g., human tracking) is neither explained nor evaluated. Datasets such as JRDB, nuScenes, or Waymo are not considered.  
• There is no discussion of method limitations or broader societal and ethical implications, which is increasingly expected for tracking work.

Detailed Comments and Suggestions  
1. Clarify MOT Scope  
   – Specify whether your system targets single or multiple concurrent objects.  
   – Describe how track initialization (birth), termination (death), and occlusion handling are performed in your framework or integrate off-the-shelf modules that address these tasks.  

2. Broaden Related Work  
   – Include classic and state-of-the-art 2D and 3D MOT methods (e.g., MOTChallenge, JRMOT, EagerMOT) and VoteNet-based trackers.  
   – Position your contribution relative to these baselines, highlighting any concrete advantages.  

3. Articulate Theoretical Novelty  
   – If there is any core algorithmic insight beyond architectural tweaks, clearly state it. Otherwise, explain why your adaptation of existing modules constitutes a significant advance.  

4. Strengthen Empirical Validation  
   – Evaluate on the KITTI tracking test set using standard metrics (MOTA, HOTA, IDF1) for fair comparison.  
   – Extend experiments to other benchmarks (JRDB for human motion, nuScenes or Waymo for diverse driving scenarios) to demonstrate generality.  

5. Address Non-Rigid Objects  
   – Provide details on how you define and update templates for deformable targets (e.g., pedestrians).  
   – Present results or failure cases to clarify your method’s limits.  

6. Discuss Limitations and Ethics  
   – Add a section on current limitations (e.g., scale, occlusions) and potential failure modes.  
   – Briefly discuss societal impacts, privacy concerns, or safety considerations associated with 3D point-cloud tracking.

In summary, while the Siamese voxel-to-BEV idea is promising and well communicated, the paper lacks crucial tracking components, comprehensive benchmarking, and any substantial theoretical advance. Rejection is recommended until these issues are addressed.

weaknesses: Unclear whether the framework handles single-object or multi-object tracking

Framework resembles a 3D detection method rather than a full tracking pipeline

Does not address non-rigid object tracking (e.g., humans) or test on datasets like JRDB, nuScenes, or Waymo
No discussion of limitations or societal/ethical impact

Integrate or describe tracking modules for initiation, termination, and occlusion handling
Expand related work to include established 2D and 3D MOT baselines (e.g., MOTChallenge, JRMOT, EagerMOT)
Demonstrate any theoretical innovation or clearly articulate novelty over existing tools

Test on additional datasets (JRDB for humans, nuScenes, Waymo) to show generality
Discuss how non-rigid object templates (e.g., humans) are defined and tracked
Include a limitations section and address potential societal or privacy impacts

Template definition for non-rigid objects (e.g., humans) is not explained
Comparisons omit VoteNet-based 3D tracking methods and 2D MOT benchmarks

title: Generalization of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning Algorithms: Recurring and Unseen Tasks

reviews.summary: The paper analyzes the generalization error of MAML in 2 scenarios that are different in the new tasks at test time which are (i) the training tasks and (ii) unseen tasks, under the assumptions that the loss function is (strongly) convex and the number of steps of SGD in the adaptation step of MAML is 1. In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.
reviews.main_review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

(quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos: 
* Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
* Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
* Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
* Lines 286 and 287: “k” should be “K”.
* Title of section 3.3: “Task” is capitalized.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.

Main Review:

(quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos:

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper offers significant theoretical advances in understanding the generalization properties of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML). The authors introduce a novel stability notion for meta-learning that explicitly depends on the number of samples per task and exploit it to derive tighter generalization bounds scaling as 1/(m n) instead of the 1/m dependence in prior work. They further extend their analysis to unseen tasks by bounding the transfer gap via the total variation distance between training and test task distributions. The results are clearly presented, and the manuscript is generally well-structured and well-written. Overall, the paper makes meaningful contributions to the theory of MAML, warranting a recommendation just above the acceptance threshold.

Strengths  
1. The paper defines a new stability metric for MAML that captures the role of per-task sample size n, yielding sharper generalization guarantees.  
2. It improves on existing bounds by achieving a 1/(m n) dependence, which is strictly tighter than the 1/m results in prior meta-learning analyses.  
3. The authors propose a clean approach to characterize generalization to unseen tasks via the total variation distance between task distributions, a novel angle in this context.  
4. The theoretical developments advance our conceptual understanding of how MAML scales with both the number of tasks m and the number of examples per task n.  
5. The manuscript is organized in a logical manner, with clear explanations and motivating discussions.

Weaknesses  
1. The entire analysis rests on the assumption of strongly convex losses, whereas in practice MAML is most often applied to deep neural networks with highly non-convex objectives. This raises questions about the scope of applicability of the results.  
2. Adaptation is restricted to exactly one SGD step, limiting the relevance to real-world MAML implementations that typically use multiple inner-loop updates.  
3. It remains unclear how the proposed bounds translate to practical performance when these restrictive assumptions are relaxed, and whether the theoretical insights carry over to standard deep learning settings.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Include a discussion—either in the main text or in an appendix—on the challenges and potential strategies for extending the analysis to multiple inner-loop SGD steps.  
2. Comment on whether and how the convexity assumption might be relaxed, or at least provide guidance on the key technical obstacles in moving to non-convex losses.  
3. Carefully proofread the manuscript to correct the minor typographical and notation errors listed below.

Minor Comments and Typos  
– Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded” should read “bounded.”  
– Line 196: citation “from [1]” should be “from [24].”  
– Line 218: “deferentially private” ➔ “differentially private.”  
– Lines 286–287: lowercase “k” should be uppercase “K.”  
– Title of Section 3.3: “Task” should not be capitalized.

Recommendation  
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold. The paper presents valuable theoretical insights into MAML generalization. While the restrictive convexity and single-step adaptation assumptions limit immediate applicability, the core contributions are solid and merit publication after addressing the suggested clarifications and typo fixes.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper analyzes the generalization error of MAML in 2 scenarios that are different in the new tasks at test time which are (i) the training tasks and (ii) unseen tasks, under the assumptions that the loss function is (strongly) convex and the number of steps of SGD in the adaptation step of MAML is 1. In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.

Main Review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

(quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos: 
* Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
* Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
* Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
* Lines 286 and 287: “k” should be “K”.
* Title of section 3.3: “Task” is capitalized.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
