reviews.summary: This paper analyzes the generalization bound of MAML-type algorithms from two aspects: (1) when the test task is a sample from the training tasks and (2) when the test task is unseen during training stage. A generalization bound on strongly convex case is given in case (1) showing the generalization error is deceasing at a rate of $O(1/mn)$. On the other hand, a bound under the total variation assumption is given in case (2), showing the bound depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task. The paper focuses on theory and thus no experiments are included.
reviews.main_review:
Overall I think this is a good paper, because it provides the generalization bound in terms of data points in the homogeneous case, and a total variation based analysis in the heterogeneous case. I didn't check all the proof line by line, but the key results make sense to me and are, to my best knowledge, new in the literature.

Regarding the technical parts, I have two concerns:

1. The authors state in line 292 Remark 2 that in the analysis of recurring tasks case, strong convexity is assumed because "The algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting." This seems a little off to me, because algorithmic stability has been studied in several previous works. For instance, Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.01240.pdf), (London, 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.06617.pdf), (Zhang et al., 2020; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11875.pdf), etc. It seems the divergence comes from a different notion of stability proposed in the paper, which captures the number of data points per task. But I don't see how such definition would prevent the analysis on non-convex problems. Could you elaborate why the non-convexity assumption is challenging based on the stability notion in your case? Maybe an example would help.

2. A minor concern: In many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms, a bilevel structure is established (which is closer to how we do meta-learning in reality). For instance (Ji et al., 2020;https://arxiv.org/pdf/2006.09486.pdf) analyzes the convergence, etc. I wonder how the results in this paper would adapt/be applicable to the bilevel case? In bilevel programming, the stability analysis is not new(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.04188.pdf), do you see any challenge there?

**Updates**
I've read the author response and I think the authors adequately answered my question regarding difficulty in relaxing the results to the non-convex case. I encourage the authors to include the discussion into the paper so as to make it clear.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: No limitation or potential negative societal impact is shown.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

This paper analyzes the generalization bound of MAML-type algorithms from two aspects: (1) when the test task is a sample from the training tasks and (2) when the test task is unseen during training stage. A generalization bound on strongly convex case is given in case (1) showing the generalization error is deceasing at a rate of $O(1/mn)$. On the other hand, a bound under the total variation assumption is given in case (2), showing the bound depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task. The paper focuses on theory and thus no experiments are included.

Main Review:
Overall I think this is a good paper, because it provides the generalization bound in terms of data points in the homogeneous case, and a total variation based analysis in the heterogeneous case. I didn't check all the proof line by line, but the key results make sense to me and are, to my best knowledge, new in the literature.

Regarding the technical parts, I have two concerns:

1. The authors state in line 292 Remark 2 that in the analysis of recurring tasks case, strong convexity is assumed because "The algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting." This seems a little off to me, because algorithmic stability has been studied in several previous works. For instance, Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.01240.pdf), (London, 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.06617.pdf), (Zhang et al., 2020; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11875.pdf), etc. It seems the divergence comes from a different notion of stability proposed in the paper, which captures the number of data points per task. But I don't see how such definition would prevent the analysis on non-convex problems. Could you elaborate why the non-convexity assumption is challenging based on the stability notion in your case? Maybe an example would help.

2. A minor concern: In many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms, a bilevel structure is established (which is closer to how we do meta-learning in reality). reviews.text:
Review for “On the Generalization of MAML: Stability and Beyond”

Summary  
This paper presents a theoretical investigation into the generalization properties of Model‐Agnostic Meta‐Learning (MAML). In the recurring‐task setting and under the assumption of strong convexity, the authors derive an O(1/(m n)) generalization bound that depends explicitly on the number of tasks m and per‐task sample size n. For unseen tasks, they further introduce a bound based on average total variation between data distributions. Central to their analysis is a novel stability definition that measures how the meta‐learner’s output changes when K + 1 points in both the support and query sets are altered. To my knowledge, both the O(1/(m n)) bound and the total variation bound for unseen tasks are new contributions to the meta‐learning literature.

Strengths  
1. The O(1/(m n)) generalization bound for recurring tasks in the strongly convex regime fills a gap in our understanding of how meta‐sample sizes jointly affect performance.  
2. The total variation–based bound for unseen tasks is a clever extension that connects distributional shift to meta‐generalization.  
3. The technical development is rigorous, and the novel stability notion—distinct from prior definitions in Hardt et al. (2016), London (2017), and Zhang et al. (2020)—is well motivated and clearly presented.  

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Strong Convexity Assumption  
   • The current analysis relies critically on strong convexity (see Remark 2, line 292) to establish the proposed stability guarantee. However, many practical meta‐learning objectives (e.g., deep networks) are nonconvex. I recommend the authors elaborate on why their stability framework resists extension to nonconvex losses—perhaps with a simple illustrative example—and discuss whether any relaxation (e.g., PL conditions) might be feasible.  
2. Lack of Empirical Illustration  
   • While the theoretical results are compelling, the paper contains no experiments or simulations to illustrate the bounds or highlight their tightness in practice. Even a brief empirical section—showing how the derived rates manifest on synthetic quadratic tasks or a small neural‐network experiment—would greatly strengthen the paper’s impact.  
3. Applicability to Bilevel Meta‐Learning  
   • The manuscript focuses on a single‐level analysis of MAML but does not clarify how these generalization results carry over to the bilevel optimization viewpoint common in recent work (e.g., Ji et al. 2020; stability in bilevel formulations [arXiv:2106.04188]). A dedicated discussion on adapting the current bounds or stability definition to bilevel meta‐learners would help readers bridge the gap to more realistic algorithmic settings.  

Additional Minor Points  
– It may help to more explicitly contrast your stability definition (which perturbs K + 1 points in S_in and S_out) with the leave‐one‐out definitions in prior works.  
– A short paragraph in the conclusion outlining possible routes to nonconvex or bilevel extensions would guide future efforts.  

Decision: 6 – Marginally above the acceptance threshold  
Overall, this paper makes a valuable theoretical contribution by deriving new generalization bounds for MAML under both recurring and unseen task regimes. The results are novel, clearly presented, and technically sound. Addressing the convexity assumption, adding even minimal empirical validation, and discussing bilevel extensions would elevate the work further. I recommend acceptance pending minor revisions.

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance.
reviews.main_review:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. My detailed comments are given as below.

Pros:
1.	This paper is well written and easy to follow. I find that the notations, definitions, assumptions and related works in this paper are clear and the explanation for the main results are clear and reasonable. 
2.	Studying the generalization for MAML type of algorithms is very important, and this paper takes a good try to provide generalization error and stability bounds for MAML under various settings. 
3.	This paper shows that the sample number for the intra-task training is also important and can be used to further reduce the generalization error. 

Cons:
1.	My first concern is the tightness of the upper bound. It is true that the dependence on $mn$ (i.e., the total number of training sample) is optimal for the strongly convex objective function (in fact, this is a  standard result in statistical learning where the optimal dependence of the excess risk on the sample size M is $O(M)$). However, it is not clear whether other parameters (even some constants) are still optimal for the authors’ upper bounds, e.g., the authors do not prove the optimality of the test sample size $K$. In addition, it seems to me that the results in this paper are not surprising (even though the derivations can be different due to the MAML structure), i.e., the bounds decrease with the total number $mn$ of the training samples (as we always see in standard supervised learning). I will be more excited if the derived upper bounds can involve some special components caused by the composite structure of MAML or the upper bounds capture some interplay between the training and the test procedures of the meta training process of MAML. In Chen et al., 2020 (as pointed out by the authors), they provide interesting findings on how the support/query split used in the meta learning (including MAML) affects the generalization (as well as the theoretical generalization bounds). However, I do not see much new things for MAML in the generalization bounds in this paper. 
2.	My another concern is that the empirical verification is not sufficient. First, the curves in the experiments contain  large variations, and I suggest the authors can run more seeds and do an average to make the results convincing. Second, the authors only verify the impact of sample sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. Since the sample size $K$ of the test data is also important, I suggest an experiment to test its impact on the generalization. Third, the authors provide a generalization bound for an unseen task, which increases with the total variation distance between tasks’ distributions. Therefore, it would be better if the authors can provide experiments to verify whether such a total variation distance metric really captures the generalization error. If so, this will be a good contribution because it may provide guidelines for how to improve the generalization of meta-learning based on the task selection.  Fourth, since the supervised classification is an important application for meta-learning, it would be better if an experiment can be done for it.
3.	The  training error characterization mainly follows from  exiting works e.g., Fallah et al., 2020, and the stability analysis is somewhat based on Bousquet & Elisseeff, 2020. Can the authors clarify here what is challenging when extending to the MAML training? 

In summary, I think this paper takes a good step to study the generalization of MAML, and it involves some novel characterizations, e.g., eliminating the dependence on $K$ for the total variation distance characterization via the maximum coupling in Hollander, 2012. Although the derivations in this paper may contain new treatments, I do not find the results interesting or exciting and the experiments are not sufficient to verify the generalization of MAML in broader applications. To attract more people form this meta-learning area,  I feel these bounds are not sufficient and more efforts should be taken, e.g., to show that such total variation bound has a tight impact on the generalization of MAML. Therefore, I tend to weakly reject this paper, but I am open to increase my score based on the authors’ feedback and other reviewers’ comments.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance.

Main Review:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. My detailed comments are given as below.

Pros:
1.	This paper is well written and easy to follow. I find that the notations, definitions, assumptions and related works in this paper are clear and the explanation for the main results are clear and reasonable. 
2.	Studying the generalization for MAML type of algorithms is very important, and this paper takes a good try to provide generalization error and stability bounds for MAML under various settings. 
3.	This paper shows that the sample number for the intra-task training is also important and can be used to further reduce the generalization error. 

Cons:
1.	My first concern is the tightness of the upper bound. It is true that the dependence on $mn$ (i.e., the total number of training sample) is optimal for the strongly convex objective function (in fact, this is a  standard result in statistical learning where the optimal dependence of the excess risk on the sample size M is $O(M)$). However, it is not clear whether other parameters (even some constants) are still optimal for the authors’ upper bounds, e.g., the authors do not prove the optimality of the test sample size $K$. In addition, it seems to me that the results in this paper are not surprising (even though the derivations can be different due to the MAML structure), i.e., the bounds decrease with the total number $mn$ of the training samples (as we always see in standard supervised learning). I will be more excited if the derived upper bounds can involve some special components caused by the composite structure of MAML or the upper bounds capture some interplay between the training and the test procedures of the meta training process of MAML. In Chen et al., 2020 (as pointed out by the authors), they provide interesting findings on how the support/query split used in the meta learning (including MAML) affects the generalization (as well as the theoretical generalization bounds). However, I do not see much new things for MAML in the generalization bounds in this paper. 
2.	My another concern is that the empirical verification is not sufficient. First, the curves in the experiments contain  large variations, and I suggest the authors can run more seeds and do an average to make the results convincing. Second, the authors only verify the impact of sample sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. Since the sample size $K$ of the test data is also important, I suggest an experiment to test its impact on the generalization. Third, the authors provide a generalization bound for an unseen task, which increases with the total variation distance between tasks’ distributions. Therefore, it would be better if the authors can provide experiments to verify whether such a total variation distance metric really captures the generalization error. If so, this will be a good contribution because it may provide guidelines for how to improve the generalization of meta-learning based on the task selection.  Fourth, since the supervised classification is an important application for meta-learning, it would be better if an experiment can be done for it.
3.	The  training error characterization mainly follows from  exiting works e.g., Fallah et al., 2020, and the stability analysis is somewhat based on Bousquet & Elisseeff, 2020. Can the authors clarify here what is challenging when extending to the MAML training? 

In summary, I think this paper takes a good step to study the generalization of MAML, and it involves some novel characterizations, e.g., eliminating the dependence on $K$ for the total variation distance characterization via the maximum coupling in Hollander, 2012. Although the derivations in this paper may contain new treatments, I do not find the results interesting or exciting and the experiments are not sufficient to verify the generalization of MAML in broader applications. To attract more people form this meta-learning area,  I feel these bounds are not sufficient and more efforts should be taken, e.g., to show that such total variation bound has a tight impact on the generalization of MAML. Therefore, I tend to weakly reject this paper, but I am open to increase my score based on the authors’ feedback and other reviewers’ comments.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper presents a uniform-stability and generalization analysis for the Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) algorithm. For recurring tasks drawn from the same distribution, the authors derive an O(1/(m n)) generalization bound under strong convexity, where m is the number of training tasks and n is the number of intra-task samples. For unseen tasks, they obtain a bound that grows with the total variation distance between the new task distribution and the training distributions, using maximal coupling to eliminate explicit dependence on the test batch size K. A small toy experiment in the supplement illustrates how m and n influence generalization in practice.

Strengths  
1. The manuscript is very well written. Notation, definitions, assumptions, and related work are laid out clearly, making the technical development easy to follow.  
2. Addressing the question of generalization and stability in MAML is important, as theoretical understanding in meta-learning remains limited.  
3. The results highlight in a precise way how increasing the intra-task sample size n directly reduces the generalization error, reinforcing an intuitive point with a clean bound.

Weaknesses  
1. Beyond the O(1/(m n)) dependence, it is unclear whether the upper bounds are tight. In particular, the paper does not establish optimality or lower bounds with respect to the test-time batch size K or other constants hidden in the big-O notation.  
2. The analysis, while technically sound, yields results that seem unsurprising and do not reveal any new phenomena specific to MAML’s composite structure or the support/query split that practitioners use.  
3. The empirical evaluation is too limited to fully support the theoretical claims. The toy experiment shows high-variance learning curves with only a few random seeds, and there is no study of how varying K impacts generalization in practice.  
4. Although the bound for unseen tasks depends on total variation distance, there is no empirical validation that this metric actually correlates with generalization performance across different target tasks.  
5. The experiments are confined to the toy regression setting. Important supervised classification benchmarks commonly used in meta-learning are missing, leaving open the question of broader applicability.  
6. The paper does not clearly articulate what new technical obstacles arise when extending classic training-error and stability proofs (e.g., Bousquet & Elisseeff uniform stability) to the nested MAML update structure.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Provide, if possible, theoretical lower bounds or matching constructions to justify the dependence on K and other constants, or at least discuss why this remains an open challenge.  
2. Increase the number of random seeds in the toy experiments, and report averaged results with confidence intervals to reduce variance in the learning curves.  
3. Add controlled experiments that vary the test batch size K to empirically demonstrate its effect on generalization, verifying (or refuting) the claim that K does not appear in the bound.  
4. Design experiments to measure total variation distance between task distributions and plot it against actual generalization error, to validate the relevance of the unseen-task bound.  
5. Incorporate at least one standard supervised classification benchmark (e.g., mini-ImageNet or CIFAR-FS) to show that the theoretical findings carry over to more realistic meta-learning scenarios.  
6. In the related work or a dedicated technical section, explicitly identify and discuss the novel challenges encountered when adapting convergence and stability analyses from single-task learning to the bi-level MAML setting.

Overall Assessment  
The paper tackles a timely problem and delivers a clean, well-presented theoretical treatment of stability and generalization for MAML. However, the lack of tightness results, limited empirical validation, and absence of MAML-specific insights hold the work back from being fully convincing. With the suggested theoretical clarifications and more comprehensive experiments, the contribution would be significantly strengthened. Given the importance of the topic and the solid foundation laid here, I recommend a borderline acceptance.

Rating: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance.

Main Review:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. My detailed comments are given as below.

Pros:
1.	This paper is well written and easy to follow. I find that the notations, definitions, assumptions and related works in this paper are clear and the explanation for the main results are clear and reasonable. 
2.	Studying the generalization for MAML type of algorithms is very important, and this paper takes a good try to provide generalization error and stability bounds for MAML under various settings. 
3.	This paper shows that the sample number for the intra-task training is also important and can be used to further reduce the generalization error. 

Cons:
1.	My first concern is the tightness of the upper bound. It is true that the dependence on $mn$ (i.e., the total number of training sample) is optimal for the strongly convex objective function (in fact, this is a  standard result in statistical learning where the optimal dependence of the excess risk on the sample size M is $O(M)$). However, it is not clear whether other parameters (even some constants) are still optimal for the authors’ upper bounds, e.g., the authors do not prove the optimality of the test sample size $K$. In addition, it seems to me that the results in this paper are not surprising (even though the derivations can be different due to the MAML structure), i.e., the bounds decrease with the total number $mn$ of the training samples (as we always see in standard supervised learning). I will be more excited if the derived upper bounds can involve some special components caused by the composite structure of MAML or the upper bounds capture some interplay between the training and the test procedures of the meta training process of MAML. In Chen et al., 2020 (as pointed out by the authors), they provide interesting findings on how the support/query split used in the meta learning (including MAML) affects the generalization (as well as the theoretical generalization bounds). However, I do not see much new things for MAML in the generalization bounds in this paper. 
2.	My another concern is that the empirical verification is not sufficient. First, the curves in the experiments contain  large variations, and I suggest the authors can run more seeds and do an average to make the results convincing. Second, the authors only verify the impact of sample sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. Since the sample size $K$ of the test data is also important, I suggest an experiment to test its impact on the generalization. Third, the authors provide a generalization bound for an unseen task, which increases with the total variation distance between tasks’ distributions. Therefore, it would be better if the authors can provide experiments to verify whether such a total variation distance metric really captures the generalization error. If so, this will be a good contribution because it may provide guidelines for how to improve the generalization of meta-learning based on the task selection.  Fourth, since the supervised classification is an important application for meta-learning, it would be better if an experiment can be done for it.
3.	The  training error characterization mainly follows from  exiting works e.g., Fallah et al., 2020, and the stability analysis is somewhat based on Bousquet & Elisseeff, 2020. Can the authors clarify here what is challenging when extending to the MAML training? 

In summary, I think this paper takes a good step to study the generalization of MAML, and it involves some novel characterizations, e.g., eliminating the dependence on $K$ for the total variation distance characterization via the maximum coupling in Hollander, 2012. Although the derivations in this paper may contain new treatments, I do not find the results interesting or exciting and the experiments are not sufficient to verify the generalization of MAML in broader applications. To attract more people form this meta-learning area,  I feel these bounds are not sufficient and more efforts should be taken, e.g., to show that such total variation bound has a tight impact on the generalization of MAML. Therefore, I tend to weakly reject this paper, but I am open to increase my score based on the authors’ feedback and other reviewers’ comments.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The paper provides a generalization analysis of MAML. Based on a new definition of stability of meta-learning, it characterizes the generalization error on seen and unseen tasks.
reviews.main_review:
Please address the following concerns:
- The results in Theorem 2 and Corollary 2 both show that the generalization error increases for a larger K (the number of shots). This conflicts with the experiment results in MAML or other meta-learning papers. So I have a major concern about whether the bound is vacuous. Moreover, it would be better to empirically verify the effect of K. 
- The theoretic results of Theorem 2, i.e., the generalization error of seen tasks, are not empirically studied. It would also better to provide some simple experiments to verify it.
- The equation in line 569 seems not so straightforward. The loss of the i-th task is not only affected by the replacement of K data points in $S_i^n$, but also affected by the replacement of data points in $S_j^n (j \neq i)$. So I wonder whether expectations of $w_S$ and $w_\tilde{S}$ are the same.

****************************************************After Rebuttal**********************************************
- While I am satisfied with the response to the assumption of strong convexity and smoothness, I am still not convinced by the response to my major concern regarding $K$. 
     - When $K$ is small which is usually the case in meta-learning, this bound is as loose as SGD. In this case, why do we need meta-learning?
     - Only when $K$ is as large as possible (Appendix E), the error decreases as $K$ increases. In fact, according to the bound, if the total number of samples across all tasks is $1M$, $K$ has to be even as large as $\sim10,000$, which is rarely practical in meta-learning.

If the latest discussion by the authors can be included, I tend to increase the score to "weakly accept".

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper provides a generalization analysis of MAML. Based on a new definition of stability of meta-learning, it characterizes the generalization error on seen and unseen tasks.

Main Review:
Please address the following concerns:
- The results in Theorem 2 and Corollary 2 both show that the generalization error increases for a larger K (the number of shots). This conflicts with the experiment results in MAML or other meta-learning papers. So I have a major concern about whether the bound is vacuous. Moreover, it would be better to empirically verify the effect of K. 
- The theoretic results of Theorem 2, i.e., the generalization error of seen tasks, are not empirically studied. It would also better to provide some simple experiments to verify it.
- The equation in line 569 seems not so straightforward. The loss of the i-th task is not only affected by the replacement of K data points in $S_i^n$, but also affected by the replacement of data points in $S_j^n (j \neq i)$. So I wonder whether expectations of $w_S$ and $w_\tilde{S}$ are the same.

****************************************************After Rebuttal**********************************************
- While I am satisfied with the response to the assumption of strong convexity and smoothness, I am still not convinced by the response to my major concern regarding $K$. 
     - When $K$ is small which is usually the case in meta-learning, this bound is as loose as SGD. In this case, why do we need meta-learning?
     - Only when $K$ is as large as possible (Appendix E), the error decreases as $K$ increases. In fact, according to the bound, if the total number of samples across all tasks is $1M$, $K$ has to be even as large as $\sim10,000$, which is rarely practical in meta-learning.

If the latest discussion by the authors can be included, I tend to increase the score to "weakly accept".

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The assumption of strong convexity and smoothness is very strong and does not hold for most neural networks. The authors have not fully addressed such limitation.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a novel stability‐based framework for analyzing the generalization performance of Model‐Agnostic Meta‐Learning (MAML) on both seen and unseen tasks. By introducing a new definition of stability tailored to meta‐learning, the authors derive theoretical generalization bounds (Theorem 2 for seen tasks and Corollary 2 for unseen tasks), characterizing the meta‐generalization error in terms of algorithmic parameters and data sizes.

Strengths  
1. Novel Stability Definition: The paper proposes an original and well‐motivated notion of stability specifically designed for the meta‐learning setting.  
2. Rigorous Theoretical Bounds: The authors provide clear generalization bounds for both seen and unseen tasks, extending classical stability arguments to the meta‐learning regime.  
3. Formal Results: Theorems and corollaries are stated cleanly, and the proofs, while technical, follow a logical progression that elucidates how meta‐algorithm parameters influence the final error.

Weaknesses  
1. Unintuitive Dependence on Shot Number K: The primary bound scales as O((1 + αLK)/(mn)), which means that as the number of shots K increases, the bound actually worsens. This stands in direct conflict with empirical observations in MAML literature, where more shots typically improve performance.  
2. Vacuous for Few‐Shot Regimes: In the small‐K regime (e.g., K = 1, 5), which is the focus of few‐shot learning, the bound degrades to the same order as standard SGD. This calls into question whether the meta‐learning framework provides any theoretical advantage under realistic settings.  
3. Unrealistic Assumptions: The analysis relies on µ‐strong convexity and L‐smoothness of the loss. Such assumptions rarely hold for modern deep neural networks, limiting the practical applicability of the results.  
4. Questionable Step in Derivation (Line 569): The argument that E[w_S] equals E[w_ẐS] after replacing K points in one task’s dataset ignores the fact that losses on task i could depend indirectly on replacements performed in other tasks S_j for j ≠ i. This cross–task dependency needs to be justified or corrected.  
5. Lack of Empirical Validation: The paper does not include experiments that investigate the effect of varying K on generalization. Without empirical evidence, it remains unclear how tight or meaningful the derived bounds are in practice.

Detailed Comments & Suggestions  
– Empirical Study of K: I strongly recommend running simple experiments that vary the shot count K and measure the actual generalization error. Plotting these curves alongside the theoretical bound would clarify whether the O((1+αLK)/(mn)) scaling is an artifact of the analysis or reflects a real phenomenon.  
– Validate Theorem 2: A small‐scale experiment on seen tasks (e.g., a toy regression or classification setup) could illustrate how well Theorem 2 predicts the generalization gap as a function of m, n, and K.  
– Clarify Line 569: Please revisit the derivation step where you replace K points in S_i and claim equality of expectations. Either explicitly account for cross‐task influences or provide a more detailed argument showing independence.  
– Discuss Large-K Behavior: The current discussion notes that only for K on the order of 10^4 does the bound begin to shrink below the SGD baseline. Please incorporate this observation into the main text, and reflect on its implications for meta‐learning practice.  
– Mitigate Strong Convexity Assumptions: At minimum, discuss how one might relax µ‐strong convexity and L‐smoothness, or outline potential avenues (e.g., local convexity approximations) to extend the analysis to non‐convex neural networks.

Recommendation  
Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)  
This work offers an interesting and rigorous stability perspective on MAML’s generalization, filling a gap in the meta‐learning theory literature. However, the restrictive assumptions, counterintuitive dependence on K, and absence of empirical validation limit its immediate impact. I recommend acceptance provided the authors address the above concerns—particularly by clarifying the questionable derivation, empirically examining the shot‐number scaling, and discussing ways to relax the convexity assumptions.

reviews.summary: The authors present generalization bounds for MAML when test tasks are the same as the ones seen during training (recurring tasks) or when they are different but sampled from the same distribution (unseen tasks). The main takeaways are a. for recurring tasks, the excess population loss is bounded by O(1/mn) with m the number of tasks and n the number of data points, and b. generalization to unseen tasks depends on the distance (total variation) between train and test task distributions.
reviews.main_review:
The results of the paper are novel and of interest to the multi-task/meta-learning community. In particular, the generalization result for recurring tasks (Th. 1 & 2) refines multi-tasks bounds for MAML when data is limited. This is important since limited data is often the main motivation for these types of meta-learning methods. While the approach to get those bounds builds on the stability of SGD, the proposed stability definition is tailored to MAML and lends itself to further study and extensions. The second result (Th. 3) directly targets transfer to new tasks, formalizing one of the main intuitions behind MAML: the closer the train and test tasks, the most effective the transfer. To the best of my knowledge, this is the first work directly assessing generalization for MAML when train and test task sets are disjoint.

It helps that the paper is clearly written and introduces all necessary background to MAML. For each theorem, assumptions are clearly stated and the results are discussed from both a theoretical and practical perspective. Although a bit dense, I enjoyed reading this paper.

The main limitation of the paper is convexity of the meta-learning loss — but similar publications also considered this setting. Specifically, I would like to see the assumptions on the fast-adaptation learning rate (alpha) relaxed, since they are rarely met in practice.

Finally, I wished the related work section were a bit more expansive. For example, issues of generalization were also considered in [1], and [2] discussed issues related to the number of samples per tasks. How does the present work differ?

References:

[1] "Global Convergence and Generalization Bound of Gradient-Based Meta-Learning with Deep Neural Nets", Wang et al., ArXiv 2020.

[2] "How to distribute data across tasks for meta-learning?", Cioba et al., ArXiv 2021.

reviews.text:
Review for “Generalization Bounds for Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning”

Summary  
This paper derives novel finite-sample generalization bounds for Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) in two distinct regimes. First, for recurring tasks drawn from the same distribution seen during training, the authors establish an O(1/(m n)) excess population loss bound—refining classic multi-task rates in the limited-data setting by leveraging a new stability argument. Second, for unseen but related tasks, they prove a transfer bound that scales with the total variation distance between the training task distribution and the test task distribution. Both results rest on a carefully tailored notion of (γ,K)-uniform stability for meta-learning, and the proofs are clearly laid out with explicit convexity, smoothness, and step-size assumptions.

Strengths  
1. Novel recurring-task bound: The O(1/(m n)) rate underlines how meta-learning can sharply improve sample efficiency when tasks are sampled repeatedly, refining prior multi-task analyses in the few-shot regime.  
2. First disjoint-task generalization: By formalizing transfer in terms of total variation distance, the paper offers the first rigorous treatment of how MAML generalizes to completely new tasks.  
3. Tailored stability definition: The proposed (γ,K)-uniform stability captures both the number of tasks and inner-loop sample perturbations, which is well suited to MAML’s two-level optimization.  
4. Clarity and completeness: Background on MAML is sufficient for non-experts, all technical assumptions (e.g., strong convexity of the meta-loss, smoothness constants L, μ, ρ, G, and α-step-size bounds) are explicitly stated, and the flow from definitions to Theorems 1–3 is logical.  
5. Balanced discussion: Despite the density of theoretical material, the authors interweave practical insights, making the significance of the bounds to few-shot learning clear.  
6. Strong motivation: The focus on few-shot and rapid adaptation is well aligned with pressing challenges in meta- and transfer-learning.

Weaknesses  
1. Convexity assumption: Relying on strong convexity of the meta-objective prevents direct application to the nonconvex neural-network settings where MAML is most often employed.  
2. Restrictive step-size (α) conditions: The requirement that α ≤ min{1/(2L), μ/(8ρG)} is quite stringent and may not hold in typical deep-learning scenarios.  
3. Related work coverage: The discussion omits several recent contributions on global generalization in meta-learning and sample-distribution divergence (e.g., Wang et al. 2020, Cioba et al. 2021), making it hard to position this work relative to the latest literature.  
4. Missing societal impact: A brief section reflecting on potential risks or broader impacts of deploying meta-learning systems is absent.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Investigate whether the fast-adaptation step-size constraints can be relaxed or replaced with more permissive conditions that better reflect practice.  
• Expand the related work section to compare and contrast with Wang et al. (2020), Cioba et al. (2021), and other recent sample-distribution bounds, clarifying incremental versus novel contributions.  
• Include a discussion on how the proposed stability framework might extend—or where it may break down—when the meta-loss is nonconvex, to guide future theoretical extensions.  
• Add a brief societal impact statement, addressing responsible use of meta-learning methods in safety-critical or high-stakes applications.

Recommendation  
Rating: 7 (Good paper, accept)  
Overall, this manuscript makes a significant theoretical contribution by delivering the first direct generalization guarantees for both recurring and unseen tasks in MAML. Although the strong convexity and step-size assumptions limit immediate applicability to deep models, the core stability-based analysis opens multiple avenues for relaxation and extension. With modest revisions to broaden the related work discussion and address the noted limitations, this work will be a valuable addition to the meta-learning literature.

Meta-loss strong convexity and smoothness under α≤min{1/(2L),μ/(8ρG)}
References to Theorems 1&2 for recurring tasks and Theorem 3 for unseen tasks
Mention of convex vs. nonconvex limitations and the challenge of extending stability arguments

reviews.summary: This paper studies the generalization properties of MAML in the strongly convex setting. It's assumed that there are $m$ training tasks, each with $n$ samples, and the inner loop adaptation is one step of SGD using $K$ samples. It's proved that if the new task is a uniformly random task from one of the $m$ training tasks, MAML incurs a generalization error of $O(\frac{K}{mn})$; if the new task is an unseen task, the error depends on the TV distance between the new task distribution and all the training task distributions as well as their average. The analysis is based on a modified version of algorithmic stability tailored to the MAML setup.
reviews.main_review:
Originality: The contribution of this paper is new. The paper obtains an $O(1/mn)$ bound in the strongly convex setting, while the previous result in [25] is $O(1/m)$ (though [25] covers non-convex losses). The proof technique is based on algorithmic stability but also needs nontrivial modification.

Quality: The paper is technically sound. The results are clearly stated and discussed.

Clarity: The paper is clearly written and well organized.

Significance:
- I think the most interesting result is the $O(1/mn)$ error bound for "in-task generalization", i.e., generalizing to a random training task. The previous bound for MAML is only $O(1/n)$ in [25]. To obtain the $O(1/mn)$ bound, a new type of stability (Definition 3) is required, which is non-trivial.
- However, I believe the result of generalization to an unseen task (Theorem 3) is very loose and does not capture the actual interesting aspects of meta-learning. Essentially, for this bound to be small it requires the new task distribution to be very close in TV distance to all the training tasks as well as their average. However, this is almost never the case. For example, suppose that all the tasks are drawn i.i.d. from a meta-distribution over tasks (which is usually true in a lot of few-shot learning benchmarks). Then $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{m+1}$ could all be very different from each other, but we can still expect MAML to generalize well to $p_{m+1}$, since it's a task drawn from the same meta-distribution. The bound provided in the current paper dramatically fails to capture this.
- The strong convexity is certainly very restrictive, and it's noted that the technique used in the paper cannot generalize beyond the convex case. That said, this is understandable and acceptable as a first step for theoretical purpose. 

Other questions:
- The paper only considers 1-step SGD adaptation in the inner loop. Is there any hope to generalize the result to multiple steps?
- It seems that a somewhat non-standard data splitting procedure is used, i.e. first splitting the data into $S_i^{in}$ and $S_i^{out}$ each with size $n$, and then the samples used for inner and outer loops are sampled from these two subsets. How important is this in the theoretical analysis and is it unavoidable?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: As noted in the main review, I believe the generalization result to an unseen task is very loose and can be misleading. A discussion of this is needed.

reviews.text:
Review for “A Stability-Based O(1/(m n)) Generalization Guarantee for MAML under Strong Convexity”

Summary  
This paper studies the generalization behavior of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) in the strongly convex setting. The authors introduce a novel (γ,K)-uniform stability definition tailored to MAML and use it to prove an O(K/(m·n)) bound on the generalization error for recurring tasks (m meta‐training tasks each with n samples). They further extend the analysis to unseen tasks, deriving a bound that scales with the total variation (TV) distance between the new task distribution and the training-task distributions. Overall, the analysis is technically sound, clearly presented, and improves upon prior O(1/m) or O(1/n) rates, but it comes with restrictive assumptions and a loose, potentially misleading unseen-task guarantee.

Strengths  
1. Originality and Theoretical Improvement: The paper delivers the first tight O(K/(m·n)) generalization bound for MAML under strong convexity, refining the dependence on both the number of tasks m and the number of samples per task n.  
2. Rigorous Analysis: The stability-based proof is carefully constructed, with clear statements of lemmas and theorems. The modified (γ,K)-uniform stability notion is nontrivial and well motivated.  
3. Clarity and Organization: The manuscript is well organized, the notation is consistent, and the proofs are presented in a clear, accessible way.  
4. Novel Stability Definition: Introducing a stability concept specific to meta‐learning is an interesting contribution that could inspire further work on stability-based bounds for other meta-learning algorithms.

Weaknesses  
1. Loose Unseen-Task Bound: The generalization guarantee for a new task depends on ∑i TV(p_{m+1},p_i) and TV(p_{m+1},average p_i). In realistic meta-distributions, these TV-distance terms can be large, rendering the bound vacuous or misleading without further refinement.  
2. Restrictive Assumptions: The strong convexity requirement on the loss severely limits the applicability of the results to common MAML applications in nonconvex settings (e.g., deep networks).  
3. Single-Step Inner Loop: The analysis is restricted to one-step SGD in the inner loop, whereas practical implementations of MAML often use multiple adaptation steps.  
4. Nonstandard Data Splitting: Each task dataset is split into Sin and Sout of equal size n. It is unclear why this particular splitting is necessary and whether the bounds hold under more conventional sampling schemes.  
5. Potential Misinterpretation: Without a thorough discussion of the limitations, readers may overestimate the practical impact of the unseen-task generalization bound.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Discuss Limitations of Unseen-Task Bound: Include a dedicated section analyzing when the TV-distance terms become large and how that affects the usefulness of the bound in practice.  
2. Extend to Multi-Step Adaptation: Explore whether the stability framework and resulting rates can be generalized to multiple inner-loop SGD steps, or at least outline the main technical challenges.  
3. Clarify Data Splitting: Provide intuition or justification for the Sin/Sout split. Could the same results be obtained by sampling with replacement or by using different proportions?  
4. Model Meta-Distribution Effects: Rather than relying on worst-case TV-distance, consider alternative measures that capture similarity within a meta-distribution (e.g., Wasserstein distance or moment-based divergences).  
5. Relax Strong Convexity: Discuss possible pathways to extend the analysis to weakly convex or nonconvex losses, even if only conjecturally or under additional assumptions.

Decision: 4: OK but not good enough—Reject  
While the paper makes a solid theoretical contribution by establishing an improved O(1/(m·n)) bound for recurring tasks and introducing a novel stability definition, its practical impact is hampered by the restrictive strong convexity assumption, loose unseen-task guarantee, and limited scope of one-step adaptation. I thus recommend rejection in its current form.

title: Stabilizing Deep Q-Learning with ConvNets and Vision Transformers under Data Augmentation

reviews.summary:
This paper studies the problem of high-variance Q-targets under data augmentation, and proposes a method to reduce the variance of the targets called SVEA. The key idea contains three parts: (1) Apply augmentation to the Q-value estimation of the current state and not the next state Q-targets for bootstrapping, and (2) modify the objective to include both the targets using augmented and original observations and (3) optimize the actor using only the unaugmented data for actor-critic algorithms, with parameter sharing. 

The paper performed experiments in DeepMind control suite and its variants (with raw pixel inputs). Compared to other data augmentation methods (e.g. DrQ) their proposed SVEA method is robust to the type of data augmentation and is more sample efficient with a better final performance. Some ablations were also presented among the above components to indicate the usefulness of all components. Generalization performance was demonstrated in the DistractingCS and robotic manipulation environments. The method also appears to help when using a Vision Transformers architecture.
reviews.main_review:
**Originality**: The proposed framework can be seen as a generalization of existing data augmentation approaches, by considering combinations of both augmented and unaugmented data in the loss function. The related work section is adequately touching upon the several areas which this framework can be viewed from. 

**Quality**: There is no theoretical analysis for the proposed framework, but the paper presents several empirical results to back up the claims, including ablations on the various components. However, I would suggest backing up the claim on Pitfall 1: Non-deterministic Q-target with some empirical data as well. How bad is the variance with non-deterministic Q-targets in practice? With SVEA, even though we have deterministic Q-targets, we still have noisy predictions due to the data augmentation. So there can still be comparison of variance in the case of combination of augmented and unaugmented current state and next state. 

For the alpha and beta hyperparameter: it was mentioned that 0.5/0.5 works well for the experiments. Have the authors tried varying this parameter throughout the training (i.e. some sort of annealing schedule), and whether that hinders or helps the performance even more?

**Clarity**: The paper is overall fairly clear to read. 

**Significance**: Given the recent popularity of applying data augmentation in vision-based reinforcement learning agents, I believe that the proposed technique, which is simple to implement, can be easily incorporated as a toolbox of RL practitioners/researchers. Demonstrating that this framework still scales to ViT architectures is also promising. It would be nice to have some more theoretical justification for the framework by the original authors, but perhaps that could be a follow up work (potentially by others).
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes, the authors have adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.summary:
(Auxiliary Review only -- the authors need not respond to this).

This work proposes issues with data augmentation in RL and proposes a couple of techniques to address those issues. It applies augmentation only on the current state and not future state to address erroneous bootstrapping. It also proposes a Q objective that uses both the augmented and un-augmented data. Also, for actor critic experiments, the actor is optimised only with un-augmented data and learn a  policy that is general via sharing parameters between the networks. These are simple techniques that help the paper demonstrate good results in a range of tasks.

This is a clear paper with good set of experiments. The problem has been described clearly and the work proposes simple refinements to the data augmentation strategies used in RL. While I am convinced of the quality of the method itself and the experiments, this work could have benefitted from more rigour with the baselines to make a stronger case for the method. Hence, I am offering a score of 6 noting that the paper is marginally above acceptance threshold.
reviews.main_review:
Note : * This is an AUXILIARY REVIEW only. The authors are not expected to respond to this review or any of the concerns presented here. * *

The paper is well-written, clear and provides a good description of the experiments. It clearly sets up the problem that standard data augmentation methods suffer from. It also provides empirical solutions to these problems and backs up these claims with experimental results. Even though the paper justifies the claims only empirically based on experimentation, the ideas proposed here have made simple and easily extendable modifications, which can be very useful in complex state action spaces when RL+Data augmentation is employed. The ideas proposed here are pretty simple and straightforward, but I am not convinced these methods alone provide us a full-fledged framework for data augmentation as claimed. Also, any theoretical justification provided with this work is not rigorous enough to demonstrate that these methods are universal for all data augmentation problems.  On the novelty front, the paper's key contribution seems to be the idea of using un-augmented inputs to the targets. There are two ways to look at this -- just as a simple innovation being not novel enough on its own or trying to understand how the work arrives at this simple innovation and whether the innovation helps improve the algorithm performance over a wide variety of tasks. I am more inclined towards the latter. I believe this is still a useful enough contribution to practitioners and researchers alike, where a few simple modifications help greatly stabilize performance.

My major concern with the paper was the hyper-parameters and architectures used for the baselines. It looks like the adapted baselines were handicapped a bit by architectural changes and these baselines were not tuned to optimality, or at least it was not very evident that this was done thoroughly. This takes a bit of the shine off the impressive results presented by the paper and I am not sure if tuning the parameters would change any relative comparison between those baselines and this method.

The paper's proposed methods are good and the results are useful on their own to the community. Having said that, because of the other minor issues mentioned earlier, I am not able to give a higher score than 6 for this paper.

reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper tackles the instability and over-regularization issues that arise when applying data augmentation in reinforcement learning. The authors observe that standard augmentation of both the current and successor states can inflate target variance and degrade performance. To address this, they propose two simple refinements: (1) apply augmentation only to the current state when computing Q-targets, leaving the successor (bootstrapped) state un-augmented, and (2) redefine the Q-learning objective to jointly train on both augmented and clean inputs. In actor-critic settings, they further stop-gradient the policy updates from augmented data and share parameters across both streams. Empirically, these modest changes yield improved stability and performance across a suite of RL benchmarks.

Strengths  
1. The paper is very well written, with a clear problem statement and concise description of the proposed methods.  
2. The core ideas—using un-augmented targets and combining data streams—are simple yet effective, making them easy to implement and extend.  
3. Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent gains in stability and final performance over standard augmentation baselines.  
4. The approach is conceptually general and could be applied in more complex state–action spaces or other augmentation schemes.

Weaknesses  
1. The manuscript lacks a rigorous theoretical foundation: there is no formal analysis of why limiting augmentation to the current state universally reduces variance or how the weighted objective guarantees better convergence.  
2. From a novelty standpoint, the contribution is limited to a small tweak (using un-augmented inputs for targets) rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic framework.  
3. It is unclear whether the competing baselines were tuned optimally; suboptimal hyperparameters or architectures may have handicapped their performance.  
4. The generality of the framework across different augmentation types (beyond the specific transforms tested) and RL settings remains insufficiently explored.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Provide theoretical analysis or illustrative proofs (e.g., variance bounds or regret guarantees) that clarify under what conditions the proposed refinements are expected to yield benefits.  
• Thoroughly tune baseline methods—both their augmentation parameters and network architectures—to ensure a fair comparison.  
• Include additional experiments showing that the performance gap persists even when baselines are carefully optimized, thereby strengthening the empirical claims.  
• Discuss the applicability of your framework to a wider variety of augmentation functions and RL domains, possibly with a small ablation study on different transforms or task complexities.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Overall, this paper offers a clear, easy-to-adopt modification to data augmentation in RL that leads to consistent empirical improvements. While the theoretical underpinnings and novelty breadth could be deepened, the practical impact and quality of the empirical study justify a marginal acceptance. I therefore recommend acceptance with a rating of 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold).
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
(Auxiliary Review only -- the authors need not respond to this).

This work proposes issues with data augmentation in RL and proposes a couple of techniques to address those issues. It applies augmentation only on the current state and not future state to address erroneous bootstrapping. It also proposes a Q objective that uses both the augmented and un-augmented data. Also, for actor critic experiments, the actor is optimised only with un-augmented data and learn a  policy that is general via sharing parameters between the networks. These are simple techniques that help the paper demonstrate good results in a range of tasks.

This is a clear paper with good set of experiments. The problem has been described clearly and the work proposes simple refinements to the data augmentation strategies used in RL. While I am convinced of the quality of the method itself and the experiments, this work could have benefitted from more rigour with the baselines to make a stronger case for the method. Hence, I am offering a score of 6 noting that the paper is marginally above acceptance threshold.

Main Review:
Note : * This is an AUXILIARY REVIEW only. The authors are not expected to respond to this review or any of the concerns presented here. * *

The paper is well-written, clear and provides a good description of the experiments. It clearly sets up the problem that standard data augmentation methods suffer from. It also provides empirical solutions to these problems and backs up these claims with experimental results. Even though the paper justifies the claims only empirically based on experimentation, the ideas proposed here have made simple and easily extendable modifications, which can be very useful in complex state action spaces when RL+Data augmentation is employed. The ideas proposed here are pretty simple and straightforward, but I am not convinced these methods alone provide us a full-fledged framework for data augmentation as claimed. Also, any theoretical justification provided with this work is not rigorous enough to demonstrate that these methods are universal for all data augmentation problems.  On the novelty front, the paper's key contribution seems to be the idea of using un-augmented inputs to the targets. There are two ways to look at this -- just as a simple innovation being not novel enough on its own or trying to understand how the work arrives at this simple innovation and whether the innovation helps improve the algorithm performance over a wide variety of tasks. I am more inclined towards the latter. I believe this is still a useful enough contribution to practitioners and researchers alike, where a few simple modifications help greatly stabilize performance.

My major concern with the paper was the hyper-parameters and architectures used for the baselines. It looks like the adapted baselines were handicapped a bit by architectural changes and these baselines were not tuned to optimality, or at least it was not very evident that this was done thoroughly. This takes a bit of the shine off the impressive results presented by the paper and I am not sure if tuning the parameters would change any relative comparison between those baselines and this method.

The paper's proposed methods are good and the results are useful on their own to the community. Having said that, because of the other minor issues mentioned earlier, I am not able to give a higher score than 6 for this paper.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.main_review:
Disclaimer: Emergency review with all the short-comings that comes with.

# Originality

While the idea of data-augmentation has been extensively used in both supervised and reinforcement learning, this paper proposes an approach that in some ways generalizes the DrQ approach, and in others simplifies it. In my view the modifications, though subtle, are novel.

# Quality

The work produces an extensive array of empirical results showing reobust generalization and impressive sample-efficiency. The comparisons to DrQ lack specification of the hyperparamters K and M, which can easily be added. If, as I suspect, the comparison is with DrQ[K=1, M=1], it would be nice to see a comparison to the default DrQ[K=2, M=2] and/or at the very least DrQ[K=2, M=1], which matches the number of forward passes through the Q-network.

Similarly, in the empirical demonstration of Pitfall 2 (Figure 1) it would be nice to see the equivalent plot for various values of K.

Finally, I would slightly quibble with the claim of "theoretically grounded" on line 30, as this paper does not feature any rigorous theoretical statements.

# Clarity

Overall the paper is very clearly and well written. The only major point of contention is that DrQ is parameterized by a K and M which control how many samples the target Q-values and TD loss, respectively, are averaged over. It would be good to specify which is being used here. It sounds like [K=1, M=1] (?) but I would think the fairest comparison would be [K=2, M=1].


## Minor points

- The targets on line 215 and line 9 of Algorithm 1 presumably look different in continuous control cases, which is the main focus of the experimental section.
- Very minor point: would the expressions not be equivalent but simpler if the trade-off was simply alpha and (1 - alpha), instead of beta?
- The experimental setup is slightly unclear in the text, copying the caption of Figure 3 into the main body would help. In particular, the sentence "all methods are trained for 500k frames and evaluated on the full set of tasks".
- Figure 6 (left) needs error-bars.

# Significance

Data-augmentation seems to be a very simple technique for improving the sample-efficiency and generalizability of RL agents. This is particularly important for real-world applications where interactions with the environment can be expensive and the sim-to-real gap can be prohibitive.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I did not see any mention of limitations. The authors mentioned the growing footprint of large models and how this work could help negotiate that.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces a simple yet broadly applicable modification to off-policy Q-learning algorithms that dramatically reduces the variance of Q-targets under data augmentation and thereby stabilizes learning. Rather than augmenting both current and successor states (as done in prior work), the proposed method only augments the current state and jointly trains on augmented and unaugmented data. This subtle change avoids over-regularization, retains sample efficiency, and yields strong generalization across a range of continuous-control benchmarks. The authors substantiate their claims with extensive experiments demonstrating improved sample efficiency and robustness, and they frame their approach as a generalization and simplification of the DrQ algorithm.

Strengths  
• Novel, broadly applicable modification: The core idea—augment only the current state in the target computation—is simple to implement yet can be plugged into any Q-learning pipeline.  
• Reduced Q-target variance and improved stability: Empirical results (e.g., learning curves) clearly show that the proposed approach lowers the variance of bootstrapped targets and yields smoother, more stable training dynamics.  
• Extensive empirical evaluation: The authors conduct a comprehensive suite of experiments on standard continuous-control tasks, demonstrating both faster learning and better generalization under environment shifts. Results are consistent across multiple domains.  
• Clear generalization of DrQ: By framing their method as a subtle simplification of DrQ, the authors both honor prior work and provide new insights into the role of multi-sample augmentation.  
• Well-written and organized: The paper is generally clear, with a logical flow from motivation through theory, algorithm, and experiments.

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Clarify DrQ comparisons (K and M hyperparameters)  
   • Weakness: The paper reports comparisons against DrQ but omits the specific values of its two key hyperparameters—K (number of target samples) and M (number of TD-loss samples).  
   • Suggestion: Explicitly report which (K, M) setting was used in all DrQ baselines. Furthermore, include ablations against the common defaults DrQ[K=2, M=2] and/or DrQ[K=2, M=1] to ensure a fair, apples-to-apples comparison.  

2. Empirical demonstration of Pitfall 2 across K values  
   • Weakness: Figure 1 highlights the so-called “Pitfall 2” (over-regularization) only for a single choice of K.  
   • Suggestion: Extend this analysis by plotting the Pitfall 2 curve for multiple K values. This will better illustrate how the proposed method avoids over-regularization across the full hyperparameter sweep.  

3. Theoretical grounding claim  
   • Weakness: The paper asserts it is “theoretically grounded,” yet does not provide rigorous proofs or formal statements.  
   • Suggestion: Either remove or tone down the phrase “theoretically grounded,” or add a concise but precise theoretical statement (with proof sketch or pointer to an appendix) that justifies the variance-reduction property.  

4. Clarity in continuous-control target expressions  
   • Weakness: The description of target Q-value expressions in the continuous-control setting (line 215 and Algorithm 1, line 9) is somewhat opaque.  
   • Suggestion: Clarify these expressions, possibly with a brief derivation in the appendix.  

5. Notation simplification  
   • Weakness: The current notation uses coefficients β and γ to weight augmented versus unaugmented targets.  
   • Suggestion: Simplify this to more intuitive symbols α and (1 − α), which may help readers follow the weighting scheme more naturally.  

6. Experimental setup clarity  
   • Weakness: The caption of Figure 3 describes the training/test environment shifts in detail, but this information is not fully echoed in the main text.  
   • Suggestion: Copy or summarize the Figure 3 caption into the main text to ensure the experimental protocol is clear without flipping pages.  

7. Presentation of results (error bars)  
   • Weakness: In Figure 6 (left), learning curves are averaged over five seeds but lack error bars, obscuring the variance across runs.  
   • Suggestion: Add error bars or shaded confidence intervals to all aggregate plots to better convey statistical significance and reproducibility.

Minor Comments  
– Overall the paper is well-structured; addressing the suggestions above will further solidify its clarity and rigor.  
– All methods are trained for 500K environment frames, which is appropriate and matches standard practice.  

Recommendation  
I recommend acceptance (rating: 7 – Good paper, accept). This work presents a compelling, easy-to-implement modification that meaningfully improves the stability and sample efficiency of off-policy RL under data augmentation. With the clarifications and ablations suggested above, the paper would be an even stronger contribution to the community.
