title: On Optimal Interpolation in Linear Regression

reviews.summary: This paper is concerned with inducing a certain type of best possible interpolator in linear regression. It is based on the notion of best possible interpolator, defined in Muthukumar et al., 2019., which is the best estimator (i.e., with minimum risk) among the class of interpolators. Based on that, the authors define a new theoretical estimator, the best estimator among interpolators that are also linear in the response variable.  They manage to induce a closed form of this estimator and prove that it is the limit of preconditional gradient descent under a specific initialization. In addition, using approximations for quantities that are usually unknown in practice (e.g.  population covariance matrix, signal to noise ratio), they turn this theoretical estimator into a fully empirical one and compare its performance to other frequently used estimators, showing that this estimator performs arbitrarily better in certain cases. Finally, they extend this notion of best possible theoretical estimator to the random features setting, where they again induce a closed form for this estimator and prove that it is the limit of preconditioned gradient descent under a specific initialization.
reviews.main_review:
The paper is interesting to read. It belongs to the long list of papers that are concerned with the overparametrized estimation, but instead of trying to explain why this type of estimation generalizes so well, it aims to find the best linear interpolator in the linear regression setting. By restricting the class of possible estimators in the definition by Muthukumar et al., 2019, they manage to turn a theoretical device into a fully observable estimator. They also define a similar notion of optimal estimator for a more complex model and induce a closed form of this estimator, something that may suggests that we could try to induce this type of optimal estimators for even more complex model like neural networks. There is also an interesting example, where we can see that a frequently used interpolator, the minimum-norm interpolator, has arbitrarily bad generalization. Finally, these examples combined with the results related to preconditional gradient descent highlight nicely the importance of initialization.

The paper could be of greater significance if
a) the construction of the fully-empirical estimator would not be restricted to the isotropic regime
b) (as they note) it included a precise guarantee on the approximation error of the estimation of the covariance matrix Σ

Summary:
This paper is concerned with inducing a certain type of best possible interpolator in linear regression. It is based on the notion of best possible interpolator, defined in Muthukumar et al., 2019., which is the best estimator (i.e., with minimum risk) among the class of interpolators. Based on that, the authors define a new theoretical estimator, the best estimator among interpolators that are also linear in the response variable.  They manage to induce a closed form of this estimator and prove that it is the limit of preconditional gradient descent under a specific initialization. In addition, using approximations for quantities that are usually unknown in practice (e.g.  population covariance matrix, signal to noise ratio), they turn this theoretical estimator into a fully empirical one and compare its performance to other frequently used estimators, showing that this estimator performs arbitrarily better in certain cases. Finally, they extend this notion of best possible theoretical estimator to the random features setting, where they again induce a closed form for this estimator and prove that it is the limit of preconditioned gradient descent under a specific initialization.

Main Review:
The paper is interesting to read. It belongs to the long list of papers that are concerned with the overparametrized estimation, but instead of trying to explain why this type of estimation generalizes so well, it aims to find the best linear interpolator in the linear regression setting. By restricting the class of possible estimators in the definition by Muthukumar et al., 2019, they manage to turn a theoretical device into a fully observable estimator. They also define a similar notion of optimal estimator for a more complex model and induce a closed form of this estimator, something that may suggests that we could try to induce this type of optimal estimators for even more complex model like neural networks. There is also an interesting example, where we can see that a frequently used interpolator, the minimum-norm interpolator, has arbitrarily bad generalization. Finally, these examples combined with the results related to preconditional gradient descent highlight nicely the importance of initialization.

The paper could be of greater significance if
a) the construction of the fully-empirical estimator would not be restricted to the isotropic regime
b) (as they note) it included a precise guarantee on the approximation error of the estimation of the covariance matrix Σ
c) instead of performing an empirical analysis for special cases, they could manage to prove a theoretical result that bounds the generalization error of the fully-empirical estimator w.r.t. the generalization error of the theoretical optimal response-linear achievable interpolator.

In addition, I think that some points in the beginning of the proof of Proposition 1 could be written with more clarity.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper investigates the class of response‐linear interpolators in overparameterized linear regression, deriving a closed‐form “optimal response‐linear achievable” (ORLA) solution and demonstrating that, under specific initialization, preconditioned gradient descent converges exactly to this ORLA interpolator. The authors then transform this theoretical construct into a practical estimator by approximating the data covariance and signal‐to‐noise ratio, and further extend their analysis to a random‐features regression setting. They also exhibit situations where the usual minimum‐norm interpolator can generalize arbitrarily poorly, highlighting the critical role of initialization and implicit bias in overparameterized models.

Strengths  
1. The manuscript introduces a novel theoretical estimator restricted to response‐linear interpolators and provides its closed‐form solution.  
2. It rigorously shows that this ORLA interpolator arises as the limiting point of preconditioned gradient descent with a specific zero‐data initialization, thereby elucidating a clear implicit‐bias mechanism.  
3. The authors successfully convert the theoretical ORLA device into a fully empirical estimator by proposing practical approximations for the covariance matrix Σ and the signal‐to‐noise ratio.  
4. The framework is elegantly extended to random‐features regression, yielding analogous closed‐form characterizations and bias analyses in that setting.  
5. By constructing an explicit example, the paper convincingly demonstrates that the standard minimum‐norm interpolator can perform arbitrarily worse, underscoring the value of the proposed approach.  
6. The work underscores the often‐underappreciated importance of initialization choice on generalization in overparameterized learning.

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Restriction to isotropic priors.  
   • Currently the fully empirical estimator is developed only under an isotropic prior assumption. It would substantially strengthen the contribution to generalize the estimator’s construction (and associated analysis) to more general covariance structures.  
2. Approximation error guarantees.  
   • While practical covariance approximations are proposed, the paper does not supply any precise bounds on the error ‖Σ̂–Σ‖ or its effect on the estimator’s risk. I recommend incorporating explicit theoretical guarantees on the quality of the covariance estimate and its impact on performance.  
3. Generalization bounds for the empirical estimator.  
   • The theoretical ORLA estimator is characterized thoroughly, but there is no bound comparing the generalization error of the empirical version to that of the ideal ORLA. Providing such a comparison would clarify how approximation and finite‐sample effects degrade optimality.  
4. Clarity in proofs.  
   • The opening steps of the proof of Proposition 1 are hard to follow. A more detailed, step‐by‐step exposition there would benefit readers.  
5. Minor technical issues.  
   • Reference accuracy: The citation to Mourtada (line –64) does not in fact concern interpolators. Please correct or replace this reference.  
   • Notation in expectations (line –129): clarify whether the subscript denotes conditioning or marginal expectation.  
   • Line 132: rephrase the sentence beginning “In linear regression (when d>n), minimizing the…” for grammatical clarity.  
   • Equation (17) (line –480): the derivation would read more naturally if you began with a general W in the admissible class before specializing.  

Overall Assessment  
The paper makes a solid theoretical contribution by identifying and characterizing the optimal response‐linear interpolator, linking it to implicit bias in preconditioned gradient methods, and demonstrating its practical relevance via an empirical estimator and a random‐features extension. Although the current empirical analysis is confined to the isotropic regime and lacks detailed error bounds, these limitations do not overshadow the originality and technical merit of the work. With the suggested clarifications and extensions, this study will be a valuable addition to the literature on overparameterized learning and implicit regularization.

Recommendation: 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold.

reviews.summary: This paper studies the generalization error of a class of interpolator in linear regression and identifies conditions for optimality. They consider a setting where there is a true linear model generating the data, whose parameters are drawn from a Gaussian distribution with zero-mean and covariance Phi. In this setting they consider a natural class of estimators that are a function of the data, the population covariance matrix, the prior covariance matrix Phi and the signal to noise ratio. Among this class of estimators they calculate a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator, with respect to the expected excess risk and show that there are certain settings where the expected excess risk of this estimator can be much lower than the risk of the OLS solution. They also provide an extension of their results to the random features model.
reviews.main_review:
The authors study an interesting problem on a topic of much recent interest---benign overfitting of linear models. While the assumptions made on the problem are pretty stringent, the authors provide a clean exposition of their results which I appreciated as a reader. 

Here are some questions/comments about the paper:

1. The authors mention the results by Amari et al. (2021), who they mentioned studied estimators whose asymptotic bias or variance is optimal. How do the techniques and setting used in this paper differ from theirs? Can the techniques used there also be used here but instead to balance the bias and the variance optimally? A discussion regarding this would be helpful to the reader. 

2. On ln 91 the authors say an interesting question that they study here is if minimum norm interpolant is always close to optimal, and show that this is not the case. But I think this question has already partially been answered given past work of Bartlett et al. (2020). Given their results, it is easy to construct cases, for example, when population covariance matrix is isotropic, where the minimum norm interpolant is not consistent but the ridge regression solution is. So, I would urge the authors to provide some more nuanced context and discussion here.

3. Regarding the assumptions both the noise xi and the true parameter w* are assumed to be drawn from a Gaussian distribution. Can this be relaxed? Further, can the results of this paper (Proposition 1 in particular) be extended to hold in the case where w* is not mean zero?

4. In the experiments described in Section 4.1, where w_O is approximated by w_{O_e}, what is the structure of the population covariance matrix? Is it isotropic or close to it? Or does it have a heavy tailed structure similar to one that was shown to be necessary by Bartlett et al. for the minimum norm solution to be consistent? Further, empirically, was the estimate Sigma_e close to Sigma? A plot to verify this would be nice.


=================== Post rebuttal comments =================== 

Firstly, I would like to thank the authors for their very carefully response to the questions raised by all of the reviewers. I continue to support acceptance of this paper post the rebuttal period. In my view, I think it would be nice if the authors incorporate the many helpful suggestions raised in all of the reviews in a subsequent version.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The authors do a good job of describing the potential negative impact of their work.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper investigates the generalization performance of a broad class of response-linear interpolators in linear regression under a zero-mean Gaussian prior on the true parameter and Gaussian noise. The authors derive a closed-form expression for the interpolator that minimizes the expected excess risk, showing that it can strictly outperform the ordinary least squares (minimum-norm) solution. They further extend their theoretical analysis to the random features regression setting, providing valuable insight into benign overfitting in both classical and overparameterized regimes.

Strengths  
1. The topic—benign overfitting in linear models—is both timely and of high interest to the theoretical machine learning community.  
2. The exposition is clear, logically organized, and accessible, making the sequence of arguments easy to follow.  
3. The derivation of the closed-form optimal interpolator with respect to expected excess risk is elegant and adds a precise quantification of how one should reweight training responses.  
4. Extending the analysis to random features regression strengthens the paper’s relevance to modern overparameterized models and suggests broader applicability.

Weaknesses  
1. The current theory rests on relatively stringent Gaussian assumptions for both the noise terms ξ_i and the true parameter w*. It is unclear how critical these assumptions are to the main results.  
2. The discussion of prior work—particularly Bartlett et al. (2020) on the suboptimality of the minimum-norm interpolant—feels cursory and could benefit from greater nuance and context.  
3. There is only limited engagement with Amari et al. (2021), which also studies optimal trade-offs between bias and variance; the distinction between their bias-variance lens and the overall risk minimization pursued here should be made clearer.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Add a dedicated comparison to Amari et al. (2021). In particular, clarify how your objective of minimizing total excess risk differs from their approach to balancing bias and variance, and highlight any technical or setting differences.  
2. Enrich the related-work section around Bartlett et al. (2020). Explain specifically how your results extend beyond or sharpen the known cases where the minimum-norm interpolant is suboptimal.  
3. Discuss the possibility of relaxing the zero-mean Gaussian assumptions on w* and the noise. Would Proposition 1 continue to hold if w* had nonzero mean or if the noise were sub-Gaussian? Even a brief remark on the potential robustness (or needed modifications) would be valuable.  
4. In the experiments, please detail the structure of the population covariance Σ—e.g., whether it is isotropic or heavy-tailed—and include a plot or quantitative metric comparing the estimated covariance Σ_e to the true Σ. This will reassure readers that the approximation w_{O_e} is indeed close to the theoretical optimizer w_O.

Technical Clarifications Requested  
- Specify in more detail how w_{O_e} is constructed from Σ_e and how the estimation error in Σ_e affects the excess risk.  
- Provide a visualization (or table) showing ‖Σ_e − Σ‖ (in operator or Frobenius norm) as a function of sample size.  
- Comment on whether nonzero-mean priors introduce any bias terms in the closed-form solution, and if so, how they might be handled.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Overall, this manuscript makes a valuable theoretical contribution by identifying and characterizing an optimal interpolator that outperforms OLS in expectation, and by extending these insights to random features regression. The paper is well written and the results are of interest to researchers studying overparameterized models and benign overfitting. With the suggested clarifications and contextual expansions—especially around related works and empirical covariance estimation—I believe the paper will be significantly strengthened.  

Rating: 7: Good paper, accept.

reviews.summary: The paper under reviews studies the problem of optimal linear interpolation. More precisely the authors derive an explicit formula for the *best* linear interpolator that can be written as a linear function of the outputs and as a function of desired quantities. From this, they show how to recover theoretically this estimator from a optimization algorithm. Finally they expose cases where the designed estimator show some good generalization properties while the miminum norm interpolator does not.  The link with kernel methods is also made.
reviews.main_review:
**Overall comments and impression**

First, I would like to say that I am not an expert in statistical learning theory, so that I may have missed some insights in the results.

To begin, I would like to say that the paper is perfectly well written: the story is very clear and the mathematical writing is precise and fluid. This made the reading very pleasant and I would like to thank the authors for that.

The aim of the author is clear: provide an (under certain constraints) optimal linear predictor that may perform better than the minimum norm one naturally considered in the literature (naturally coming from GD or SGD on the empirical risk). The authors do so quite effortlessly ad give, to my opinion a nice result for those asking whether minimum norm solutions are always good estimators. **Yet**, I would have liked a lot more comments on the shape of this estimator: what intuition should the reader build when reading the paper ? A clear weakness is that the authors never really take the time of analysing why makes the estimator optimal (beyond technical considerations). In the very same manner, Proposition 2 gives that the optimal linear response is in fact the solution recovered by GD with a certain initialization: could the authors comment a bit on this ? 

The result on the divergence of the minimum norm linear interpolator (whereas the optimal one does not) is nice: however I would really need to understand further *why* and *when* we should expect this to happen. And in general cases *how close* should I expect the minimum norm interpolator to be close to the optimal one ? 


**Minor comments:**

- I guess that the part on the random matrix concepts arrive too early. As far as I understood, there is absolutely no need to put this part at the beginning, and this could be postpone in section 4.2 or even in the Appendix. 

- The part on random features model gives nothing new to the results: they are not commented (at all) and are absolutely expected once the linear model is covered. If these results are not further commented, then I recommend to put it in the Appendix as they do not give any additional insight on the paper. A nice introduction on why linear model are trendy is enough in my opinion (NTK, random features...)

- In Section 4.2, is this possible to derive a close form for the asymptotic risk of the optimal interpolator ? If not, why is this complicated ?

- Give more motivation and justification for the form of the *optimal linear interpolating estimator* you build. Why are you okay with the dependance on \Sigma, \phi, \delta. How could relax these ? I understand that these parameter will be estimated on the fly later: but once again, the comments on this are not deep precise enough to convince me. In particular there are only a few comments on the Graphiical Lasso approximation and why this is a good idea to approximate the population covariance.

- Plots commenting Proposition 2 would rely complement the result! If not, it seems not so important: a comparison with GD without preconditionning would be welcomed.


**Conclusion**

Even if I really enjoyed reading the paper, my opinion is that, under this form, the results lack from commentaries, intuition, insights and a bit of further developments.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

To begin, I would like to say that the paper is perfectly well written: the story is very clear and the mathematical writing is precise and fluid. This made the reading very pleasant and I would like to thank the authors for that.

The aim of the author is clear: provide an (under certain constraints) optimal linear predictor that may perform better than the minimum norm one naturally considered in the literature (naturally coming from GD or SGD on the empirical risk). The authors do so quite effortlessly ad give, to my opinion a nice result for those asking whether minimum norm solutions are always good estimators. **Yet**, I would have liked a lot more comments on the shape of this estimator: what intuition should the reader build when reading the paper ? A clear weakness is that the authors never really take the time of analysing why makes the estimator optimal (beyond technical considerations). In the very same manner, Proposition 2 gives that the optimal linear response is in fact the solution recovered by GD with a certain initialization: could the authors comment a bit on this ? 

The result on the divergence of the minimum norm linear interpolator (whereas the optimal one does not) is nice: however I would really need to understand further *why* and *when* we should expect this to happen. And in general cases *how close* should I expect the minimum norm interpolator to be close to the optimal one ? 


**Minor comments:**

- I guess that the part on the random matrix concepts arrive too early. As far as I understood, there is absolutely no need to put this part at the beginning, and this could be postpone in section 4.2 or even in the Appendix. 

- The part on random features model gives nothing new to the results: they are not commented (at all) and are absolutely expected once the linear model is covered. If these results are not further commented, then I recommend to put it in the Appendix as they do not give any additional insight on the paper. A nice introduction on why linear model are trendy is enough in my opinion (NTK, random features...)

- In Section 4.2, is this possible to derive a close form for the asymptotic risk of the optimal interpolator ? If not, why is this complicated ?

- Give more motivation and justification for the form of the *optimal linear interpolating estimator* you build. Why are you okay with the dependance on \Sigma, \phi, \delta. How could relax these ? I understand that these parameter will be estimated on the fly later: but once again, the comments on this are not deep precise enough to convince me. In particular there are only a few comments on the Graphiical Lasso approximation and why this is a good idea to approximate the population covariance.

- Plots commenting Proposition 2 would rely complement the result! If not, it seems not so important: a comparison with GD without preconditionning would be welcomed.


**Conclusion**

Even if I really enjoyed reading the paper, my opinion is that, under this form, the results lack from commentaries, intuition, insights and a bit of further developments.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This manuscript presents a novel optimal linear interpolator that provably outperforms the classical minimum-norm solution in certain high-dimensional regimes. The authors derive an explicit, closed-form expression for their estimator, situate it within the broader context of kernel methods and double-descent phenomena, and establish a connection to preconditioned gradient descent. The paper is very well written, with a clear narrative and precise mathematical statements. Overall, the technical contribution is interesting and relevant to current discussions about the implicit bias of overparameterized models.

Strengths  
1. The exposition is clear, fluid, and mathematically precise. Definitions, assumptions, and proofs are organized in a way that aids readability.  
2. The paper tells a coherent story, motivating the need for an alternative to the minimum-norm interpolator and guiding the reader through the main results.  
3. The explicit formula for the optimal linear interpolator is compelling and appears to be a genuine new result.  
4. The authors effectively connect their findings to kernel regression literature and to recent work on benign overfitting and random matrix theory.

Weaknesses  
1. While the technical derivations are solid, the manuscript offers little intuition for why the proposed estimator takes its particular form, and how its geometry depends on the covariance Σ, feature map Φ, or noise level δ.  
2. Proposition 2, which shows that gradient descent with a special preconditioning and initialization exactly recovers the optimal solution, is stated without deeper commentary on why this link holds or how sensitive it is to the initialization choice.  
3. The conditions under which the minimum-norm interpolator’s error diverges (and the underlying mechanisms) are not fully spelled out; nor is there discussion of how “close” the minimum-norm and optimal interpolators are when divergence does not occur.  
4. The random matrix theory background is introduced very early, interrupting the flow; similarly, the section on random features contains little explanation of its significance.  
5. The choice to approximate the population covariance Σ via the Graphical Lasso is not justified in depth—readers would benefit from a discussion of its appropriateness and limitations in this context.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Add intuitive commentary alongside the algebraic derivations of the optimal interpolator—e.g., describe in words how the estimator balances signal and noise and why this leads to lower risk.  
2. Expand the discussion of Proposition 2: explain why the particular preconditioning and initialization naturally induce the optimal solution, and perhaps characterize the landscape of the preconditioned objective. Including plots that compare gradient descent trajectories with and without this preconditioning would make the implicit-bias argument more concrete.  
3. Clarify the regimes (in terms of sample size, spectrum of Σ, noise δ, etc.) where the minimum-norm interpolator diverges, and quantify its performance gap relative to the optimal interpolator in more benign regimes.  
4. Move the random matrix preliminaries to a later section or to the appendix so that readers first see the main ideas; similarly, relocate the random features material to the appendix and enrich it with discussion of its implications.  
5. Discuss the feasibility of deriving a closed-form expression for the asymptotic risk of the optimal interpolator; if this proves intractable, provide a brief argument for why a closed‐form result may be hard to obtain.  
6. Provide more justification for modeling choices—especially the dependence on Σ, Φ, δ—and for employing the Graphical Lasso to estimate Σ, perhaps by citing prior work or including a short empirical study of its accuracy.

Decision  
Rating: 6 – Marginally above the acceptance threshold.  
This paper advances our understanding of overparameterized linear models by identifying and characterizing an optimal interpolator. Although certain intuitive and organizational aspects could be strengthened, the theoretical contribution and clarity of exposition merit acceptance after minor revisions.

More precisely the authors derive an explicit formula for the *best* linear interpolator that can be written as a linear function of the outputs and as a function of desired quantities. From this, they show how to recover theoretically this estimator from a optimization algorithm. Finally they expose cases where the designed estimator show some good generalization properties while the miminum norm interpolator does not.  The link with kernel methods is also made.

reviews.summary: This paper considers the problem of optimal interpolation in linear regression. It defines the class of response-linear achievable interpolators, and provides a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator in this class. The paper is about an important topic in machine learning and statistics. However, it is unclear to me whether the "achievable" interpolators are really achievable, and as a result if the investigation is meaningful.
reviews.main_review:
Major comments and questions:
1. The paper claims that the realization of true data-generating parameter and the realization of the noise in the training data are unknown. However, it assumes that the population covariance, the signal-to-noise ratio, and the covariance of the prior for the signal are known. The latter seems to be more unrealistic. For high dimensional data, the covariance matrix contains much more number of parameters than the regression coefficient so it is a much harder problem to estimate the covariance matrix. In a Bayesian setting, the primary goal is to find the distribution of the parameter. If the distribution of the parameter is assumed to be known, then there is no estimation problem.

2. For $\Phi$, do you mean it is the correlation matrix? Otherwise calling $r^2$ the signal is not appropriate. 
   
3. The population risk between lines 125 and 126 is not clear.It is written as the conditional expectation given $X, \xi, w^*$, but this does not make sense. A risk should not be random.

4. I think the $y$ in line 131 should be $y_i$, but what is the $y$ in line 132? The $y$ in line 117 should be $y_i$? How about the $y$ in lines 120 and 124? Each of these may be typos or poor notations, but when there are too many this type of problems and makes it hard to following the paper it becomes a major issue. 
   
5. Line 142, I believe you need $\gamma\ge1$.

6. Line 156 says "rank(X) = n with probability 1" while this becomes a condition in line 160.

7. Definition 4, do you mean there exists a known function? Otherwise, the function $f$ may depends further on other unknowns.

8. As in my first point, the $w_O$ is not realistic. The updating formula in (7) requires the inverse of $\Sigma$ which is almost impossible for high dimensional data. 

9. I don't think Hastie et al. (2019) considered the case of $\Phi=I_d$. It is probably a typo here.
   

Minor issues and typos:

Line 53, "are best" to "are the best".

In (5), use paratheses to make the definition clear.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Please see the main review.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper considers the problem of optimal interpolation in linear regression. It defines the class of response-linear achievable interpolators, and provides a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator in this class. The paper is about an important topic in machine learning and statistics. However, it is unclear to me whether the "achievable" interpolators are really achievable, and as a result if the investigation is meaningful.

Main Review:
Major comments and questions:
1. The paper claims that the realization of true data-generating parameter and the realization of the noise in the training data are unknown. However, it assumes that the population covariance, the signal-to-noise ratio, and the covariance of the prior for the signal are known. The latter seems to be more unrealistic. For high dimensional data, the covariance matrix contains much more number of parameters than the regression coefficient so it is a much harder problem to estimate the covariance matrix. In a Bayesian setting, the primary goal is to find the distribution of the parameter. If the distribution of the parameter is assumed to be known, then there is no estimation problem.

2. For $\Phi$, do you mean it is the correlation matrix? Otherwise calling $r^2$ the signal is not appropriate. 
   
3. The population risk between lines 125 and 126 is not clear.It is written as the conditional expectation given $X, \xi, w^*$, but this does not make sense. A risk should not be random.

4. I think the $y$ in line 131 should be $y_i$, but what is the $y$ in line 132? The $y$ in line 117 should be $y_i$? How about the $y$ in lines 120 and 124? Each of these may be typos or poor notations, but when there are too many this type of problems and makes it hard to following the paper it becomes a major issue. 
   
5. Line 142, I believe you need $\gamma\ge1$.

6. Line 156 says "rank(X) = n with probability 1" while this becomes a condition in line 160.

7. Definition 4, do you mean there exists a known function? Otherwise, the function $f$ may depends further on other unknowns.

8. As in my first point, the $w_O$ is not realistic. The updating formula in (7) requires the inverse of $\Sigma$ which is almost impossible for high dimensional data. 

9. I don't think Hastie et al. (2019) considered the case of $\Phi=I_d$. It is probably a typo here.
   

Minor issues and typos:

Line 53, "are best" to "are the best".

In (5), use paratheses to make the definition clear.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Please see the main review.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper studies optimal interpolation strategies in linear regression by introducing the class of response-linear achievable interpolators and deriving a closed-form optimal solution within this class. The topic—understanding interpolation in overparameterized models—is timely and relevant, and the paper’s formal definition of response-linear interpolators alongside an explicit formula for the best interpolator are valuable contributions. However, I find several critical issues—particularly concerning unrealistic assumptions, clarity of presentation, and technical consistency—that must be addressed before this work can be considered for publication.

Strengths  
1. Addresses a fundamental problem in modern statistics and machine learning: the behavior of interpolating solutions in linear regression.  
2. Clearly defines the novel concept of response-linear achievable interpolators.  
3. Provides an explicit closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator in this class, which may serve as a useful theoretical benchmark.

Weaknesses and Major Concerns  
1. Unrealistic Knowledge Assumptions  
   – The analysis assumes that the population covariance matrix Σ, the signal-to‐noise ratio δ, and the prior covariance are known exactly. In high-dimensional settings, estimating these quantities reliably is often prohibitive, since Σ has O(d²) parameters versus only d parameters in the regression vector. Without a discussion of how to obtain or approximate these inputs, the practical relevance is limited.  
2. Ambiguity in Population Risk Definition  
   – The population risk is presented as E[· | X, ξ, w*], making it a random quantity conditional on the data. This contradicts standard use (which defines population risk as an unconditional expectation). A clear, nonrandom formulation is needed.  
3. Notation Inconsistencies  
   – There are multiple instances where y and y_i are used interchangeably (e.g., lines 131–132), leading to confusion. Consistent notation throughout is essential for readability.  
4. Missing Condition on γ  
   – In the asymptotic results (e.g., line 142), the ratio γ = d/n should be required to satisfy γ ≥ 1 for interpolation to occur. This condition is never explicitly stated, despite being critical to the theorems.  
5. Contradictory Rank Assumptions  
   – The text alternately claims rank(X)=n as a fact and later treats it as an additional assumption (lines 156 vs. 160). This contradiction needs resolution: either the rank condition follows from earlier assumptions or it must be clearly stated as a separate requirement.  
6. Definition 4’s Scope  
   – Definition 4 introduces a function f that maps inputs (X, y, etc.) to interpolators but does not specify that f must be known or computable in practice. Clarify that f is a pre‐specified (and implementable) function rather than an existential object.  
7. Inversion of Σ in the Optimal Solution  
   – The proposed optimal interpolator w_O (equation (7)) depends on Σ⁻¹. In high dimensions, directly inverting Σ is computationally infeasible without additional structure. The authors should discuss computational feasibility or propose practical approximations.  
8. Incorrect Attribution to Hastie et al. (2019)  
   – The paper claims that Φ=I_d in Hastie et al. (2019), which is inaccurate. The reference should be checked and corrected.

Suggestions for Improvement  
– Provide justification or alternative approaches for obtaining Σ, δ, and the prior covariance in high-dimensional regimes (e.g., shrinkage estimators, low‐rank structure).  
– Rewrite the population risk definition as an unconditional expectation over new test points, removing any conditional randomness.  
– Standardize notation for responses (always use y_i when summing or indexing) and carefully proofread all equations.  
– Explicitly state the requirement γ ≥ 1 wherever interpolation assumptions are made.  
– Clarify the role of rank(X)=n: either derive it from existing assumptions or label it clearly as an additional hypothesis.  
– Amend Definition 4 to specify that f is a known algorithmic mapping.  
– Discuss the computational burden of Σ inversion and, if possible, propose efficient approximations (e.g., using conjugate gradient methods or low‐rank approximations).  
– Verify and correct the citation regarding Φ in Hastie et al. (2019).  
– Fix minor typos (e.g., change “are best” to “are the best” and ensure parentheses in equation (5) are clear).

Recommendation  
Rating: 4: Ok but not good enough – rejection  
While the paper tackles an important question and offers a neat closed‐form result, the combination of unrealistic assumptions, notational issues, and unaddressed computational concerns prevents me from recommending acceptance in its current form. I encourage the authors to address the points above for a significantly improved revision.

title: Differentiable rendering with perturbed optimizers

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes a new differentiable renderer by introducing perturbed optimizers. The paper re-writes the non-differentiable rasterization and aggregation steps to heaven side functions and relaxes them to be differentiable by adding noise. 
The author shows that previous work, especially softras, can be treated as a special case of the perturbed renderer with specific prior selections. The authors also demonstrated in several experiments they achieve better or comparable results than the state of the art.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths

1) Differentiable reformulation
This paper treats rasterization as a linear programing problem.  It extends the [4] to the rendering pipeline and relaxes the non-differentiable rendering to be differentiable by introducing perturbed optimizers. It provides a new differentiable renderer from a new perspective.

2) Better performance
The authors show AS and VR are very useful in optimization, for both perturbed renderer and softras. In the 6D pose comparison, the results are better than softras.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
There are several weak points of this paper.

1) The paper is not self-contained.  I have a hard time understanding each equation. While the authors largely rely on [4], it makes readers hard to follow without knowing [4]. Moreover, the notations in this paper are very vague.   E.g. line 66 it should be j \in [1,.., m]?  line 76 z should be depth order?  If it is depth, how do you compute <z, y>?  line 88, what's v?  The paper writing definitely needs to be improved.


2) Relation of softras and noise types.  The authors show that softras can be treated as the special case of the perturbed renderer. Softras has some hyperparameters which are hard to explain but in the new renderer, all the parameters have clear noise intensity meaning.  While this is very good, but the paper lacks further analysis of which noise should we use. E.g. in table 1, it seems Gaussian noise is the best. But could it be generalized to other cases? 


3) Comparison.  In line 214 the authors said, "Unless stated, the results for the other renderers are extracted from their original publications, as we were not able to get access to their implementations."  I am a little confused about this.  Clearly, N3mr, softras, dibr released their renderers. Thus, why not simply replace the renderer from perturbed renderer to new renderers and compare the score?  Directly copying scores from others' papers may have the wrong comparison. While all the methods use shapenet, the rendered images could vary from lighting to camera views. I would highly recommend the authors redo the comparison.


4)  The authors assume each pixel will be influenced by all the triangles. For each pixel, it renders the influences of all the triangles and combines them together(Eq 3).  I wonder what's the rendering speed of the perturbed renderer? Theoretically, such design plus the MC sampling will make the renderer very time-consuming.  The authors should show the rendering speed comparison.


5) Following the last concern.  Generally speaking, rasterization accelerates the rendering speed by assuming each pixel is influenced by the frontest triangle.  On the other side, MC is always used in ray tracing to compute multiple bounces. The authors introduce MC in rasterization, which seems to adopt the weak points of two rendering methods.   I wonder did the authors consider extending it to RT?

Post rebuttal:

The authors addressed my concerns and I will raise the score to be marginally above the threshold and be happy to see this paper get accepted.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Moreover, the notations in this paper are very vague.   E.g. line 66 it should be j \in [1,.., m]?  line 76 z should be depth order?  If it is depth, how do you compute <z, y>?  line 88, what's v?  The paper writing definitely needs to be improved.


2) Relation of softras and noise types.  The authors show that softras can be treated as the special case of the perturbed renderer. Softras has some hyperparameters which are hard to explain but in the new renderer, all the parameters have clear noise intensity meaning.  While this is very good, but the paper lacks further analysis of which noise should we use. E.g. in table 1, it seems Gaussian noise is the best. But could it be generalized to other cases? 


3) Comparison.  In line 214 the authors said, "Unless stated, the results for the other renderers are extracted from their original publications, as we were not able to get access to their implementations."  I am a little confused about this.  Clearly, N3mr, softras, dibr released their renderers. Thus, why not simply replace the renderer from perturbed renderer to new renderers and compare the score?  Directly copying scores from others' papers may have the wrong comparison. While all the methods use shapenet, the rendered images could vary from lighting to camera views. I would highly recommend the authors redo the comparison.


4)  The authors assume each pixel will be influenced by all the triangles. For each pixel, it renders the influences of all the triangles and combines them together(Eq 3).  I wonder what's the rendering speed of the perturbed renderer? Theoretically, such design plus the MC sampling will make the renderer very time-consuming.  The authors should show the rendering speed comparison.


5) Following the last concern.  Generally speaking, rasterization accelerates the rendering speed by assuming each pixel is influenced by the frontest triangle.  On the other side, MC is always used in ray tracing to compute multiple bounces. The authors introduce MC in rasterization, which seems to adopt the weak points of two rendering methods.   I wonder did the authors consider extending it to RT?

Post rebuttal:

The authors addressed my concerns and I will raise the score to be marginally above the threshold and be happy to see this paper get accepted.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a novel approach to differentiable rendering by framing rasterization and Z‐buffer aggregation as linear programs relaxed via perturbed optimizers. Building on recent work in randomized smoothing, the authors introduce adaptive smoothing (AS) and a variance reduction (VR) scheme, and demonstrate improved convergence and pose‐estimation accuracy over SoftRas on a 6D object‐pose benchmark.

Strengths  
1. Novel formulation: The use of perturbed optimizers to relax discrete rasterization and aggregation steps is original and well motivated, effectively unifying previous soft‐rasterization methods as special cases under different noise priors.  
2. New optimization techniques: The adaptive smoothing and variance reduction strategies are clearly useful, yielding tangible performance gains in terms of both convergence speed and final accuracy.  
3. Empirical improvements: On the 6D pose‐estimation task, the proposed method consistently outperforms SoftRas, suggesting that the differentiable LP formulation brings real benefits in practice.

Weaknesses  
1. Clarity and self‐containment: Key notation is left undefined (for example, the index j in j∈[1..m], the depth vector z and its inner product ⟨z,y⟩, and variable v). This makes the presentation difficult to follow without frequent back‐and‐forth.  
2. Limited noise analysis: The paper reports that Gaussian noise gave the best results, but does not systematically compare other priors (e.g., Cauchy, Gumbel) or offer guidelines for selecting noise distributions in new applications.  
3. Questionable baseline comparisons: All numbers for SoftRas and related methods appear to be taken from their original publications, which may use different lighting, camera, or shading configurations. Without reimplementing these baselines under a common codebase, it is hard to gauge the true performance gap.  
4. Missing runtime evaluation: The proposed Monte Carlo sampling over all triangles per pixel could be a serious bottleneck, yet there is no measurement of forward/backward pass time or memory usage.  
5. Scope limited to rasterization: The discussion does not address whether and how this perturbed‐optimizer framework could extend to ray‐traced rendering with multiple bounces, a key challenge for physically based rendering.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Clarify exposition: Provide a fully self‐contained mathematical description by defining every symbol (j, z, v, inner products, etc.) the first time it appears. A notation table might help.  
2. Noise priors study: Include a systematic comparison of Gaussian, Cauchy, Gumbel, and perhaps uniform noise, along with a discussion of when each prior is preferable.  
3. Unified baselines: Re‐run SoftRas, DIB‐R, NMR, and any other relevant methods in the same codebase and under identical rendering settings to ensure fair, apples‐to‐apples comparisons.  
4. Runtime and memory benchmarks: Report per‐pixel or per‐image timings for forward and backward passes, as well as peak memory consumption, to quantify the computational overhead of MC sampling.  
5. Broader scope discussion: At least discuss the prospects of extending perturbed smoothing to ray tracing with multiple light bounces, or outline potential obstacles and future work in that direction.

Recommendation  
Rating: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)  
This paper makes a significant conceptual contribution by introducing perturbed optimizers for differentiable rendering and demonstrates promising empirical results. However, major revisions are needed in terms of clarity, comparative analysis, and computational evaluation before the approach can be fully validated and widely adopted. I lean toward acceptance, provided the authors address the above concerns in a revised submission.

Summary:
This paper proposes a new differentiable renderer by introducing perturbed optimizers. The paper re-writes the non-differentiable rasterization and aggregation steps to heaven side functions and relaxes them to be differentiable by adding noise. 
The author shows that previous work, especially softras, can be treated as a special case of the perturbed renderer with specific prior selections. The authors also demonstrated in several experiments they achieve better or comparable results than the state of the art.

Main Review:
Strengths

1) Differentiable reformulation
This paper treats rasterization as a linear programing problem.  It extends the [4] to the rendering pipeline and relaxes the non-differentiable rendering to be differentiable by introducing perturbed optimizers. It provides a new differentiable renderer from a new perspective.

2) Better performance
The authors show AS and VR are very useful in optimization, for both perturbed renderer and softras. In the 6D pose comparison, the results are better than softras.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
There are several weak points of this paper.

1) The paper is not self-contained.  I have a hard time understanding each equation. While the authors largely rely on [4], it makes readers hard to follow without knowing [4]. extracted_ideas.summary: The reviewer appreciates the novel formulation of differentiable rendering via perturbed optimizers and the empirical performance gains (AS and VR) over SoftRas, but finds the paper hard to follow due to vague notation, incomplete analysis of noise choices, potentially flawed baseline comparisons, and missing runtime evaluations.
strengths: Novel differentiable reformulation: rasterization and aggregation as LPs relaxed via perturbed optimizers, extending prior work [4] to rendering.
Empirical performance gains: adaptive smoothing (AS) and variance reduction (VR) improve optimization, outperforming SoftRas on 6D pose estimation.
weaknesses: Paper is not self-contained; many equations and notations (e.g. j∈[1..m], depth ordering z, inner product <z,y>, variable v) are undefined or unclear.
Lacks analysis of different noise priors; while Gaussian appears best in reported table, no guidance on generalization to other distributions.
Baseline comparisons use numbers lifted from original publications under potentially different rendering setups (lighting, camera) rather than re-implementing all methods under a common codebase.
No evaluation of rendering speed or computational cost; per-pixel MC sampling over all triangles may be very slow.
Design borrows weak points of rasterization (only front triangle) and Monte Carlo (high cost); no discussion of extension to ray tracing for multiple bounces.
suggestions: Rewrite and clarify exposition: define all symbols and equations for a self-contained presentation.
Provide a systematic comparison of noise priors (Gaussian, Cauchy, Gumbel, etc.) and discuss when each is most appropriate.
Re-run all baseline methods (SoftRas, DIB-R, NMR) in the same codebase and under identical rendering settings for fair comparisons.
Include quantitative runtime benchmarks (forward/backward pass times, memory usage) to assess computational overhead.
Discuss or explore extending the perturbed optimizer framework to ray-traced rendering for multi-bounce illumination.

SoftRas and other prior methods emerge as special cases under specific noise priors in the perturbed renderer framework.
Adaptive smoothing (AS) and variance reduction (VR) are key techniques that yield improved convergence and accuracy.
Presentation suffers from undefined notation and lack of self-containment, hindering accessibility.
Baseline evaluation may be unreliable due to inconsistent setups and use of published numbers.
Computational performance (speed and memory) is not addressed despite use of MC sampling over all triangles.

Line 76: variable z referred to as depth ordering; inner product ⟨z,y⟩ is not explained or computed.
Line 88: symbol v is used without definition.
Monte Carlo sampling requires evaluating all triangles per pixel, raising concerns about rendering throughput.
Extracted baseline results may differ due to variations in lighting, shading, and camera parameters across publications.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a new differentiable rendering method, differentiable perturbed renderers,  wherein the gradients of the rasterization step are approximated using perturbed optimizers. They implement this change on top of the SoftRas method and demonstrate that it leads to better pose estimation, and on par performance with state of the art methods in unsupervised single image 3d object reconstruction.
reviews.main_review:
This is a strong paper. It is written clearly, and the method is explained properly. Perturbed optimizers seem to be a new way for tackling optimization over discrete operations in deep learning, and their application to rasterization is well motivated and appropriate. The theory required for applying them to this new setting seems right, and is well explained. The results are also adequate for the claims made. It would have been nice to see higher performance on the reconstruction task, though diff rendering is a relatively new and so highlighting new avenues to explore , such as your proposed perturbed optimizers,  is important, and so I do not believe it is necessary.

 I would say one nice set of experiments to see would be optimization over the same set of parameters as in the DIB-R  paper Figure 2. ( camera position, texture , vertex positions, etc. ), with qualitative and quantitative comparisons to DIB-R and SoftRas. Having this would go a long was to increasing my score, and I believe would sell you paper quite a bit better. The pose estimation seems quite a bit easier than optimizing for texture and vertex position for example, and it would be nice to see how you method fairs, even if the performance is only on par or worse.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
This paper proposes a new differentiable rendering method, differentiable perturbed renderers,  wherein the gradients of the rasterization step are approximated using perturbed optimizers. They implement this change on top of the SoftRas method and demonstrate that it leads to better pose estimation, and on par performance with state of the art methods in unsupervised single image 3d object reconstruction.

Main Review:
This is a strong paper. It is written clearly, and the method is explained properly. Perturbed optimizers seem to be a new way for tackling optimization over discrete operations in deep learning, and their application to rasterization is well motivated and appropriate. The theory required for applying them to this new setting seems right, and is well explained. The results are also adequate for the claims made. It would have been nice to see higher performance on the reconstruction task, though diff rendering is a relatively new and so highlighting new avenues to explore , such as your proposed perturbed optimizers,  is important, and so I do not believe it is necessary.

 I would say one nice set of experiments to see would be optimization over the same set of parameters as in the DIB-R  paper Figure 2. ( camera position, texture , vertex positions, etc. ), with qualitative and quantitative comparisons to DIB-R and SoftRas. Having this would go a long was to increasing my score, and I believe would sell you paper quite a bit better. The pose estimation seems quite a bit easier than optimizing for texture and vertex position for example, and it would be nice to see how you method fairs, even if the performance is only on par or worse.

Yes.

7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a novel approach for differentiable rendering by integrating perturbed optimizers into a rasterization pipeline (built on top of SoftRas). By injecting random perturbations to approximate the gradient of discrete rendering operations, the authors derive a theoretically grounded method that yields significant improvements in 6D pose estimation. When applied to unsupervised single‐image 3D mesh reconstruction, the proposed technique achieves results on par with state‐of‐the‐art methods such as SoftRas and DIB‐R.

Strengths  
- The paper is very clear and well written, with a coherent motivation for why perturbed optimizers can address the non-differentiability of rasterization.  
- The theoretical framework is solid: the authors adapt existing perturbation-based gradient estimators to the rendering context in a principled way.  
- Empirical results convincingly show that the perturbed optimizer improves 6D object pose estimation over prior differentiable renderers.  
- On the challenging task of unsupervised single-image 3D reconstruction, the method matches or slightly exceeds current top performers.  
- The technical implementation details—how perturbations are integrated into SoftRas and how gradients are approximated via randomized sampling—are clearly explained.

Weaknesses  
- While the unsupervised reconstruction results are competitive, absolute performance still lags behind fully supervised or other recent hybrid methods, suggesting headroom for improvement.  
- The evaluation is heavily focused on pose estimation, which is relatively easier compared to other rendering-based optimization tasks.  
- There is no analysis of optimizing other critical factors such as camera extrinsics (beyond pose), texture parameters, or mesh vertex positions—areas where differentiable renderers like DIB-R claim strong performance gains.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. To strengthen the empirical validation, I recommend adding experiments that jointly optimize camera position, texture maps, and vertex adjustments, mirroring the evaluation protocol used in the DIB-R paper.  
2. Provide both qualitative visualizations and quantitative metrics comparing your method, DIB-R, and SoftRas on these expanded tasks. This will clarify where perturbed optimizers offer unique advantages or limitations.  
3. Explore a more challenging set of optimization problems—beyond 6D pose—to demonstrate the broader applicability of the perturbed optimization framework in differentiable rendering.

Decision: 7: Good paper, accept  
This work makes a meaningful theoretical and practical contribution by bringing perturbed optimizers into the differentiable rendering domain. Its clear presentation, sound theory, and strong pose estimation results justify acceptance. With the suggested additional experiments, the paper’s impact and completeness would be further enhanced.

reviews.summary:
This paper presents a method for converting a standard non-differentiable rasterization-based renderer into a differentiable counterpart using recent advances in the perturbed optimizer. The main contribution lies in the generic nature provided by the theoretical formulation, where some specifically chosen priors in the proposed framework can retrieve recent state-of-the-art differentiable renderers, e.g. SoftRas. 
As the proposed approach mainly relies on a Monte-Carlo estimator for generating the smoothing parameters. Variance-reduction mechanism and sensitivity analysis are also incorporated into the proposed framework to ensure the robustness of the approximate renderer. Experiments on pose estimation and single-view reconstruction are conducted for comparing the performance of the proposed method and previous state-of-the-art approaches.
reviews.main_review:
Pros:

- By introducing the perturbed optimizer to analyzing the non-differentiability of standard rasterization-based rendering pipeline provides new insight into constructing a generic differentiable renderer. The perturbed differentiable rendering can also be implemented on top of existing renderers without major modifications, which makes this approach versatile and a potential performance booster for existing DR techniques.

Cons:

- In terms of technical novelty, it seems to be a direct application of perturbed optimizer to differentiable rendering. Though it requires some efforts (such as the variance reduction and sensitivity analysis) to adapt the existing theory to this specific application, it is not very challenging as there are existing protocols to follow.

- Evaluation-wise, in the task of single-view reconstruction, though the proposed method can achieve comparable performance compared to the state-of-the-art approaches, it still fails to outperform DIB-R in mean accuracy and also outperformed by SoftRas in many categories, such as the Dresser, Car, etc. The 3D pose estimation is only evaluated on a simple cube which cannot provide a very comprehensive measurement of the proposed approach.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

The main contribution lies in the generic nature provided by the theoretical formulation, where some specifically chosen priors in the proposed framework can retrieve recent state-of-the-art differentiable renderers, e.g. SoftRas. 
As the proposed approach mainly relies on a Monte-Carlo estimator for generating the smoothing parameters. Variance-reduction mechanism and sensitivity analysis are also incorporated into the proposed framework to ensure the robustness of the approximate renderer. Experiments on pose estimation and single-view reconstruction are conducted for comparing the performance of the proposed method and previous state-of-the-art approaches.

Main Review:
Pros:

- By introducing the perturbed optimizer to analyzing the non-differentiability of standard rasterization-based rendering pipeline provides new insight into constructing a generic differentiable renderer. The perturbed differentiable rendering can also be implemented on top of existing renderers without major modifications, which makes this approach versatile and a potential performance booster for existing DR techniques.

Cons:

Though it requires some efforts (such as the variance reduction and sensitivity analysis) to adapt the existing theory to this specific application, it is not very challenging as there are existing protocols to follow.

- Evaluation-wise, in the task of single-view reconstruction, though the proposed method can achieve comparable performance compared to the state-of-the-art approaches, it still fails to outperform DIB-R in mean accuracy and also outperformed by SoftRas in many categories, such as the Dresser, Car, etc. The 3D pose estimation is only evaluated on a simple cube which cannot provide a very comprehensive measurement of the proposed approach.

reviews.text:
Review for “A Generic Differentiable Renderer via Perturbed Optimizers”

Summary  
This paper proposes a unified, differentiable rendering framework built on the concept of perturbed optimizers. By smoothing inherently non-differentiable rasterization and aggregation operations through Monte Carlo sampling, and further stabilizing gradients with control-variate–based variance reduction and sensitivity analysis of noise levels, the authors derive unbiased gradient estimators. The framework can recover existing differentiable renderers (for example, SoftRas under Gumbel priors) by choosing appropriate smoothing distributions, and it can be layered on top of current renderers with minimal implementation overhead. Empirical evaluation includes single-view 3D mesh reconstruction on several ShapeNet categories and 6D pose estimation on a simple cube.

Strengths  
1. The use of perturbed optimizers to handle rasterization’s non-differentiability offers a clear, theoretically grounded perspective that unifies prior smoothing approaches.  
2. Specializing the generic framework to particular priors (e.g., Gumbel or logistic noise) elegantly recovers state-of-the-art renderers such as SoftRas, highlighting its generality.  
3. The proposed implementation strategy—treating the framework as a thin abstraction layer over existing renderers—makes adoption straightforward, increasing its practical appeal.  
4. Incorporating control variates for variance reduction, along with sensitivity analysis to adaptively tune noise levels, demonstrates attention to the often-overlooked stability issues of Monte Carlo gradient estimators.

Weaknesses  
1. The technical novelty is relatively modest: the paper largely adapts established perturbed-optimizer techniques to the rendering domain, and the variance-reduction/sensitivity-analysis components follow existing recipes.  
2. In the reconstruction experiments, the method does not outperform DIB-R in mean accuracy and falls short of SoftRas on several object categories, limiting the empirical impact.  
3. The 6D pose estimation results are demonstrated only on a simple cube, providing only weak evidence of the framework’s applicability to more complex, real-world objects.  

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Broaden the 6D pose estimation benchmark to include a variety of object shapes and textures—this would better showcase the generality and robustness of the proposed smoothing approach.  
2. Investigate enhancements (e.g., task-specific priors or hybrid loss functions) that could push single-view reconstruction performance beyond DIB-R and SoftRas across all categories.  
3. Expand the evaluation suite with additional rendering tasks or datasets (e.g., multi-view stereo, more challenging real-image benchmarks) to more thoroughly demonstrate practical benefits.

Detailed Comments  
• The Monte Carlo–based approximation of smoothed argmax operators is well motivated, but readers would benefit from a brief complexity analysis comparing sampling requirements to existing continuous approximations (e.g., SoftRas’s softmax-based method).  
• The control-variate estimator is clearly described; however, a sensitivity study on the number of samples versus variance reduction gain would strengthen the empirical case.  
• In Section 4, the link between particular choices of priors and existing differentiable renderers is nicely illustrated. It might help to include a side-by-side table summarizing how Gumbel, Gaussian, or logistic smoothing each map to known methods.  
• The cube-based pose estimation experiment is a good sanity check, but more varied geometry (e.g., objects with concavities or non-convex silhouettes) would provide stronger evidence that the GPU-friendly implementation scales to practical scenarios.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Overall, this work delivers a coherent and extensible theoretical framework for differentiable rendering via perturbed optimizers, together with a flexible implementation strategy. While the technical contributions build on established smoothing and variance-reduction ideas, the unification of these components and the ability to recover prior methods under a single formalism represent a meaningful, if incremental, advance. The empirical results are promising but would be strengthened by broader benchmarks.  

Given its solid theoretical foundation, practical implementation benefits, and the potential for impact once extended, I recommend a weak accept (rating 6), marginally above the acceptance threshold.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a differentiable rendering method that builds connection between the 3D model and 2D perspective appearance. Different from previous methods, the differentiable rendering is formulated into an optimization problem. Two computer vision tasks, including pose estimation and 3D shape from color image, are evaluated with the proposed renderer and compared to SoftRas [22].
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- The performance for pose estimation outperforms existing methods.
- The formulation seems novel.

Weakness:
- Could the proposed formulation be applied to other 3D shape representations, e.g. point cloud, implicit? If so, an experiment should be added. If not, then better to mention this is a mesh-specific technic.

- The algorithm seems to be computational costy, e.g. 1 min per image. Would this heavily affect the training efficiency? While comparing to other methods, e.g. SoftRas, the run-time efficiency and hyper-parameters, e.g. number of iteration, training epoch, should be mentioned. Otherwise the comparison may not be on a fair basis.

- Keeping the boundary differentiable is one of the main issues in existing differentiable rendering technics, which is also argued mainly in this paper. However it is not clear how this is improved by the proposed method. From Fig 2 and 4, it seems the boundary area has obvious artifacts. Also some experiments should be performed to highlight the improvements over occlusion boundary.

- The performance on 3D shape prediction does not outperform existing approaches. 

Overall, despite a novel formulation, the improvements over existing approaches are not clear. As shown in the experiment, the propose method does not necessarily produce more accurate rendering or support better for vision tasks. Considering that the proposed tech is also quite slow, the overall advantage over existing approaches is not very clear.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a novel optimization-based formulation of differentiable rendering and demonstrates its effectiveness for 6-DoF pose estimation, achieving results that surpass several existing methods. While the approach is creative and yields strong pose estimation performance, there are significant concerns regarding its scope, efficiency, and overall improvement over the state of the art in 3D reconstruction.

Strengths  
1. The optimization-based differentiable rendering framework is an original contribution that departs from fully analytic or rasterization-based differentiable renderers.  
2. On the task of object pose estimation, the proposed method consistently outperforms baseline techniques, indicating that the formulation can indeed yield very accurate pose predictions.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Representation Scope: The current method is demonstrated only on mesh-based models. It remains unclear whether the approach can be generalized to other popular 3D representations such as point clouds or implicit surface functions.  
2. High Computational Cost: Rendering and gradient computation take on the order of one minute per image, which is prohibitively expensive for large-scale training or real-time inference.  
3. Missing Runtime and Hyper-parameter Details: Comparisons to competing methods such as SoftRas lack crucial information about run times, number of optimization iterations, and training epochs, making it difficult to evaluate fairness and reproducibility.  
4. Unclear Occlusion and Boundary Handling Gains: Although the paper claims improved handling of occlusions and differentiating along object boundaries, the provided figures (e.g., Figures 2 and 4) still exhibit visible artifacts. Quantitative evidence for boundary-aware improvements is not presented.  
5. 3D Shape Prediction Lagging: In the task of reconstructing 3D shape from color images, the proposed approach does not outperform existing state-of-the-art methods, calling into question its broader applicability beyond pose estimation.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Representation Generalization: Either extend the experiments to include other 3D formats—such as point clouds or implicit surfaces—or clarify in the manuscript that the method is specifically tailored to meshes.  
2. Detailed Efficiency Reporting: Provide precise runtime measurements (rendering time per image, total training/inference time) and full hyper-parameter settings (iteration counts, learning rates, epoch numbers) for both the proposed method and baseline approaches to support fair comparison.  
3. Boundary/Occlusion Experiments: Introduce targeted quantitative evaluations—such as boundary sharpness metrics or occlusion error rates—to substantiate claims of improved handling at object edges and occluded regions.  
4. Computational Cost Mitigation: Explore strategies to accelerate rendering and gradient computation, for example via multi-resolution representations, caching intermediate results, or leveraging GPU acceleration, to make the approach more practical for both training and deployment.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
While the paper introduces an interesting optimization-based differentiable renderer with demonstrated gains in pose estimation, its restricted applicability to meshes, high computational demands, lack of critical experimental details, and underwhelming performance on 3D reconstruction tasks limit its impact. I therefore recommend rejection (Rating: 4 – Ok but not good enough) in its current form.

title: NAS-Bench-x11 and the Power of Learning Curves

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes another benchmark dataset for NAS, NAS-Bench-x11. Compared to the original benchmarks NAS-Bench-101, NAS-Bench-301 and NAS-Bench-NLP, NAS-Bench-x11 contains the full training
information for each candidate architecture especially the learning curves so that it allows multi-fidelity NAS algorithms

NAS-Bench-x11 is a surrogate benchmark, which trains a surrogate model to predict the learning curve of the vast amount of candidate architectures. Prediction of the learning curve can be accomplished by singular value decomposition and noise modeling.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:

The paper is well motivated and clearly written. The paper is clear about limitations too.

I think this paper will be valuable to the community.  The created NAS-bench-x11 contains the full training information of different architectures, which may make it more convenient to analyse NAS algorithms and promote further NAS algorithm design. The idea of predicting the learning curve is very interesting to me. 

The authors conducted extensive experiments to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.  

Weakness:

This paper is a completion of the previously created benchmarks. Providing learning curves is also considered before in NAS-Bench-201, which weakens the novelty of this paper. So I recommend a weak acceptence.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces NAS-Bench-x11, a surrogate benchmark that provides full training information—including complete learning curves—for a large set of neural architectures. The core technical contribution is a learning-curve prediction model built on singular value decomposition (SVD) and noise modeling, which allows the authors to approximate the full training trajectories of hundreds of thousands of architectures at minimal cost. The resulting benchmark is designed to support multi-fidelity NAS methods and offers a practical resource for rapid prototyping and analysis. Extensive experiments demonstrate that NAS-Bench-x11 reliably replicates true learning curves and enables efficient search strategies.

Strengths  
1. Clear motivation and exposition: The manuscript is well structured, with a lucid description of the need for full learning-curve information in NAS and how the proposed surrogate addresses this gap.  
2. Valuable community resource: By providing complete training histories, NAS-Bench-x11 simplifies benchmarking of multi-fidelity and early-stopping NAS algorithms, potentially catalyzing new research directions.  
3. Thoughtful limitations: The authors explicitly discuss the surrogate’s reliance on SVD and noise assumptions, acknowledging the boundaries of their approach.  
4. Innovative modeling: Applying singular value decomposition to compress learning-curve data and adding realistic noise yields a lightweight yet accurate prediction model.  
5. Thorough empirical validation: The paper includes extensive experiments across multiple search spaces and settings, confirming that the surrogate curves closely match ground-truth performance and enable competitive NAS outcomes.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited novelty relative to prior work: Benchmarks such as NAS-Bench-201 have already released full learning-curve information, which partially diminishes the paper’s originality.  
2. Extension rather than reinvention: Rather than introducing a fundamentally new concept, this work mainly completes and expands existing benchmark suites by synthesizing missing curves via prediction.  

Recommendation  
While the technical approach and empirical rigor are solid, the contribution is largely incremental given that earlier benchmarks already provide learning-curve data. Nevertheless, the surrogate modeling technique and the scope of architectures covered make NAS-Bench-x11 a useful addition to the NAS community. Accordingly, I recommend weak acceptance (Rating: 6), as the paper offers practical value but falls short of a major conceptual breakthrough.

Minor Comments and Suggestions  
- Please clarify in the introduction how the new noise modeling compares quantitatively to the variability found in actual training runs.  
- In Section 4, consider adding a brief comparison of the SVD-based surrogate against simpler interpolation baselines to highlight the benefits of your approach.  
- It may be helpful to include guidelines on leveraging NAS-Bench-x11 for common multi-fidelity strategies (e.g., Successive Halving) in the appendix.
