reviews.summary: Existing NAS benchmarks often only include the architecture performance at the final or some selected checkpoint epochs. However, per-epoch information is essential for developing and evaluating multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. This work proposed to fit a surrogate model to predict the learning curve given an architecture encoding. Utilizing the proposed predictor, the author extends three existing benchmarks NASBench-101, NASBench-301, and NASBench-NLP to include per-epoch accuracies. Further, to demonstrate the necessity of developing multi-fidelity algorithms for NAS, the author also describes a procedure to extend the single-fidelity algorithms to multi-fidelity ones by using learning curve extrapolation to filter out candidate proposals with partial training. Despite possible room for improvements on the performance of the learning curve predictor used, the proposed method would enable researchers to develop and rapidly test multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. I vote for acceptance.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
1. The contribution of this paper is relevant to the NAS community and would accelerate research on multi-fidelity NAS algorithms.
2. The performance of the proposed learning curve predictor model is overall satisfactory.
3. The author adopted the proposed method to extend three existing benchmarks to include per-epoch information.

Weakness:
1. The author reported ranking correlation and KL divergence between the predicted learning curve and ground truth. The numbers seem reasonable, but the errors are still non-negligible. It is unclear how misleading these errors would be if someone tries to develop multi-fidelity algorithms by querying the proposed extended benchmark. How would the existing multi-fidelity NAS algorithms perform under the proposed benchmark, over ground-truth data? This might be doable for NASBench201 or NASBench111 (the version created without using information about the given 4 checkpoint epochs), as they provide ground-truth accuracies on at least some checkpoints?

Comments and Questions:
1. For NASBench-NLP, using the accuracy from the first few epochs as features significantly improves the learning curve predictor's performance. Can we also expect visible improvements on other spaces, if the info of the first few epochs is also provided?

2. You mentioned in para 355 that future surrogate works should save full learning curve info to avoid the need for any extra extensions. But surrogate benchmarks like NASBench-301 only train a small portion of the architectures fully, and the large majority part of the search space will be benched by the predictor. So it seems that such surrogate benchmark would still need to be extended with learning curve prediction anyway. Could you elaborate more on this?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

reviews.text:
Review for “Extending NAS Benchmarks with Per-Epoch Learning-Curve Surrogates for Multi-Fidelity Optimization”

Summary  
This paper tackles an important limitation in current neural architecture search (NAS) benchmarks: the lack of full learning-curve data needed by multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. The authors train a learning-curve predictor that maps architecture encodings to per-epoch validation accuracies, and use it to extend three popular benchmarks—NAS-Bench-101, NAS-Bench-301, and NAS-Bench-NLP—so that users can query predicted performance at any epoch without incurring the cost of full training. They further present a simple framework to turn single-fidelity NAS methods into multi-fidelity variants by using early stopping and learning-curve extrapolation for filtering. Evaluation via Kendall’s Tau rank correlation and KL-divergence shows that the surrogate achieves reasonable accuracy, though non-negligible errors remain. Overall, the contribution is well-motivated and generally well-executed, and I recommend acceptance (Rating: 7).

Strengths  
1. Addresses a clear and pressing gap in existing NAS benchmarks by providing full per-epoch performance information, enabling systematic research on multi-fidelity methods.  
2. Proposes a learning-curve surrogate that yields overall satisfactory performance in predicting entire validation curves.  
3. Successfully extends three widely used benchmarks—NAS-Bench-101, NAS-Bench-301, and NAS-Bench-NLP—to support per-epoch queries, vastly broadening their utility.  
4. Presents a straightforward yet effective framework for converting existing single-fidelity NAS algorithms into multi-fidelity ones via early filtering based on curve extrapolation.

Weaknesses  
1. The reported surrogate errors—as measured by rank correlation (Kendall’s Tau) and KL-divergence—are non-negligible, raising concerns about how prediction inaccuracies might distort downstream NAS evaluations.  
2. It remains unclear how these surrogate errors impact the relative performance and ranking of multi-fidelity NAS algorithms when using the extended benchmarks instead of true ground-truth curves.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. To quantify the practical impact of surrogate inaccuracies, the authors could evaluate a handful of existing multi-fidelity NAS algorithms on the extended benchmarks and compare their results to runs that use actual per-epoch ground-truth data (for example, on NAS-Bench-201 and NAS-Bench-111 where full curves are available). This comparison would clarify how much surrogate noise affects algorithm ranking and speed–accuracy trade-offs.  
2. The positive effect of feeding early-epoch accuracies as additional features to the predictor in NAS-Bench-NLP is encouraging. I suggest experimenting with the same strategy for NAS-Bench-101 and NAS-Bench-301 surrogates to see if including, say, the first few epochs of real measurements can similarly boost prediction fidelity and reduce downstream bias.

Technical Details and Discussion  
• The learning-curve predictor is evaluated primarily via Kendall’s Tau for ranking consistency and KL-divergence for distributional alignment against held-out ground-truth curves.  
• In NAS-Bench-NLP, the use of early epochs as input features substantially improves predictor accuracy, suggesting that hybrid “partial ground-truth + surrogate” approaches are promising.  
• NAS-Bench-301’s surrogate nature—where only a small subset of architectures is fully trained and most curves are purely predicted—amplifies the importance of quantifying surrogate error impact.

Conclusion  
This work makes a valuable contribution by filling a significant gap in NAS benchmark capabilities and by enabling rigorous multi-fidelity research without the prohibitive cost of full training. While the surrogate’s prediction errors warrant careful consideration, the paper acknowledges these limitations and provides directions for mitigation. I find the methodology sound, the experiments convincing, and the release of extended benchmarks likely to benefit the community. I therefore recommend acceptance (Rating: 7).

reviews.summary: The authors describe a mechanism to predict learning curves which they use to generate surrogate benchmarks from existing NAS-Bench versions without learning curves. The authors explore different ways to create learning curve embeddings, different ways to predict these embeddings using the architecture representation as an input and different ways to model the training noise. Furthermore, the authors discuss a short study on using learning curve extrapolation in context of speeding-up NAS.
reviews.main_review:
The authors provide a good description of their method, alternative ways to solve each problem faced and support their decisions with empirical evidence.

My main concern is about the usefulness of this surrogate benchmark which has problems as partly discussed by the authors. It remains unclear what impact these problems would have.

The noise modelling in this paper leads to artifacts we would not see in real learning curves. First, it is very unlikely that the noise is architecture- and hyperparameter-independent. Second, given just the very few examples in Figure 1 we observe that the predicted learning curve in early epochs can already exceed to highest value of the entire learning curve (possibly due to very high noise levels).
In general, all surrogate benchmarks have the problem that they introduce some sort of bias which will not match what you would see in reality. Therefore, these datasets are more similar to synthetic datasets than real ones. They might be nice to prototype but I wouldn’t use them to evaluate NAS methods.

The experiment on combining learning curve extrapolation is a nice reminder for the NAS community to keep these approaches in mind when comparing different approaches. However, it provides no novelty since these methods have been used in the context of NAS already (see [1]).

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors describe a mechanism to predict learning curves which they use to generate surrogate benchmarks from existing NAS-Bench versions without learning curves. The authors explore different ways to create learning curve embeddings, different ways to predict these embeddings using the architecture representation as an input and different ways to model the training noise. Furthermore, the authors discuss a short study on using learning curve extrapolation in context of speeding-up NAS.

Main Review:
The authors provide a good description of their method, alternative ways to solve each problem faced and support their decisions with empirical evidence.

My main concern is about the usefulness of this surrogate benchmark which has problems as partly discussed by the authors. It remains unclear what impact these problems would have.

The noise modelling in this paper leads to artifacts we would not see in real learning curves. First, it is very unlikely that the noise is architecture- and hyperparameter-independent. Second, given just the very few examples in Figure 1 we observe that the predicted learning curve in early epochs can already exceed to highest value of the entire learning curve (possibly due to very high noise levels).
In general, all surrogate benchmarks have the problem that they introduce some sort of bias which will not match what you would see in reality. Therefore, these datasets are more similar to synthetic datasets than real ones. They might be nice to prototype but I wouldn’t use them to evaluate NAS methods.

The experiment on combining learning curve extrapolation is a nice reminder for the NAS community to keep these approaches in mind when comparing different approaches. However, it provides no novelty since these methods have been used in the context of NAS already (see [1]).

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper introduces a framework for generating full learning-curve surrogates on NAS benchmarks that do not provide complete epoch-wise data. The authors explore several design choices—curve embeddings, regression models for interpolation, and noise‐injection schemes—and empirically justify their selections. They further demonstrate how learning‐curve extrapolation (LCE) methods can accelerate NAS by early stopping. While the methodology is clearly laid out and the experiments are generally sound, I have concerns about the realism and practical utility of the surrogate benchmarks, as well as the novelty of the LCE component. Overall, I believe the paper falls marginally below the acceptance bar (Rating: 5).

Strengths  
1. Methodological Clarity: The procedure for embedding partial learning curves, fitting predictive models, and injecting noise is described in a well-structured and reproducible manner.  
2. Comprehensive Ablations: The authors systematically compare multiple embedding strategies, regression architectures (e.g., Gaussian processes vs. neural nets), and noise‐model variants, providing quantitative justifications for their design choices.  
3. Empirical Validation: Experimental results support the selected configuration, showing that the chosen surrogate approach can recover full-curve statistics in controlled settings.  
4. LCE Reminder: The learning‐curve extrapolation study is competently executed and serves as a good reminder that extrapolation can yield meaningful speed-ups in NAS workflows.

Weaknesses  
1. Questionable Benchmark Realism: The surrogate benchmarks rely on a noise model that treats residuals as isotropic and independent of architecture or hyperparameters. In practice, noise characteristics vary significantly across models and training settings.  
2. Unnatural Artifacts: As shown in Figure 1, early-epoch predictions sometimes exceed the true maximum accuracy—a behavior rarely seen in real training curves. Moreover, the surrogates exhibit overly high variance in initial epochs.  
3. Synthetic‐Data Bias: Because the noise model is simplistic, the generated curves resemble synthetic data more than real learning dynamics. This undermines the practical usefulness of these benchmarks for NAS evaluation.  
4. Limited Novelty in LCE: The LCE experiments—while well conducted—largely replicate standard early‐stopping and extrapolation ideas that have appeared in prior NAS studies, offering little in the way of innovation.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Evaluate Real‐World Impact: Include experiments or case studies showing how surrogate artifacts affect downstream NAS rankings or algorithm comparisons in practice. Quantify any discrepancies relative to real full-curve benchmarks.  
2. Refine Noise Modeling: Develop a noise‐injection scheme that conditions on architecture features or hyperparameter settings, to better capture heteroskedasticity observed in real training runs.  
3. Mitigate Synthetic Bias: Incorporate constraints or corrections (e.g., clipping predictions at realistic maxima) to prevent implausible curve behaviors and more faithfully mirror true learning trajectories.  
4. Strengthen Validation: Before deploying these surrogates for NAS benchmarking, provide rigorous cross-validation against held-out architectures or benchmarks to demonstrate fidelity.  
5. Highlight LCE Contributions: If possible, identify and emphasize any novel twist in the extrapolation method (e.g., new loss functions, adaptivity schemes) that distinguishes it from existing approaches.

Conclusion  
The paper tackles an important need—providing full learning‐curve data when only partial observations are available—and presents a thorough empirical study of several modeling alternatives. However, the current noise‐modeling approach introduces unrealistic artifacts, raising doubts about the surrogates’ fidelity and utility for NAS benchmarking. Additionally, the LCE experiments, though competently done, lack sufficient novelty. For these reasons, I recommend rejection in its current form (Rating: 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold). I encourage the authors to address the above concerns regarding noise realism, bias mitigation, and the distinct contribution of their extrapolation study in a revised submission.

reviews.summary: Existing NAS benchmark datasets (e.g., NAS-Bench-101) do not provide full training information of architectures, which make it infeasible to evaluate the multi-fidelity algorithms (e.g., learning curve extrapolation). To address these issues, this paper create surrogate benchmark datasets, namely NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311 and NAS-Bench-NLP11, with singular value decomposition and noise modeling methods. Extensive experimental results with the full training information on these datasets show the superiority of the proposed method.
reviews.main_review:
## Positive points:
1. The paper presents a method to extend the existing single-fidelity benchmarks to multi-fidelity ones with full training information.
2. The authors create NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311, and NAS-Bench-NLP11 benchmark datasets, which may be beneficial to develop new NAS algorithm.

## Negative points:
1. Does the proposed method apply to real world NAS application? In practice, given a dataset, we may have no available training information of any architectures. In this case, how to apply the proposed method? It would be limited if the proposed method is only able to extend the existing NAS benchmarks.
2. In line 220, why do the authors sample architectures from BANANAS, local search and regularized evolution?
3. In line 225, the authors mention that the method is able to improve the performance with the accuracies from the tabular benchmark (at epochs 4, 12, 36, 108) as additional features. More details of this should be provided.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
Existing NAS benchmark datasets (e.g., NAS-Bench-101) do not provide full training information of architectures, which make it infeasible to evaluate the multi-fidelity algorithms (e.g., learning curve extrapolation). To address these issues, this paper create surrogate benchmark datasets, namely NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311 and NAS-Bench-NLP11, with singular value decomposition and noise modeling methods. Extensive experimental results with the full training information on these datasets show the superiority of the proposed method.

Main Review:
## Positive points:
1. The paper presents a method to extend the existing single-fidelity benchmarks to multi-fidelity ones with full training information.
2. The authors create NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311, and NAS-Bench-NLP11 benchmark datasets, which may be beneficial to develop new NAS algorithm.

## Negative points:
1. Does the proposed method apply to real world NAS application? In practice, given a dataset, we may have no available training information of any architectures. In this case, how to apply the proposed method? It would be limited if the proposed method is only able to extend the existing NAS benchmarks.
2. In line 220, why do the authors sample architectures from BANANAS, local search and regularized evolution?
3. In line 225, the authors mention that the method is able to improve the performance with the accuracies from the tabular benchmark (at epochs 4, 12, 36, 108) as additional features. More details of this should be provided.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper proposes a general approach to transform existing single-fidelity NAS benchmarks into multi-fidelity surrogate benchmarks by modeling full training trajectories via singular value decomposition and noise injection. Applying this recipe to three popular tabular benchmarks, the authors release NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311, and NAS-Bench-NLP11, each of which provides surrogate learning curves at multiple epochs. These resources have the potential to accelerate research in multi-fidelity NAS by enabling reproducible and fast prototyping.

Strengths  
1. The core methodology is clearly presented: by decomposing recorded training curves into a low-rank basis and adding realistic noise, one can reconstruct approximate full learning curves for architectures in a tabular benchmark.  
2. The paper delivers three new benchmark suites (NAS-Bench-111, ‑311, NLP11) covering both vision and NLP search spaces. These surrogates fill a notable gap by providing epoch-wise performance data without the cost of full training runs, thereby supporting the evaluation of multi-fidelity optimization strategies.

Weaknesses  
1. It is not obvious how this approach would extend to practical NAS tasks in domains where no exhaustive training logs are available. The surrogate construction relies entirely on pre-collected learning curves, raising questions about generalization to novel datasets or architectures.  
2. The manuscript samples architectures from three search algorithms—BANANAS, a local search procedure, and regularized evolution—but offers little justification for these choices or analysis of how representative these samples are of the full architecture space.  
3. While the paper mentions augmenting surrogate inputs with intermediate accuracies (at epochs 4, 12, 36, and 108), it provides limited explanation or empirical study on how these features influence fidelity and predictive performance of the surrogate models.

Suggestions for Improvement  
• Applicability without Pre-existing Data: Include a discussion or experiment on how one might bootstrap the surrogate pipeline in settings where no tabular benchmark exists. For instance, could small pilot studies or transfer-learning of basis functions alleviate the need for exhaustive logs?  
• Sampling Rationale: Clarify why architectures from BANANAS, local search, and regularized evolution were chosen. Does each algorithm capture distinct regions of the architecture space? Providing statistics on coverage or diversity would strengthen confidence in the surrogate’s representativeness.  
• Feature Augmentation Details: Expand the description of how intermediate epoch accuracies are integrated into the surrogate model. A step-by-step account of feature construction and an ablation showing the impact of including versus excluding these data points would be valuable to practitioners.

Overall Assessment  
The work addresses an important limitation of existing NAS benchmarks by supplying multi-fidelity surrogates and offers a clear, reproducible methodology along with publicly available datasets. However, critical questions remain about real-world applicability in the absence of complete training logs, the rationale behind sampling strategies, and the precise role of intermediate accuracy features. I find the paper thought-provoking and practically useful but feel it falls just short of the threshold for acceptance in its current form.

Rating: 5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Compressed Video Contrastive Learning

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a self-supervised method for compressed video representation learning, which helps save both storage and computation.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:

- The proposed method directly uses RGB and motion vectors in the compressed video for efficient representation learning.
- Experimental results show that the proposed method reduces the total (inference) time and storage, and achieves marginal improvement compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Weakness:

- Adding temporal learning objectives such as changing the temporal order of RGB frames or motion vectors within a clip ( as in IMRNet ), which is missing from the proposed method, would greatly help learning video representation.
- Modeling of the proposed method mostly applies the InfoNCE loss, only selecting of positive and negative samples are updated for RGB and motion vectors.
- As shown in table 1, the computation overhead largely comes from the preprocessing time. Though not an apple-to-apple comparison, parallel re-encoding for IMRNet may largely mitigate the overhead?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The reviewer would encourage the authors to discuss limitations and impacts of the proposed method, as mentioned in the 'Main Review'.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a self-supervised method for compressed video representation learning, which helps save both storage and computation.

Main Review:
Strength:

- The proposed method directly uses RGB and motion vectors in the compressed video for efficient representation learning.
- Experimental results show that the proposed method reduces the total (inference) time and storage, and achieves marginal improvement compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Weakness:

- Adding temporal learning objectives such as changing the temporal order of RGB frames or motion vectors within a clip ( as in IMRNet ), which is missing from the proposed method, would greatly help learning video representation.
- Modeling of the proposed method mostly applies the InfoNCE loss, only selecting of positive and negative samples are updated for RGB and motion vectors.
- As shown in table 1, the computation overhead largely comes from the preprocessing time. Though not an apple-to-apple comparison, parallel re-encoding for IMRNet may largely mitigate the overhead?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The reviewer would encourage the authors to discuss limitations and impacts of the proposed method, as mentioned in the 'Main Review'.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a self-supervised approach for learning video representations directly from compressed streams, exploiting both RGB keyframes and motion vectors without full decoding. By tailoring positive/negative sampling in an InfoNCE contrastive loss, the method reduces storage needs and inference time, achieving small but consistent gains over recent state-of-the-art baselines.

Strengths  
1. Direct use of compressed data. The core innovation lies in bypassing conventional full decoding: the model ingests raw RGB keyframes and motion vectors for representation learning.  
2. Efficient storage and computation. Experiments (e.g., Table 1) clearly demonstrate that the proposed pipeline lowers both inference time and disk footprint relative to decoding-based methods.  
3. Competitive performance. Despite focusing on efficiency, the approach yields marginal improvements over strong baselines, indicating that compressed-stream features can indeed support high-quality self-supervision.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited temporal supervision. The current framework relies solely on static InfoNCE-based contrastive learning and does not include auxiliary temporal objectives (such as frame or motion-vector order prediction), which could deepen temporal understanding.  
2. Narrow pretext task design. Beyond updating positive/negative selection for RGB and motion vectors, no additional self-supervised tasks or contextual modeling are explored to enrich video features.  
3. Preprocessing bottleneck. As shown in Table 1, decoding and data preparation remain the dominant share of runtime, undermining some of the claimed efficiency benefits.  
4. Fairness of comparison to IMRNet. The baseline IMRNet is penalized by serial re-encoding overhead; adopting parallel re-encoding strategies could narrow the gap, suggesting the current comparison may overstate the proposed method’s speed advantage.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Incorporate temporal learning objectives. Adding tasks such as shuffled-order detection or predicting motion-vector sequences could encourage richer temporal embeddings.  
2. Enrich the self-supervised formulation. Exploring additional pretext tasks or contextual cues beyond basic contrastive sampling may further boost representation quality.  
3. Address preprocessing overhead. Investigate or at least discuss parallel re-encoding schemes for IMRNet to ensure a fair runtime comparison and explore optimizations to reduce the model’s own decoding cost.  
4. Reflect on limitations and impact. A dedicated discussion of the approach’s boundaries (e.g., types of video content where compressed features may fail) and potential societal consequences (e.g., privacy, surveillance) would strengthen the manuscript.

Conclusion and Recommendation  
Overall, this work offers a meaningful step toward efficient video representation learning by leveraging compressed streams directly and achieves modest performance gains with lower storage and inference costs. However, the reliance on a single contrastive objective and the remaining preprocessing bottleneck limit its broader impact. I therefore rate the paper a 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold.

reviews.summary: The authors propose a self-supervised learning (SSL) method for learning strong visual representations based on compressed videos. Compared with prior work, which sees videos in RGB only, the proposed method is more efficient and leverages the motion information that's already accessible in compressed videos. The authors propose to use a contrastive learning method to supervise on kind of modality with correspondence of another modality. On multiple datasets (mainly UCF and HMDB), good performance is achieved, with both linear probing and transfer learning settings.
reviews.main_review:
[originality: medium-high]
 - The idea of using compressed videos for self-supervised learning is novel/original. Most prior work that operates on compressed videos focues on supervised learning setting only. The implementation of this idea is standard/expected (this is not a drawback).

[quality: medium-high]
 - The paper covers useful ablation study that I'd expect. In particular, the authors demonstrate the usefulness of the compressed-video modality (MV) compared to RGB only (Table 3). Compared to prior work, the performance is also strong while being significantly more efficient. The ablation study that compares with optical flow and low-res optical flow is also interesting (Table 2).

[clarity: high]
 - I think this paper is overall clearly writen. One question about implementation details: How is the similarity calculated in the hard-positive mining procedure?

[significance: medium-high]
 - The area of SSL draws a lot of attention in our community and is of great potential. I think this work extends our knowledge from a new dimension with the use of compressed videos. I think this work can lead to promising new directions in SSL research.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
1. In the paper, the authors perform experiments/evaluation solely on videos. I was wondering if the method learns good representations for "image" tasks as well. Namely, leveraging MV can potentially give us better RGB representations for images, too. I wonder what the authors think about this.
2. If the hard-positive mining is based on the learned features, would there be any "chichen-and-egg" problem introduced by the proposed procedure?
3. I like the comparison to MV_{CoViAR}, but I wonder how each specific compression setting affects the results. For example, how does GOP size, the use of B-frame vs P-frame, etc. affect results.

Regarding societal impact, I don't see anything particularly concerning (or more concerning) than other SSL work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
This paper presents a compelling self-supervised framework that taps into compressed video modalities—specifically, motion vectors—to learn rich spatio-temporal representations without the heavy cost of optical flow computation. By treating RGB frames and motion vectors as two views in a contrastive learning setup, the authors show that their method yields performance on par with (or even exceeding) prior approaches that rely on full optical flow, all while substantially cutting down storage and processing overhead. Extensive experiments on UCF101 and HMDB51 under both linear probing and full fine-tuning confirm the effectiveness of the proposed approach, and a thorough suite of ablation studies examines the relative merits of motion vectors, optical flows, and low-resolution flows.

Strengths  
1. Novel Use of Compressed Modalities: Introducing motion vectors as a second modality for self-supervision is an innovative leap that addresses the cost bottleneck of conventional flow-based methods.  
2. Strong Empirical Results: The approach delivers state-of-the-art or highly competitive accuracy on UCF101 and HMDB51, demonstrating the practical utility of the learned features.  
3. Efficiency Gains: By sidestepping full optical flow decoding, the method reduces both storage footprint and computational load, making it attractive for large-scale or resource-constrained settings.  
4. Comprehensive Ablations: The paper systematically compares motion vectors to RGB-only training, optical flow, and even low-resolution flow, clearly illustrating where the efficiency-accuracy tradeoffs lie.  
5. Clarity of Presentation: Overall, the manuscript is well structured and clearly written, guiding readers through the methodology, experimental setup, and key findings with ease.

Weaknesses  
1. Standard Implementation Choices: While the core idea is novel, many implementation aspects (network backbones, contrastive loss schedules, data augmentations) follow established conventions, which slightly diminishes the methodological novelty.  
2. Hard-Positive Mining Detail: The paper does not fully explain how similarity is measured during the hard-positive mining step—e.g., which distance metric is used, how thresholds are chosen, and whether this metric evolves over training.  
3. Limited Task Scope: All evaluations are confined to video datasets. It remains unclear whether motion-vector–based pretraining could transfer benefits to static image tasks or other modalities.  
4. Potential Chicken-and-Egg Issue: Hard-positive mining relies on the very feature embeddings that are being jointly learned, raising questions about stability and convergence early in training.  
5. Compression Parameter Analysis: The impact of different compression settings (GOP size variations, the balance of B-frames vs. P-frames) on representation quality is not studied, leaving an important dimension of practical deployment unexamined.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Provide full details on the hard-positive mining procedure, including the precise similarity metric, thresholding strategy, and any schedule that adapts these choices over time.  
2. Explore or at least discuss whether the proposed motion-vector pretraining can boost performance on image representation benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet) or other downstream tasks.  
3. Conduct an ablation on video compression configurations, analyzing how varying GOP sizes and the ratio of B-frames to P-frames affect downstream accuracy and efficiency.  
4. Investigate mechanisms to mitigate the circular dependency in hard-positive mining—perhaps by using a momentum encoder or a warm-up stage to stabilize embeddings before mining strict positives.  

In summary, this work fills an important gap by combining efficiency and representation quality in video self-supervision, leveraging under-utilized compressed video signals. Despite some areas that would benefit from deeper analysis and clarification, the paper makes a strong empirical and conceptual contribution. I recommend acceptance.  

Rating: 7: Good paper, accept.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The authors propose a self-supervised learning (SSL) method for learning strong visual representations based on compressed videos. Compared with prior work, which sees videos in RGB only, the proposed method is more efficient and leverages the motion information that's already accessible in compressed videos. The authors propose to use a contrastive learning method to supervise on kind of modality with correspondence of another modality. On multiple datasets (mainly UCF and HMDB), good performance is achieved, with both linear probing and transfer learning settings.

Main Review:
[originality: medium-high]
 - The idea of using compressed videos for self-supervised learning is novel/original. Most prior work that operates on compressed videos focues on supervised learning setting only. The implementation of this idea is standard/expected (this is not a drawback).

[quality: medium-high]
 - The paper covers useful ablation study that I'd expect. In particular, the authors demonstrate the usefulness of the compressed-video modality (MV) compared to RGB only (Table 3). Compared to prior work, the performance is also strong while being significantly more efficient. The ablation study that compares with optical flow and low-res optical flow is also interesting (Table 2).

[clarity: high]
 - I think this paper is overall clearly writen. One question about implementation details: How is the similarity calculated in the hard-positive mining procedure?

[significance: medium-high]
 - The area of SSL draws a lot of attention in our community and is of great potential. I think this work extends our knowledge from a new dimension with the use of compressed videos. I think this work can lead to promising new directions in SSL research.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
1. In the paper, the authors perform experiments/evaluation solely on videos. I was wondering if the method learns good representations for "image" tasks as well. Namely, leveraging MV can potentially give us better RGB representations for images, too. I wonder what the authors think about this.
2. If the hard-positive mining is based on the learned features, would there be any "chichen-and-egg" problem introduced by the proposed procedure?
3. I like the comparison to MV_{CoViAR}, but I wonder how each specific compression setting affects the results. For example, how does GOP size, the use of B-frame vs P-frame, etc. affect results.

Regarding societal impact, I don't see anything particularly concerning (or more concerning) than other SSL work.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

Achieves strong performance on UCF101 and HMDB51 benchmarks in both linear probing and transfer learning settings.
Demonstrates significant efficiency gains compared to prior methods that use RGB only or require optical flow computation.
Provides comprehensive ablation studies, including comparisons between motion vectors, optical flows, and low-resolution optical flows.

Unclear how similarity is calculated in the hard-positive mining procedure.
Evaluation is limited to video tasks; no experiments on image representation tasks are provided.
Potential “chicken-and-egg” issue due to hard-positive mining based on features that are simultaneously being learned.
The impact of different video compression settings (e.g., GOP size, B-frames vs. P-frames) on performance is not analyzed.
suggestions: Clarify the details of the similarity computation used in the hard-positive mining stage.
Include experiments or discussion on whether motion vectors can improve image representation tasks.
Analyze how varying compression configurations (GOP size, B-frames vs. P-frames) affect downstream performance.
Discuss and mitigate potential issues arising from hard-positive mining based on evolving feature representations.
main_points: Leverages motion vectors from compressed videos to introduce an additional modality for self-supervised learning.
Exploits contrastive learning with cross-modal supervision between RGB frames and motion vectors.
Shows that motion vectors can approach or exceed the utility of optical flows while greatly reducing storage and computation.
Validates the approach with extensive ablations and strong results on standard video benchmarks.
Fills a gap in prior work by addressing both efficiency and representation quality simultaneously.
technical_details: Uses a contrastive learning framework where RGB and motion vector streams supervise each other.
Ablation studies compare motion vectors versus RGB only, and optical flow versus low-resolution optical flow.
Evaluations are conducted on UCF101 and HMDB51 with both linear probing and full fine-tuning.
Comparison is made to an MV_CoViAR baseline, which decodes motion vectors only from P-frames.
Hard-positive mining relies on measuring similarity between learned feature embeddings.
Raises questions about the effects of compression parameters like GOP size and use of B-frames vs. P-frames.

reviews.summary: The paper tackles the problem of self-supervised video representation learning. The authors propose a compressed video based self-supervised learning approach, where the model is forced to learn matching representations between raw RGB clip and its corresponding motion vector clip. This is achieved using a relatively standard contrastive learning formulation. The authors then demonstrate that their model achieves state-of-the-art performance on a few downstream tasks (i.e., action recognition, and video retrieval) while also being significantly more efficient (particularly in terms of the video processing time).
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
- The paper is written clearly, and it addresses a well motivated and an important research problem.
- A nice idea of using compressed video for self-supervised feature learning.
- The proposed approach delivers performance that is comparable/better to previous methods (e.g. CoCLR [Han et al., 2020b]) while being much more efficient.
- Thorough ablation studies.

Weaknesses:
- The major weakness of this paper is its limited technical novelty. While the idea of using compressed videos in the context of self-supervised learning is new and interesting, overall the proposed approach is largely built on the ideas from CoCLR [Han et al., 2020b]. Having said this, the proposed approach seems to be both more effective and more efficient than CoCLR. Therefore, I think this would be a good "poster" paper.
- The proposed approach performs quite a bit worse than the recent CVPR 2021 methods such as CVRL, and especially the method of Feichtenhofer et al. "A Large-Scale Study on Unsupervised Spatiotemporal Representation Learning" (CVPR 2021). Having said this, given a very large computational cost of both of these prior methods (i.e. 800 epoch training), I think that it would be unfair to compare the proposed MVCGC approach directly with these methods. The authors should still add the second reference to their related work section, and also to their quantitative comparisons. A discussion on why the direct comparison between these methods is unfair would also be useful.

Questions / Minor Details:
- In L85-88, the authors mention that unlike CoCLR, they construct sample pairs between different views of different clip (i.e., RGB frames of one clip and motion vectors of another clip from the same video). However, the caption of Fig.3 says that the motion vector clip corresponds to the raw RGB clip. Therefore, I was confused, which one is it? Also, if it's the former, could the authors also ablate the latter?
- Typo in Table 3 (it should be Retrieval instead of Retrival). 
- In the third part of Table 4, the authors highlight/bold the numbers corresponding to their approach even though CVRL achieves higher accuracy.
- If the authors could include the linear evaluation on Kinetics-400 (similar to "A Large-Scale Study on Unsupervised Spatiotemporal Representation Learning") that would be great. I think the evaluation on UCF and HMDB is a bit saturated and outdated. Therefore, it would be useful to have additional numbers on Kinetics, which is a bit more challenging dataset.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitations were not discussed.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper tackles the problem of self-supervised video representation learning. The authors propose a compressed video based self-supervised learning approach, where the model is forced to learn matching representations between raw RGB clip and its corresponding motion vector clip. This is achieved using a relatively standard contrastive learning formulation. The authors then demonstrate that their model achieves state-of-the-art performance on a few downstream tasks (i.e., action recognition, and video retrieval) while also being significantly more efficient (particularly in terms of the video processing time).

Main Review:
Strengths:
- The paper is written clearly, and it addresses a well motivated and an important research problem.
- A nice idea of using compressed video for self-supervised feature learning.
- The proposed approach delivers performance that is comparable/better to previous methods (e.g. CoCLR [Han et al., 2020b]) while being much more efficient.
- Thorough ablation studies.

Weaknesses:
- The major weakness of this paper is its limited technical novelty. While the idea of using compressed videos in the context of self-supervised learning is new and interesting, overall the proposed approach is largely built on the ideas from CoCLR [Han et al., 2020b]. Having said this, the proposed approach seems to be both more effective and more efficient than CoCLR. Therefore, I think this would be a good "poster" paper.
- The proposed approach performs quite a bit worse than the recent CVPR 2021 methods such as CVRL, and especially the method of Feichtenhofer et al. "A Large-Scale Study on Unsupervised Spatiotemporal Representation Learning" (CVPR 2021). Having said this, given a very large computational cost of both of these prior methods (i.e. 800 epoch training), I think that it would be unfair to compare the proposed MVCGC approach directly with these methods. The authors should still add the second reference to their related work section, and also to their quantitative comparisons. A discussion on why the direct comparison between these methods is unfair would also be useful.

Questions / Minor Details:
- In L85-88, the authors mention that unlike CoCLR, they construct sample pairs between different views of different clip (i.e., RGB frames of one clip and motion vectors of another clip from the same video). However, the caption of Fig.3 says that the motion vector clip corresponds to the raw RGB clip. Therefore, I was confused, which one is it? Also, if it's the former, could the authors also ablate the latter?
- Typo in Table 3 (it should be Retrieval instead of Retrival). 
- In the third part of Table 4, the authors highlight/bold the numbers corresponding to their approach even though CVRL achieves higher accuracy.
- If the authors could include the linear evaluation on Kinetics-400 (similar to "A Large-Scale Study on Unsupervised Spatiotemporal Representation Learning") that would be great. I think the evaluation on UCF and HMDB is a bit saturated and outdated. Therefore, it would be useful to have additional numbers on Kinetics, which is a bit more challenging dataset.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitations were not discussed.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review for “Compressed-Video Contrastive Learning”  
Rating: 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

Summary  
This paper introduces a self-supervised learning framework that leverages compressed-video motion vectors as a lightweight alternative to optical flow. By matching RGB clips with motion-vector clips using a contrastive loss, the approach achieves comparable or superior performance to CoCLR while substantially reducing video processing overhead. Extensive ablation studies demonstrate the impact of key design choices, and empirical results on UCF101 and HMDB51 confirm the method’s efficiency–accuracy trade-off.

Strengths  
• Clarity and motivation: The manuscript is well written, and the problem of efficient video representation learning is clearly motivated.  
• Innovative use of compression artifacts: Employing motion vectors from compressed video is a clever, resource-saving alternative to optical flow.  
• Strong efficiency gains: The proposed method matches or outperforms CoCLR with significantly lower compute cost.  
• Comprehensive ablations: The authors systematically explore architectural and training variations, providing valuable insights.

Weaknesses  
• Limited novelty: The technical contributions largely extend ideas from CoCLR, with only incremental modifications.  
• Performance gap to recent methods: The approach underperforms large-scale SSL methods such as CVRL and the study by Feichtenhofer et al. (CVPR 2021).  
• Inconsistent description: Lines 85–88 describe sample-pair construction differently than the caption of Figure 3.  
• Presentation issues: Table 3 contains a typo (“Retrival” vs. “Retrieval”), and Table 4 improperly bolds results that are not the best.  
• Missing discussion: There is no treatment of broader limitations or societal impact.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Related work and comparisons: Include and discuss the Feichtenhofer et al. CVPR 2021 reference, and clarify why direct comparison may be unfair (e.g., 800-epoch training).  
2. Sample-pair clarity: Resolve the inconsistency between the text and Figure 3 regarding whether pairs are from the same or different clips, and consider ablation of both pairing strategies.  
3. Table corrections: Fix the typo in Table 3 and remove or adjust bold formatting in Table 4 so it accurately highlights the top results.  
4. Additional evaluation: Add a linear-probing experiment on Kinetics-400 to bolster the UCF/HMDB findings.  
5. Limitations and impact: Briefly discuss any practical limitations of compressed-video representation learning and potential societal implications.

Conclusion  
Overall, this paper presents a practical and efficient approach to video contrastive learning with strong experimental support. While its novelty is somewhat limited and there remain performance gaps to the latest large-scale methods, the clear writing, solid ablations, and significant efficiency gains make it a valuable contribution. I recommend acceptance.

reviews.summary: This paper presents a self-supervised algorithm for representation learning of videos. The authors propose using RGB clips and Motion Vectors (MV) available in compressed videos to reduce the computational burden of processing. The two streams are seen as views in the contrastive setting: positive pairs are composed of RGB and MV clips of the same video, negative ones have clips belonging to different samples. Moreover, hard positive mining extends the set of positive pairs by seeking other videos that are similar in both views. The approach compares favorably on action datasets UC101 and HMDB51 for both action recognition and video retrieval tasks.
reviews.main_review:
## Strenghts
* The contribution of this paper is timely: the computational requirements of contrastive learning hinders its applicability on video representation learning. Compressed videos enables the shrinkage of required time and storage.
* The work proves how effective use of the less informative MV is on par with the computationally demanding optical flow
* Extensive experiments on action recognition and retrieval prove that the proposed gets competitive results with state of the art methods: linear evaluation and full fine-tuning accuracy for action recognition and R@k metrics for retrieval.

## Weaknesses
* The method builds on [1] by extending the single-view contrastive approach to learn the correspondence between two views and changing the hard-positive condition. However, it is well known that cross-view contrastive is beneficial [2, 3, 4]. Yet, the showed technique for harvesting hard positives is an incremental contribution to the literature.
* How cross guidance and hard positive mining contribute to the final performance is not well disentangled and it's not easy to understand what their impact is. What's the performance loss without hard-positives? What is the effect of using hard positives in the initialization phase? What is the performance of the ensemble of RGB and MV model right after initialization?

### Minor
* Table 2: I find somewhat surprising that the same technique applied to OF gets lower performances, could the authors provide some explanations/insights?
* Line 258: concerning the claim "MVCGC is robust to motion vectors under different encoding configurations": is not really clear how this is proved. In table 2 MV_CoViAr is used for initialization only.
* Line 303: I think that it is "by retrieval with only" RGB "are even higher"
* Appendix, fig 1: the underlying assumptions is that semantically similar videos are close in both RGB and MV space. How do the authors think the model would handle failures of such assumption, e.g., mopping a gymnastic floor, biking in a volleyball arena, etc.?
--------------------
* **Correctness:** The proposed method and the experimental evaluation are sound.
* **Clarity:** The paper is well written and easy to follow. The content is well organized: contribution is expressed concisely and the experimental part is well explained.
* **Relation to prior work:** Authors relates their work with previous literature while concisely highlighting their contribution.
* **Reproducibility:** Architecture, method and optimization hyperparameters are available for implementation, missing augmentation parameters (random cropping, blurring, jittering) and missing code limits the reproducibility of the work though.
* **Originality**: The contribution of this paper is incremental with respect to cited papers, the real advantages of the technique are not thoroughly highlighted in the ablation part.
------------------------
[1] Han, Tengda, Weidi Xie, and Andrew Zisserman. "Self-supervised Co-Training for Video Representation Learning." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 33 (2020): 5679-5690.

[2] Tian, Yonglong, Dilip Krishnan, and Phillip Isola. "Contrastive multiview coding." Computer Vision–ECCV 2020: 16th European Conference, Glasgow, UK, August 23–28, 2020, Proceedings, Part XI 16. Springer International Publishing, 2020.

[3] Bachman, Philip, R. Devon Hjelm, and William Buchwalter. "Learning Representations by Maximizing Mutual Information Across Views." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 32 (2019): 15535-15545.

[4] Chen, Ting, et al. "A simple framework for contrastive learning of visual representations." International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2020.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: N/A. However, I would argue that no specific discussion is needed on potential negative societal impact for a contrastive learning method.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper presents a self-supervised algorithm for representation learning of videos. The authors propose using RGB clips and Motion Vectors (MV) available in compressed videos to reduce the computational burden of processing. The two streams are seen as views in the contrastive setting: positive pairs are composed of RGB and MV clips of the same video, negative ones have clips belonging to different samples. Moreover, hard positive mining extends the set of positive pairs by seeking other videos that are similar in both views. The approach compares favorably on action datasets UC101 and HMDB51 for both action recognition and video retrieval tasks.

Main Review:
## Strenghts
* The contribution of this paper is timely: the computational requirements of contrastive learning hinders its applicability on video representation learning. Compressed videos enables the shrinkage of required time and storage.
* The work proves how effective use of the less informative MV is on par with the computationally demanding optical flow
* Extensive experiments on action recognition and retrieval prove that the proposed gets competitive results with state of the art methods: linear evaluation and full fine-tuning accuracy for action recognition and R@k metrics for retrieval.

## Weaknesses
* The method builds on [1] by extending the single-view contrastive approach to learn the correspondence between two views and changing the hard-positive condition. However, it is well known that cross-view contrastive is beneficial [2, 3, 4]. Yet, the showed technique for harvesting hard positives is an incremental contribution to the literature.
* How cross guidance and hard positive mining contribute to the final performance is not well disentangled and it's not easy to understand what their impact is. What's the performance loss without hard-positives? What is the effect of using hard positives in the initialization phase? What is the performance of the ensemble of RGB and MV model right after initialization?

### Minor
* Table 2: I find somewhat surprising that the same technique applied to OF gets lower performances, could the authors provide some explanations/insights?
* Line 258: concerning the claim "MVCGC is robust to motion vectors under different encoding configurations": is not really clear how this is proved. In table 2 MV_CoViAr is used for initialization only.
* Line 303: I think that it is "by retrieval with only" RGB "are even higher"
* Appendix, fig 1: the underlying assumptions is that semantically similar videos are close in both RGB and MV space. How do the authors think the model would handle failures of such assumption, e.g., mopping a gymnastic floor, biking in a volleyball arena, etc.?
--------------------
* **Correctness:** The proposed method and the experimental evaluation are sound.
* **Clarity:** The paper is well written and easy to follow. The content is well organized: contribution is expressed concisely and the experimental part is well explained.
* **Relation to prior work:** Authors relates their work with previous literature while concisely highlighting their contribution.
* **Reproducibility:** Architecture, method and optimization hyperparameters are available for implementation, missing augmentation parameters (random cropping, blurring, jittering) and missing code limits the reproducibility of the work though.
* **Originality**: The contribution of this paper is incremental with respect to cited papers, the real advantages of the technique are not thoroughly highlighted in the ablation part.
------------------------
[1] Han, Tengda, Weidi Xie, and Andrew Zisserman. "Self-supervised Co-Training for Video Representation Learning." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 33 (2020): 5679-5690.

[2] Tian, Yonglong, Dilip Krishnan, and Phillip Isola. "Contrastive multiview coding." Computer Vision–ECCV 2020: 16th European Conference, Glasgow, UK, August 23–28, 2020, Proceedings, Part XI 16. Springer International Publishing, 2020.

[3] Bachman, Philip, R. Devon Hjelm, and William Buchwalter. "Learning Representations by Maximizing Mutual Information Across Views." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 32 (2019): 15535-15545.

[4] Chen, Ting, et al. "A simple framework for contrastive learning of visual representations." International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2020.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
N/A. However, I would argue that no specific discussion is needed on potential negative societal impact for a contrastive learning method.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

* **Relation to prior work:** Authors relates their work with previous literature while concisely highlighting their contribution.
* **Reproducibility:** Architecture, method and optimization hyperparameters are available for implementation, missing augmentation parameters (random cropping, blurring, jittering) and missing code limits the reproducibility of the work though.
* **Originality**: The contribution of this paper is incremental with respect to cited papers, the real advantages of the technique are not thoroughly highlighted in the ablation part.
------------------------
[1] Han, Tengda, Weidi Xie, and Andrew Zisserman. "Self-supervised Co-Training for Video Representation Learning." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 33 (2020): 5679-5690.

[2] Tian, Yonglong, Dilip Krishnan, and Phillip Isola. "Contrastive multiview coding." Computer Vision–ECCV 2020: 16th European Conference, Glasgow, UK, August 23–28, 2020, Proceedings, Part XI 16. Springer International Publishing, 2020.

[3] Bachman, Philip, R. Devon Hjelm, and William Buchwalter. "Learning Representations by Maximizing Mutual Information Across Views." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 32 (2019): 15535-15545.

[4] Chen, Ting, et al. "A simple framework for contrastive learning of visual representations." International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2020.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
N/A. However, I would argue that no specific discussion is needed on potential negative societal impact for a contrastive learning method.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: Unifying Gradient Estimators for Meta-Reinforcement Learning  via Off-Policy Evaluation

reviews.summary:
The paper uses the recently developed framework of Taylor expansions for value functions (ref [29] in the paper), expands the results, and shows how they could be applied to Off-Policy Evaluation, and by a non-trivial extension to meta-learning. The main algorthmic contribution of the paper is the application of the ideas to meta-learning, but I feel the main contribution is a conceptual one, further developing the ideas from [29] and drawing theoretical connections between OPE and meta-learning.

The paper includes an experimental section demonstrating marginal improvement over baselines in several domains.
reviews.main_review:
Overview: Despite some flaws, I think this is a good paper and leaning towards acceptance. I think the two main limitations of the paper are:
(1) It draws very heavily on the theory and novelty of [29] and therefore its own novelty is a bit limited.
(2) The empirical improvements obtained for meta-learning (the problem the paper sets out to advance) are limited.
Additionally, there are some changes that should be made to make the paper clearer, but I think those can be easily fixed for the camera ready if the paper is accepted. Despite the drawbacks, the reason I support acceptance is that I feel the additional contributions on top of the already interesting results of [29] are non-trivial, and this is a potentially interesting research direction which could provide insight into many aspects of RL. For example, viewing meta-learning through the lens of Taylor expansions for value functions allows for a very clear connection and unification of derivatives estimations of value functions and OPE.

A general suggestion for the authors which I feel might improve the overall story of the paper, is to lean more heavily on the theoretical results and their ability to shed light on interesting insights such as the connection between OPE and meta-learning, and OPE to high order derivatives. At least my personal experience reading the paper was expecting significant novelty and improvement of results on meta-learning, which set me up for a disappointment. It was only on a second read that I appreciated the theoretical insights in the first half of the paper more. Additionally, I would set up the connection with [29] more - the authors do a good enough job of properly citing [29], but not enough to make it clear that the reader should be familiar with [29] to fully appreciate this paper. After emphasizing the theory and insights, I think the meta-learning algorithm and result can be presented as a cool implication (this is obviously a very subjective suggestion).

Some more specific comments:
- In lines 120-122 regarding how the approximation maintains the higher-order dependencies on \theta. I feel like I'm missing something and the sentence is failing to convey an idea it is trying to. Obviously when you make an approximation in some parameter you maintain higher order terms in the small parameter, and choose how many terms to keep. So this statement seems trivial and I'd like to know if there's something deeper which the authors are trying to convey and I'm missing.
- In section 3.2 and Proposition 3.1 - Is K a typo and should be m? (if yes, it is a consistent typo, which is why I'm confused). If it isn't a typo, I couldn't see where K is defined, and also can't follow the proof of Proportion 3.1, so this is a point I would really like clarification on.
- In section 5.1, the authors claim to investigate the bias/variance tradeoff of their method, but only show results for the overall MSE of estimating the derivative. I think the authors should show explicit plots of the decomposed variance and bias of each estimator shown.
- very minor - the y-labels in Figure 2 a-b are cut.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
The authors do a good job of discussing the limitations of their method compared to alternative methods.

The paper is mostly theoretical and I don't see a potential negative soceital impact.

reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper proposes a unified theoretical framework that leverages high-order Taylor expansions of value functions to bridge off‐policy evaluation (OPE) and meta‐learning in reinforcement learning. Building on the framework of prior work [29], the authors derive new expressions for gradient estimators, establish connections between OPE bias/variance trade‐offs and meta‐learning updates, and validate their approach on several benchmark tasks. While the empirical gains over strong baselines are modest, the work offers a clear conceptual contribution by showing how Taylor‐based expansions can simultaneously address evaluation and adaptation challenges.

Strengths  
1. Conceptual Unification: The paper’s main contribution is theoretical—it elegantly extends Taylor expansions for value functions to encompass both OPE and meta‐learning, offering a unified view that was not previously articulated.  
2. Non‐trivial Extension of [29]: Although building on prior methods, the authors introduce several non‐obvious generalizations and clarify how higher‐order terms impact both evaluation accuracy and adaptation performance.  
3. Clear Theoretical Framework: The presentation of the framework linking off‐policy estimators, high‐order derivatives, and meta‐gradient updates is well­motivated and likely to spark further investigations in the RL community.  
4. Empirical Validation: Experiments demonstrate consistent, if marginal, improvements over competitive baselines in meta‐learning benchmarks, supporting the practical relevance of the theoretical insights.  
5. Research Direction: By framing OPE and meta‐learning within a common Taylor expansion perspective, the paper opens up a promising line of inquiry that could yield broader insights into bias/variance trade‐offs in learning algorithms.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Novelty Over [29]: The core technical machinery still heavily relies on the setup introduced in [29], which slightly diminishes the perceived originality of the contribution.  
2. Marginal Empirical Gains: While the proposed estimators outperform baselines, the improvements in meta‐learning tasks are relatively small, raising questions about practical impact.  
3. Clarity Issues in Exposition: Certain passages—most notably the claim in lines 120–122 about maintaining higher‐order dependencies on θ—are hard to parse.  
4. Confusing Notation and Proofs: In Section 3.2 and Proposition 3.1, the use of “K” instead of “m” (or vice versa) and the lack of a clear definition for K make the derivation difficult to follow.  
5. Incomplete Bias/Variance Analysis: The experiments in Section 5.1 report only overall MSE of the gradient estimators, without separating out bias and variance components, which would better illustrate the trade‐offs discussed in the theory.  
6. Presentation Quirks: Figure 2(a)–(b) have partially cut y‐axis labels that hinder readability.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Reframe Emphasis on Theory: Consider restructuring the paper to foreground the theoretical contributions—present the Taylor‐expansion unification and bias/variance insights first, and then discuss meta‐learning as a natural application.  
2. Clarify Lines 120–122: Rewrite this sentence to explicitly state how the approximation preserves higher‐order dependence on θ, perhaps with a brief illustrative example.  
3. Fix Notation in Section 3.2: Replace or define “K” consistently (or confirm if it was intended to be “m”), and ensure all symbols are introduced before use. A more detailed sketch of the proof of Proposition 3.1 would also help.  
4. Decompose Bias and Variance in Experiments: Add explicit plots or tables in Section 5.1 that report bias and variance separately for each estimator, instead of only overall MSE. This will concretely demonstrate the theoretical claims.  
5. Clean Up Figures: Adjust the axes in Figure 2(a)–(b) so labels are fully visible and the plots are easier to interpret.  
6. Strengthen Discussion of Novelty: In your related‐work section, more explicitly delineate which technical steps are new versus which follow directly from [29], so readers can appreciate the paper’s distinct contributions.

Overall Assessment  
This paper delivers a valuable conceptual advance by unifying off‐policy evaluation and meta‐learning through Taylor expansions, and it provides solid, if incremental, empirical support. With clearer exposition, corrected notation, and a more thorough bias/variance analysis, the manuscript will offer even greater impact. I recommend acceptance (Rating: 7—Good paper, accept).

reviews.summary: This paper considers meta-RL with gradient-based adaptations, which relies heavily on the estimation of the Hessians of value functions. Though there are previous approaches that focus on unbiased/biased estimations of Hessian. This paper gives a unified view for estimating higher-order derivatives of value functions, through the lens of off-policy evaluation. This subsumes several previous works, also sheds some light on the bias-variance tradeoff of the estimate. Empirically, the new proposed second-order estimate is also incorporated in meta-RL algorithms to showcase the empirical efficiency.
reviews.main_review:
(Originality): The main contributions I see from this paper are: (1). a unified framework for estimating higher-order derivatives of value functions, through OPE methods. (2). the use of Taylor expansion in the value function, which controls the number of IS ratios in the value estimate, hence sheds some light on the bias-variance trade-off. (3). also a new method arising from this framework.

(Quality): The motivation is clear, and the method is clearly stated. Some comments here include:
[1]. For the bias-variance trade-off with the Taylor expansion, how to select the optimal $K$? Also, how does this bias-variance trade-off translate into the downstream meta-gradient estimations? Any comments on this?
[2]. In experiment section 5.1, the bias-variance trade-off examination seems a little bit confusing to me. It seems the argument here is the second-order method achieves a better trade-off through high correlation with the ground truth. Is there any way to empirically examine the bias and variance in this tabular setting?
[3]. For the continuous control problem, it seems the improvement from the new proposed approach is pretty marginal since the bias might be a dominating issue here. Are there any environments/MDPs/settings that we can expect a larger improvement of it? It would be great to include some discussions/new experiments on this.

(Clarity): The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitations are discussed in Section 3, while it seems it does not have any societal impact.

reviews.text:
Review for “A Unified Off-Policy Evaluation Framework for Higher-Order Meta-RL Derivatives”

Rating: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)

Summary  
This paper introduces a unified framework for off-policy evaluation of higher-order derivatives in meta-reinforcement learning. By interpreting various prior Hessian estimation methods through the lens of a Taylor expansion in the number of importance sampling ratios, the authors derive a controllable bias–variance trade-off parameterized by the expansion order K. Within this framework, they propose a novel second-order estimator and demonstrate its integration into existing meta-RL algorithms, evaluating both a tabular domain (Section 5.1) and a suite of continuous control benchmarks.

Strengths  
- The problem statement is clearly motivated, and the exposition throughout is easy to follow.  
- The framework elegantly subsumes several prior Hessian estimation techniques, highlighting the common structure behind them.  
- Employing a Taylor expansion to interpolate between unbiased but high-variance and biased but low-variance estimates is an insightful contribution.  
- The derivation of a new second-order estimator offers a concrete advancement and is seamlessly plugged into meta-RL workflows.  
- The paper is organized logically, helping the reader appreciate both theoretical and practical aspects.

Weaknesses  
- It remains unclear how a practitioner should choose the optimal Taylor expansion order K in a real-world setting.  
- The manuscript does not sufficiently analyze how bias and variance in the Hessian estimates propagate to the overall meta-gradient performance.  
- In Section 5.1, the evaluation of bias and variance relies on correlation metrics with ground-truth derivatives rather than directly reporting bias and variance, making the results difficult to interpret.  
- On continuous control tasks, the empirical gains from the proposed second-order estimator are rather marginal, raising questions about the practical impact in more complex environments.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Provide practical guidance or formal criteria for selecting the Taylor expansion order K, perhaps based on computational budget, variance thresholds, or task characteristics.  
2. Include an analysis or discussion—analytical or empirical—detailing how the bias–variance trade-off in Hessian estimation influences the final meta-gradient bias and variance.  
3. In the tabular evaluation, report direct measurements of bias and variance (e.g., mean error and variance statistics) instead of relying solely on correlation with ground-truth derivatives.  
4. To strengthen the empirical contribution, consider adding benchmarks or settings (e.g., harder exploration tasks, sparse-reward domains, or larger-scale meta-RL problems) where the new second-order method yields more substantial improvements.  
5. Augment the discussion section (or add targeted experiments) to highlight regimes in which the tunable bias–variance mechanism leads to clear performance advantages.

Conclusion  
Overall, this paper offers a novel and coherent unification of higher-order derivative estimation methods, enriched by a flexible Taylor expansion perspective and a new second-order estimator. While certain practical aspects—most notably the choice of K and the direct evaluation of bias versus variance—require further clarification, the core idea is sound and promising. The empirical gains on continuous control are modest, but the theoretical framework and initial results warrant publication. I therefore recommend a rating of 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold.

reviews.summary: The paper is concerned with meta reinforcement learning. The author(s) introduced a framework for learning high-order derivatives of value functions using off-policy evaluation. Specifically, the author(s) proposed to adopt existing off-policy evaluation methods to derive the value estimator and then take the derivative with respect to the target parameters to construct the estimate. This technique is further adopted to implement meta reinforcement learning.
reviews.main_review:
I have a few major comments regarding the proposed procedure.

1. The author(s) proposed to adopt importance sampling and doubly-robust estimators to evaluate the value function. These estimators suffer from the curse of horizon, leading to the sub-optimality of the learned optimal policy. Given that the author(s) considered an MDP setting, there are more efficient estimators for value evaluation (see, e.g., https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.12429; https://arxiv.org/abs/1909.05850).

2. Instead of constructing estimators for the value, it might be more efficient to directly evaluate the gradient of the value and its Hessian matrix. In the single agent setting, there are efficient off-policy policy gradient estimators developed in the literature (see, e.g., https://arxiv.org/abs/2002.04014).

3. I think the author(s) focused on offline meta reinforcement learning problems. It remains unclear to me how the proposed method would work when the positivity assumption (e.g., the policy ratio shall be bounded away from infinity) in the offline dataset is violated. In the single agent setting, the pessimism principle is commonly adopted to mitigate this issue. Would it be useful to combine the pessimism principle with the proposed method?
