reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper is concerned with meta reinforcement learning. The author(s) introduced a framework for learning high-order derivatives of value functions using off-policy evaluation. Specifically, the author(s) proposed to adopt existing off-policy evaluation methods to derive the value estimator and then take the derivative with respect to the target parameters to construct the estimate. This technique is further adopted to implement meta reinforcement learning.

Main Review:
I have a few major comments regarding the proposed procedure.

1. The author(s) proposed to adopt importance sampling and doubly-robust estimators to evaluate the value function. These estimators suffer from the curse of horizon, leading to the sub-optimality of the learned optimal policy. Given that the author(s) considered an MDP setting, there are more efficient estimators for value evaluation (see, e.g., https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.12429; https://arxiv.org/abs/1909.05850).

2. Instead of constructing estimators for the value, it might be more efficient to directly evaluate the gradient of the value and its Hessian matrix. In the single agent setting, there are efficient off-policy policy gradient estimators developed in the literature (see, e.g., https://arxiv.org/abs/2002.04014).

3. I think the author(s) focused on offline meta reinforcement learning problems. It remains unclear to me how the proposed method would work when the positivity assumption (e.g., the policy ratio shall be bounded away from infinity) in the offline dataset is violated. In the single agent setting, the pessimism principle is commonly adopted to mitigate this issue. Would it be useful to combine the pessimism principle with the proposed method?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The authors have discussed the limitations of their work on Page 6.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper proposes a unified framework for estimating higher-order derivatives of value functions in meta-reinforcement learning by leveraging off-policy evaluation (OPE) methods. Specifically, the authors suggest taking a standard OPE estimator—such as importance sampling (IS) or a doubly-robust (DR) estimator—and differentiating it with respect to the policy parameters to obtain gradients and Hessians of the value. The framework is then embedded into meta-RL algorithms to enable second‐order or higher‐order adaptation. The idea of reusing existing OPE techniques in this way is conceptually appealing and represents a neat unification of derivative estimation under a single roof.

Strengths  
1. General Framework: The paper introduces a broad, principle‐driven approach for obtaining high‐order derivatives of policy value functions purely via differentiation of off‐policy value estimates. This unifies first‐ and second‐order meta‐RL updates under the same methodology.  
2. Reuse of Existing Methods: By building on standard IS and DR evaluators, the approach connects to a large body of prior work and makes it easy to plug in any future advances in OPE.  
3. Integration with Meta‐RL: The authors clearly demonstrate how the derivative estimates can be slotted into typical meta‐learning pipelines, facilitating accelerated policy adaptation across tasks.

Weaknesses  
1. Curse of Horizon & Variance: Relying on IS and DR estimators means the proposed method inherits their high variance over long horizons. In practice, this may lead to poor or unstable meta‐policies in tasks with extended trajectories.  
2. Overlooked More Efficient OPE Methods: Recent works have developed value evaluators with substantially better sample complexity in MDPs (e.g., arXiv:1810.12429; arXiv:1909.05850). These estimators are not discussed or compared, leaving open whether they could alleviate the variance issues.  
3. Indirect Gradient/Hessian Estimation: Constructing a full value estimator and then differentiating it may be less efficient or accurate than designing estimators that directly target the policy gradient and Hessian (see, for instance, arXiv:2002.04014).  
4. Positivity Assumption in Offline Data: The framework assumes bounded importance weights (positivity), which is often violated in offline meta‐RL datasets. It is unclear how sensitive the method is to coverage gaps or extreme policy ratios.  
5. Lack of Pessimism: Contemporary offline RL typically incorporates pessimistic corrections to mitigate distributional mismatch. The current approach does not embed any such principle, raising concerns about robustness when logged data has limited task coverage.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Incorporate Advanced OPE Estimators: Evaluate or integrate alternative long‐horizon evaluators (e.g., those in arXiv:1810.12429, arXiv:1909.05850) to reduce variance and improve sample efficiency in the meta‐RL setting.  
2. Explore Direct Derivative Estimators: Consider methods that directly estimate the policy gradient and Hessian off‐policy (e.g., approaches from arXiv:2002.04014) rather than differentiating a value function estimator. This may yield more stable and accurate updates.  
3. Add Pessimistic Corrections: To handle coverage violations and distributional shift in offline data, augment the framework with a pessimism principle—such as penalty terms or confidence intervals—ensuring the estimated derivatives remain reliable under limited support.  
4. Empirical Study of Coverage Violations: Conduct experiments that vary the degree of positivity violation to assess how sensitive the derivative estimates are to changes in policy-ratio bounds.

Decision  
Rating: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)  
This work offers a compelling, general-purpose framework for high‐order derivative estimation via off‐policy evaluation and demonstrates integration into meta‐RL. However, key practical challenges—variance under long horizons, the existence of more efficient evaluators, indirectness of the differentiation approach, and lack of pessimistic safeguards—limit its robustness in offline settings. With attention to the above suggestions, the paper would make a stronger contribution. Given its conceptual novelty but also its current empirical and methodological gaps, I recommend marginal acceptance.

reviews.summary: This paper uses Huang and Jiang (2020) to talk about gradient-based meta learning in reinforcement learning using the language of off-policy evaluation. The logical connection behind those concepts stands from the fact that policy gradient methods in reinforcement learning are in fact instances of the likelihood ratio derivative estimator, which itself stems from important sampling. Proposition 3.2 and 3.3 are obtained from Huang and Jiang. Proposition 3.1 tries to extend their results to talk about higher order derivatives. The author's finally propose to use ideas from Taylor policy optimization from Tang et al. (2020) as a variance reduction method within the doubly robust policy gradient framework of Huang and Jiang.
reviews.main_review:
In my opinion the paper doesn't go deep enough into the individual ideas being put forward. The doubly robust estimator of Huang and Jiang is at the core of this paper.  In the specific context of meta learning, what does it allow us to say or do that we couldn't achieve before? Why is doubly robust necessary? The doubly robust being a variance reduction technique why this VRT in particular and not another one, in the meta learning context? Regarding the Taylor approximation, why is it related to the doubly robust estimator? The paper does not do a convincing job at showing that these two components strongly depend on each other: conceptually, or experimentally. Overall, the paper lacks cohesion and depth.

On line 83 the notation suggests that the expectation is not taken over x and only over the task g. While technically correct, you don't really have to write the objective as an expectation over the value function. Your objective should simply be the expected discounted or on discounted return, written plainly as a sum of rewards.

In equation 2, I would suggest using a different notation when you're writing nabla of theta prime. Theta prime should be a function, but the way that it's written suggests that it's a variable instead.

Another suggestion for notation would be to write "v of theta" (instead of a superscript) in equation 2. This way it would be clearer how you have to apply the chain rule later on.

Reading more carefully on line 92 and equation 1 now I realize that you assume a fixed initial distribution starting from x0. This is confusing because on line 83 you simply write v of x and it looks like x should be a random variable: your initial state. Perhaps you should state explicitly that you assume (without loss of generality) a given initial state.

On line 92, the expression the "Hessian of the value function" is misleading because really the Hessian that you're taking is that of the expected return objective, not that of the value function.

I think that the unnumbered equation below line 106 is redundant: it simply says that the two quantities are unbiased estimators. Words suffice here. Instead, I would suggest that you explain the nature of the issue. The correlation problem that you're describing comes from taking the expectation over a product of two random variables. The fact that it's not simply the product of the individual expectations and that here is also a covariance term.

I'm confused by proposition 3.1 especially around line 137 when you introduce a new notation for the estimator nabla hat v. What you have in this proposition should be something about a derivative estimator: score function/REINFORCE, reparametrization. Now you don't seem to be defining how you obtain this nabla theta m  v hat, and in that sense proposition 3.1 does not quite stand on its own.

On line 171 you allude to the fact that the variance of policy learning is essentially due to having a large number of importance sampling terms. While this is a reasonable intuition to have, note that we can show formally that PDIS-type estimators need not have larger variance than let's say stationary or marginal IS.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The limitation of the framework per se are not quite explored. The authors talk about what can be said within that framework (dice) but not quite what it cannot say. Experimentally the paper looks at the performance of the combination of doubly robust plus Taylor approximation as an algorithm of its own. It does not quite try to understand what the doubly robust formulation allows us to address specifically in meta learning. It would be interesting to gain more insights into the value of the proposed method by taking a more ablative approach. Since the authors pitch a framework as the main contribution, performance results matter much less than experimental insights at the conceptual level.  I think that the paper could be restructured around the algorithm and to de-emphasize the framework part. To go all in on the algorithm (which might be how things happened chronologically anyways).
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper uses Huang and Jiang (2020) to talk about gradient-based meta learning in reinforcement learning using the language of off-policy evaluation. The logical connection behind those concepts stands from the fact that policy gradient methods in reinforcement learning are in fact instances of the likelihood ratio derivative estimator, which itself stems from important sampling. Proposition 3.2 and 3.3 are obtained from Huang and Jiang. Proposition 3.1 tries to extend their results to talk about higher order derivatives. The author's finally propose to use ideas from Taylor policy optimization from Tang et al. (2020) as a variance reduction method within the doubly robust policy gradient framework of Huang and Jiang.

Main Review:
In my opinion the paper doesn't go deep enough into the individual ideas being put forward. The doubly robust estimator of Huang and Jiang is at the core of this paper.  In the specific context of meta learning, what does it allow us to say or do that we couldn't achieve before? Why is doubly robust necessary? The doubly robust being a variance reduction technique why this VRT in particular and not another one, in the meta learning context? Regarding the Taylor approximation, why is it related to the doubly robust estimator? The paper does not do a convincing job at showing that these two components strongly depend on each other: conceptually, or experimentally. Overall, the paper lacks cohesion and depth.

On line 83 the notation suggests that the expectation is not taken over x and only over the task g. While technically correct, you don't really have to write the objective as an expectation over the value function. Your objective should simply be the expected discounted or on discounted return, written plainly as a sum of rewards.

In equation 2, I would suggest using a different notation when you're writing nabla of theta prime. Theta prime should be a function, but the way that it's written suggests that it's a variable instead.

Another suggestion for notation would be to write "v of theta" (instead of a superscript) in equation 2. This way it would be clearer how you have to apply the chain rule later on.

Reading more carefully on line 92 and equation 1 now I realize that you assume a fixed initial distribution starting from x0. This is confusing because on line 83 you simply write v of x and it looks like x should be a random variable: your initial state. Perhaps you should state explicitly that you assume (without loss of generality) a given initial state.

On line 92, the expression the "Hessian of the value function" is misleading because really the Hessian that you're taking is that of the expected return objective, not that of the value function.

I think that the unnumbered equation below line 106 is redundant: it simply says that the two quantities are unbiased estimators. Words suffice here. Instead, I would suggest that you explain the nature of the issue. The correlation problem that you're describing comes from taking the expectation over a product of two random variables. The fact that it's not simply the product of the individual expectations and that here is also a covariance term.

I'm confused by proposition 3.1 especially around line 137 when you introduce a new notation for the estimator nabla hat v. What you have in this proposition should be something about a derivative estimator: score function/REINFORCE, reparametrization. Now you don't seem to be defining how you obtain this nabla theta m  v hat, and in that sense proposition 3.1 does not quite stand on its own.

On line 171 you allude to the fact that the variance of policy learning is essentially due to having a large number of importance sampling terms. While this is a reasonable intuition to have, note that we can show formally that PDIS-type estimators need not have larger variance than let's say stationary or marginal IS.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The limitation of the framework per se are not quite explored. The authors talk about what can be said within that framework (dice) but not quite what it cannot say. Experimentally the paper looks at the performance of the combination of doubly robust plus Taylor approximation as an algorithm of its own. It does not quite try to understand what the doubly robust formulation allows us to address specifically in meta learning. It would be interesting to gain more insights into the value of the proposed method by taking a more ablative approach. Since the authors pitch a framework as the main contribution, performance results matter much less than experimental insights at the conceptual level.  I think that the paper could be restructured around the algorithm and to de-emphasize the framework part. To go all in on the algorithm (which might be how things happened chronologically anyways).

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Active Voltage Control on Power Distribution Networks

reviews.main_review:
This paper is clearly written and well-organized. The main objective is to present/formulate a problem in power networks as an exciting and yet challenging real-world scenario for application of multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), developing an open-source environment of active voltage control for MARL, conducting some initial empirical study, and summarizing the main observations (including some key observed challenges/limitations of existing MARL techniques in such problem and some potential future research directions). It mainly targets for bridging the gap between the power community and MARL community in this research topic.

I think the problem is interesting and the paper contains valuable results. Bridging the gap between MARL community and power community is important, and the research work here might potentially motivate more future follow-up research in this direction and would also be helpful for real world large scale application of MARL in power networks.

However, for publication of this result on a top MARL/ML conference like NIPS, I'm still not fully convinced by the novelty and contribution of this study. The main contribution of this this paper is more about formulating the active voltage control problem in power network as MARL problem, conducted some initial study of existing MARL techniques in such problem, and identified some key limitations/difficulties there. If the authors could have some results where they addressed some of these identified limitations and made some improvements (which can be somewhat customized to such power network domain) to the existing MARL techniques, then I'd be more convinced that its contribution is sufficient for publication as a regular paper on such a top tier MARL/ML conference like NIPS.

On the other hand, to be clear, I do think this paper indeed studies an interesting problem and it contains valuable research results that might be good for publication at some good conference/journals, and it will be beneficial for future research, especially for interaction between power community and MARL community. But I just think the contribution and novelty in current manuscript might not be strong enough for publication as a regular paper on such a top tier MARL/ML conference like NIPS.

Update: I've read all the review discussions here and checked out all the detailed responses and clarifications from the authors. I'm slightly more convinced about the contribution and novelty of this paper, and I decided to upgrade my rating from 5 to 6. Though I do agree with several other reviewers that, even after the responses and clarifications from the authors, this paper still seems to be quite at the border line.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this work, the authors studied applying multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) to active voltage control in the power distribution networks, and targeting for bridging the gap between the power community and MARL community. Main contributions include: 
1) develop an open-source environment of active voltage control for MARL and formally define this problem as a
65 Dec-POMDP;
2) conduct large scale experimentation with 8 state-of-the-art MARL algorithms on different scenarios of voltage control problem;
3) found valuable observations, e.g.,  notice the importance of converting voltage constraints to auxiliary reward functions and verify the sensitivity of MARL performances to the auxiliary reward functions, with a new bowl-shape auxiliary reward proposed which stands out in the test results;
4) by analyzing the experimental results, the authors imply the possible difficulties of applying MARL and the further research directions. The authors also conducted the comparison with traditional control methods, and empirically show the rationality and hope to use MARL as a solution for active voltage control.

Main Review:
This paper is clearly written and well-organized. The main objective is to present/formulate a problem in power networks as an exciting and yet challenging real-world scenario for application of multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), developing an open-source environment of active voltage control for MARL, conducting some initial empirical study, and summarizing the main observations (including some key observed challenges/limitations of existing MARL techniques in such problem and some potential future research directions). It mainly targets for bridging the gap between the power community and MARL community in this research topic.

I think the problem is interesting and the paper contains valuable results. Bridging the gap between MARL community and power community is important, and the research work here might potentially motivate more future follow-up research in this direction and would also be helpful for real world large scale application of MARL in power networks.

However, for publication of this result on a top MARL/ML conference like NIPS, I'm still not fully convinced by the novelty and contribution of this study. The main contribution of this this paper is more about formulating the active voltage control problem in power network as MARL problem, conducted some initial study of existing MARL techniques in such problem, and identified some key limitations/difficulties there. If the authors could have some results where they addressed some of these identified limitations and made some improvements (which can be somewhat customized to such power network domain) to the existing MARL techniques, then I'd be more convinced that its contribution is sufficient for publication as a regular paper on such a top tier MARL/ML conference like NIPS.

On the other hand, to be clear, I do think this paper indeed studies an interesting problem and it contains valuable research results that might be good for publication at some good conference/journals, and it will be beneficial for future research, especially for interaction between power community and MARL community. But I just think the contribution and novelty in current manuscript might not be strong enough for publication as a regular paper on such a top tier MARL/ML conference like NIPS.

Update: I've read all the review discussions here and checked out all the detailed responses and clarifications from the authors. I'm slightly more convinced about the contribution and novelty of this paper, and I decided to upgrade my rating from 5 to 6. Though I do agree with several other reviewers that, even after the responses and clarifications from the authors, this paper still seems to be quite at the border line.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper tackles the active voltage control problem in distribution grids by framing it as a Decentralized POMDP (Dec-POMDP) and providing an open-source Python environment built on real-world data. The authors evaluate eight state-of-the-art MARL algorithms (IDDPG, MADDPG, COMA, IPPO, MAPPO, SQDDPG, MATD3) across three realistic scenarios. A key focus is on transforming voltage constraints into auxiliary rewards: the paper compares L1‐ and L2‐shape penalty functions against a novel “bowl‐shape” reward. Extensive experiments also benchmark MARL approaches against classic control methods—Optimal Power Flow (OPF) and droop control—using metrics such as controllable rate and power loss. The study surfaces important insights about reward‐shaping sensitivity, robustness, and interpretability, and outlines directions for future research. Overall, the manuscript is well written, well organized, and tackles a practically critical problem, but its contributions remain primarily empirical and descriptive rather than algorithmic or theoretical.  

Strengths  
1. Relevance and Impact: The paper addresses an important real-world challenge at the intersection of multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) and power systems control.  
2. Clear Formulation and Resources: By formally defining active voltage control as a Dec-POMDP and releasing an open-source environment, the work provides a valuable platform for the community.  
3. Comprehensive Empirical Study: The authors conduct large‐scale experiments with eight leading MARL algorithms, offering a broad view of current capabilities.  
4. Reward‐Shaping Insights: The comparison of L1, L2, and the proposed bowl-shape auxiliary rewards is well motivated; the bowl-shape function in particular yields consistently better voltage regulation.  
5. Baseline Comparisons: Empirical juxtaposition with OPF and droop control highlights both the promise and limitations of MARL in this domain.  
6. Thoughtful Analysis: The paper identifies key challenges—sensitivity to reward design, interpretability of learned policies, and robustness under varying conditions—and suggests fruitful avenues for future work.  

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Algorithmic Novelty: Beyond the problem formulation and reward-shaping function, there are no methodological advances or custom MARL algorithms tailored to power networks.  
2. Incremental Contribution for Top ML Venue: The main deliverables are an environment, a Dec-POMDP framing, and an empirical evaluation. In the absence of new learning techniques or theoretical analysis, the paper’s novelty is borderline for a premier ML conference.  
3. Descriptive Rather Than Prescriptive: While the empirical results are solid, the work stops short of proposing concrete algorithmic improvements that directly address the identified limitations (e.g., coordination mechanisms, scalability strategies).  

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Develop Domain-Specific MARL Enhancements: Incorporate extensions to existing algorithms (e.g., structured credit assignment, hierarchical coordination) that tackle the unique challenges of voltage control.  
2. Introduce Theoretical or Algorithmic Insights: Complement the empirical study with theoretical results (e.g., convergence guarantees under the proposed reward structures) or propose novel architectures tailored to power grid constraints.  
3. Demonstrate Performance Gains: Augment the current evaluation by showing that any introduced modifications yield statistically significant improvements over the baselines, both in simulation and, if possible, in small‐scale field tests.  

Conclusion and Recommendation  
This paper makes a valuable empirical contribution by bridging MARL and power systems, offering a formal Dec-POMDP framework, an open-source benchmark, and important insights on reward shaping. However, the absence of algorithmic or theoretical novelty limits its impact for a top‐tier ML conference. Overall, I recommend a marginally above-threshold acceptance (Rating: 6), contingent upon strengthening the paper’s methodological contributions in future revisions.

In this work, the authors studied applying multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) to active voltage control in the power distribution networks, and targeting for bridging the gap between the power community and MARL community. Main contributions include: 
1) develop an open-source environment of active voltage control for MARL and formally define this problem as a
65 Dec-POMDP;
2) conduct large scale experimentation with 8 state-of-the-art MARL algorithms on different scenarios of voltage control problem;
3) found valuable observations, e.g.,  notice the importance of converting voltage constraints to auxiliary reward functions and verify the sensitivity of MARL performances to the auxiliary reward functions, with a new bowl-shape auxiliary reward proposed which stands out in the test results;
4) by analyzing the experimental results, the authors imply the possible difficulties of applying MARL and the further research directions. The authors also conducted the comparison with traditional control methods, and empirically show the rationality and hope to use MARL as a solution for active voltage control.

Main Review:
This paper is clearly written and well-organized. The main objective is to present/formulate a problem in power networks as an exciting and yet challenging real-world scenario for application of multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), developing an open-source environment of active voltage control for MARL, conducting some initial empirical study, and summarizing the main observations (including some key observed challenges/limitations of existing MARL techniques in such problem and some potential future research directions). It mainly targets for bridging the gap between the power community and MARL community in this research topic.

I think the problem is interesting and the paper contains valuable results. Bridging the gap between MARL community and power community is important, and the research work here might potentially motivate more future follow-up research in this direction and would also be helpful for real world large scale application of MARL in power networks.

However, for publication of this result on a top MARL/ML conference like NIPS, I'm still not fully convinced by the novelty and contribution of this study. The main contribution of this this paper is more about formulating the active voltage control problem in power network as MARL problem, conducted some initial study of existing MARL techniques in such problem, and identified some key limitations/difficulties there. If the authors could have some results where they addressed some of these identified limitations and made some improvements (which can be somewhat customized to such power network domain) to the existing MARL techniques, then I'd be more convinced that its contribution is sufficient for publication as a regular paper on such a top tier MARL/ML conference like NIPS.

On the other hand, to be clear, I do think this paper indeed studies an interesting problem and it contains valuable research results that might be good for publication at some good conference/journals, and it will be beneficial for future research, especially for interaction between power community and MARL community. But I just think the contribution and novelty in current manuscript might not be strong enough for publication as a regular paper on such a top tier MARL/ML conference like NIPS.

Update: I've read all the review discussions here and checked out all the detailed responses and clarifications from the authors. I'm slightly more convinced about the contribution and novelty of this paper, and I decided to upgrade my rating from 5 to 6. Though I do agree with several other reviewers that, even after the responses and clarifications from the authors, this paper still seems to be quite at the border line.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

strengths: Addresses an important real‐world problem at the intersection of MARL and power systems.
Clearly written and well organized presentation.
Provides an open‐source environment and formally defines the active voltage control problem as a Dec‐POMDP.
Conducts large‐scale experiments with eight state‐of‐the‐art MARL algorithms.
Highlights the importance of converting voltage constraints into auxiliary reward functions and proposes a novel bowl‐shape function.
Offers valuable observations on algorithm sensitivity to reward shaping and suggests future research directions.
Includes empirical comparison with traditional control methods (OPF and droop control).
weaknesses: Lacks methodological or algorithmic contributions beyond problem formulation and empirical study.
Novelty is limited for a top‐tier ML conference—no customized MARL techniques addressing identified limitations.
Main contributions are descriptive and evaluative rather than providing new learning algorithms or theoretical insights.
Overall contribution may be borderline for acceptance without stronger innovations.
suggestions: Incorporate results that directly address the identified limitations by customizing or improving existing MARL methods for the power network domain.
Introduce algorithmic or theoretical innovations rather than focusing solely on problem setup and evaluation.
Strengthen the novelty by demonstrating how proposed changes (e.g., to reward shaping or coordination) yield clear performance gains over baselines.
main_points: The paper successfully formulates active voltage control as a Dec‐POMDP and releases an open‐source MARL environment.
Extensive experiments show how different auxiliary reward shapes affect MARL performance, with the proposed bowl‐shape reward standing out.
The study identifies key challenges in applying MARL to power systems (e.g., sensitivity to reward design, interpretability, robustness).
Comparison with traditional methods (OPF, droop control) underscores MARL’s potential but also its current limitations.

technical_details: Formal definition of the active voltage control problem as a Dec‐POMDP.
Development of a Python‐based open‐source MARL environment with three real‐data scenarios.
Evaluation of eight state‐of‐the‐art MARL algorithms: IDDPG, MADDPG, COMA, IPPO, MAPPO, SQDDPG, MATD3.
Design and comparison of three voltage barrier (auxiliary reward) functions: L1‐shape, L2‐shape, and the proposed bowl‐shape.
Empirical comparison against Optimal Power Flow (OPF) and droop control baselines over multiple network topologies.
Analysis of controllable rate (CR) and power loss (PL) metrics to assess algorithm performance and reward sensitivity.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a new environment for multi-agent reinforcement learning that addresses the problem of active voltage control on electric power distribution networks. The authors formulate the voltage control problem as a Dec-POMDP, and implement a number of different MARL baselines to assess their performance on the environment. They also assess the effect of different voltage loss functions on this environment.
reviews.main_review:
I am very much on the borderline regarding this paper. On the one hand, I think it addresses a very important problem, and that it would be extremely beneficial to both the ML/MARL and power systems communities to have this kind of distribution system control environment readily available. On the other hand, I think one of the key assumptions underpinning the current framework needs to be better justified, that there should be more details about the actual usability of the environment, and that some of the technical notations and explanations need to be cleaned up.

Key assumption: One of my main concerns is regarding the assumption made in the paper that multiple inverters are controlled by one company that has full information within its operational region. I do not believe this model is common in present distribution networks -- that is, where smart inverter control exists, it is generally the case that each inverter operates separately based on local information. The authors should provide much more context around this assumption (e.g., how does this grid operation mode relate to existing grid operation modes?), both to avoid inadvertently misleading the ML community about the state of current grid operations, and to better justify the technical assumptions on which the rest of the work is based.

Usage instructions: Since the proposed contribution is an environment with baselines, I was surprised to see no details relating to the practical usability of the environment. For instance, would this environment be integrated within existing MARL frameworks, and/or does it have a user interface that is similar? Is the plan to provide a quickstart guide to help users easily get started? These kinds of details are important to understanding whether this kind of environment would actually be able to be widely adopted, or whether ML researchers/practitioners would find it difficult to use in practice.

Terminology and explanations: As one of the goals of the paper is to make the voltage control setting accessible to ML practitioners, the authors should be careful to go through and make sure all power systems terminology and assumptions are properly defined, e.g.,:
* Line 92: Define "power curtailment"
* Line 127--129: It would be important to clarify why and when the distribution network can be modeled as a tree graph (especially since the 33-bus system evaluated is actually not a tree, though the authors mention in the appendix that they modify it to remove loops).
* Line 140: Define "per unit"

Other questions and comments:
* Lines 41--42: Voltage and power are indeed related, through the power flow equations, which means that the constraint and control action are indeed linked. Would it be possible to clarify what is meant by this statement?
* Line 140: "When the load is heavy during the nighttime" -- Why is load heavier at night, especially during seasons with no HVAC? Or is it just meant that *net* load is heavy due to low availability of PV power at night?
* Line 184--186: The current notation for $\mathcal{R}$ implies that it is a set of sets, rather than a union of sets.
* Line 191: What is meant by "at the last step"?
* Line 205, "State Transition Probability Function": The notation here should be cleaned up, as $\delta$ outputs a state but $Pr(s_{t+1}|s_{t})$ outputs a probability, so it's not immediately clear to me how $\mathcal{T}$ is actually defined.
* Line 339--340: More details should be given on how and why MARL beats droop control methods.

Minor:
* Line 193: "radius" --> "radians"
* Lines 194 and 195: "measures" --> "measurements"
* Line 218: Since "loss" can have multiple meanings in this context, it may be worth clarify that "loss" is used in the ML context here.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes, the authors have adequately addressed these points.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a new environment for multi-agent reinforcement learning that addresses the problem of active voltage control on electric power distribution networks. The authors formulate the voltage control problem as a Dec-POMDP, and implement a number of different MARL baselines to assess their performance on the environment. They also assess the effect of different voltage loss functions on this environment.

Main Review:
I am very much on the borderline regarding this paper. On the one hand, I think it addresses a very important problem, and that it would be extremely beneficial to both the ML/MARL and power systems communities to have this kind of distribution system control environment readily available. On the other hand, I think one of the key assumptions underpinning the current framework needs to be better justified, that there should be more details about the actual usability of the environment, and that some of the technical notations and explanations need to be cleaned up.

Key assumption: One of my main concerns is regarding the assumption made in the paper that multiple inverters are controlled by one company that has full information within its operational region. I do not believe this model is common in present distribution networks -- that is, where smart inverter control exists, it is generally the case that each inverter operates separately based on local information. The authors should provide much more context around this assumption (e.g., how does this grid operation mode relate to existing grid operation modes?), both to avoid inadvertently misleading the ML community about the state of current grid operations, and to better justify the technical assumptions on which the rest of the work is based.

Usage instructions: Since the proposed contribution is an environment with baselines, I was surprised to see no details relating to the practical usability of the environment. For instance, would this environment be integrated within existing MARL frameworks, and/or does it have a user interface that is similar? Is the plan to provide a quickstart guide to help users easily get started? These kinds of details are important to understanding whether this kind of environment would actually be able to be widely adopted, or whether ML researchers/practitioners would find it difficult to use in practice.

Terminology and explanations: As one of the goals of the paper is to make the voltage control setting accessible to ML practitioners, the authors should be careful to go through and make sure all power systems terminology and assumptions are properly defined, e.g.,:
* Line 92: Define "power curtailment"
* Line 127--129: It would be important to clarify why and when the distribution network can be modeled as a tree graph (especially since the 33-bus system evaluated is actually not a tree, though the authors mention in the appendix that they modify it to remove loops).
* Line 140: Define "per unit"

Other questions and comments:
* Lines 41--42: Voltage and power are indeed related, through the power flow equations, which means that the constraint and control action are indeed linked. Would it be possible to clarify what is meant by this statement?
* Line 140: "When the load is heavy during the nighttime" -- Why is load heavier at night, especially during seasons with no HVAC? Or is it just meant that *net* load is heavy due to low availability of PV power at night?
* Line 184--186: The current notation for $\mathcal{R}$ implies that it is a set of sets, rather than a union of sets.
* Line 191: What is meant by "at the last step"?
* Line 205, "State Transition Probability Function": The notation here should be cleaned up, as $\delta$ outputs a state but $Pr(s_{t+1}|s_{t})$ outputs a probability, so it's not immediately clear to me how $\mathcal{T}$ is actually defined.
* Line 339--340: More details should be given on how and why MARL beats droop control methods.

Minor:
* Line 193: "radius" --> "radians"
* Lines 194 and 195: "measures" --> "measurements"
* Line 218: Since "loss" can have multiple meanings in this context, it may be worth clarify that "loss" is used in the ML context here.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes, the authors have adequately addressed these points.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review for “An Open-Source Multi-Agent RL Environment for Active Voltage Control in Distribution Networks”

Summary  
This paper presents a novel open‐source environment and accompanying baselines for decentralized active voltage control in power distribution networks, formulated as a Dec-POMDP. The authors evaluate several state‐of‐the‐art multi‐agent reinforcement learning (MARL) algorithms and study how different voltage barrier (loss) functions impact learning. This contribution is relevant to both the ML/MARL and power‐systems communities. The work is well motivated, and the open‐source release should foster further research at this intersection. However, several key modeling assumptions, notational choices, and missing practical details temper enthusiasm.

Strengths  
- Tackles an important real‐world problem at the intersection of MARL and power systems.  
- Provides a new open‐source environment and baseline implementations for decentralized reactive power control via inverters.  
- Formulates the problem as a Dec-POMDP and benchmarks multiple modern MARL algorithms.  
- Investigates the influence of various voltage barrier (loss) functions on agent performance, offering insight into reward design.

Weaknesses and Main Concerns  
1. Centralized Control Assumption  
   The environment assumes a single operator controls all inverters with full regional information—a setup uncommon in real distribution networks. This strong assumption needs better justification or discussion of scenarios (e.g., microgrids, community energy schemes) where it might hold.  
2. Practical Usage Details  
   The manuscript lacks guidance on how to integrate the environment with popular MARL toolkits, usage examples, or a quickstart guide. Without these, adoption by the ML community could be limited.  
3. Terminology and Jargon  
   Many power‐systems terms (e.g., “power curtailment,” “per‐unit”) and units are not defined for an ML audience. An interdisciplinary paper must define such terms upon first use.  
4. Modeling Choices and Test Case Modifications  
   Treating the distribution network as a tree graph is reasonable but the rationale and any modifications to standard test cases (e.g., the 33‐bus network) are not clearly explained.  
5. Unclear or Inconsistent Notation  
   - The region set ℛ is denoted as a set of sets but should reflect a union.  
   - The phrase “at the last step” in the reactive power variable definition is ambiguous.  
   - The state transition probability notation (δ versus Pr) is inconsistent and confusing.  
6. Insufficient Explanations  
   - The linkage between voltage constraints and reactive power actions via power‐flow equations needs clearer exposition (Lines 41–42).  
   - The claim of heavier nighttime load despite low PV output requires justification.  
7. Lack of Comparative Analysis  
   The experiments note MARL outperforms traditional droop control, but quantitative comparisons and explanations for the performance gap are sparse.  
8. Minor Typos and Clarifications  
   Several minor fixes are needed, such as “radius” → “radians,” “measures” → “measurements,” and a clearer distinction of “loss” in ML (negative reward) versus power systems (real power loss).

Suggestions for Improvement  
- Provide context or literature justification for the single‐operator inverter control assumption, or frame it as one scenario among several.  
- Include a quickstart guide and code examples demonstrating integration with standard MARL frameworks (e.g., RLlib, OpenAI Gym).  
- Define all power‐systems terminology and units at first mention.  
- Clarify the tree‐graph model: specify when this approximation holds and detail any alterations to benchmark cases.  
- Expand on the power‐flow linkage and nighttime load patterns with references or supplementary material.  
- Revise notation throughout—ensure ℛ, δ, Pr, and “last step” are consistent and unambiguous.  
- Enrich the experimental section with numerical results comparing MARL agents to droop control, and discuss why MARL yields improvements.  
- Correct minor typographical errors and unify the use of “loss” across domains.

Decision: 6 – Marginally above the acceptance threshold  
This work makes a valuable contribution by releasing an open‐source MARL environment for voltage control and by benchmarking modern algorithms. With stronger justification of modeling assumptions, clearer notation, and practical usage guidance, the paper would be well positioned to have significant impact. I recommend acceptance after revision to address the points above.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces a new open-source environment for training RL agents on controlling the voltage of a power distribution network. The problem is formulated as a multi-agent Dec-POMDP. Agents control PV inverters using only local measurements coming from sensors situated in the region they operate. Their task is to maintain the voltage within safety range. Several MARL algorithms are evaluated in three different power networks. The paper also investigates different reward functions.
reviews.main_review:
The main contribution of this work is the introduction of a new benchmark domain for testing MARL algorithms. This consists of a simulator of a power network. The environment is built using existent network topologies and real data. I definitely agree with the authors' view on the importance of having realistic scenarios where RL methods can be tested appropriately before using them in real-world applications. It is very unfortunate that the code is not provided in the supplementary material. It is hard to assess how useful this work will be for the community without being able to test the environment.

I miss some intuitive explanation of the dynamics of the problem. Some technical details are given in the Appendix but these are hard to follow for someone lacking in knowledge of the field. In particular, I am looking for details like, how much past information about the network state is useful for making decisions. How do the actions taken in the past affect the network in the long run. I.e. what's the horizon of the problem? All this could be illustrated using some sample trajectories coming from the standard operation of the network. To what extent do the changes in the policies of the agents in one region affect other regions? I.e how strongly coupled are the different regions in the network? This information could shed some light on why some MARL algorithms seem to perform better than others.

It is not clear whether an agent controls one or multiple PV inverters. Why are there multiple agents for each region? As far as I can see, all agents in a region receive the same information. If that’s the case, wouldn’t it be more effective to have all PV inverters within a region be controlled by a single policy? 

The environment is partially observable, yet according to Section 5: MARL Algorithm Settings (line 283-287) the policies are modeled with MLPs receiving only the last two observations as input. Using only the last two observations doesn’t seem sufficient to satisfy the Markov assumption, especially considering the intrinsic non-stationarity of the problem (power generation and consumption fluctuate during the day).

Minor details:

h is first defined as a function that maps nodes to the corresponding subset of measures for that node (line 195) and then also used as the history length (line 228).
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
Review for “Benchmarking Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning in Active Voltage Control of Power Distribution Networks”

Summary  
This manuscript presents the design and evaluation of an open-source, realistic benchmark environment for testing multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) methods in active voltage control of power distribution systems. The authors construct their simulator using real-world feeder topologies and historical photovoltaic (PV) generation data, and they compare several MARL algorithms across three distribution network scenarios with different reward (barrier) functions. The stated goal is to foster reproducible research and to bridge the gap between simulation and field deployment of MARL-based control strategies.

Strengths  
1. Realistic Benchmark Domain: The paper introduces a novel domain for MARL that closely reflects real-world voltage regulation challenges in distribution networks.  
2. Use of Real Data and Topologies: By leveraging existing feeder layouts and historical PV profiles, the environment achieves a high degree of fidelity.  
3. Emphasis on Practical Relevance: The authors rightly stress the need for in-depth simulation studies before moving to costly and risky field trials, underscoring the paper’s potential impact on both research and industrial practice.

Weaknesses and Concerns  
1. Missing Code and Reproducibility: Although the benchmark is advertised as open-source, the code and environment implementation are not included in the supplementary material. This omission directly undermines reproducibility and community uptake.  
2. Insufficient Intuitive Exposition: Key aspects of the voltage-control problem—such as the effective decision horizon, coupling between different network regions, and long-term state-action dependencies—are not explained with intuitive examples or sample trajectories.  
3. Buried Technical Details: Crucial algorithmic and modeling decisions (e.g., definition of observation history, overloading of the function “h”) are relegated to the appendix, making them hard to parse for readers not already expert in power systems.  
4. Agent Design Ambiguity: It is unclear exactly how many PV inverters each agent controls and why multiple agents are assigned per region when they appear to receive identical sensor measurements.  
5. Questionable Observation Model: Section 5 describes policies implemented as MLPs that ingest only the last two observations. In a partially observable, non-stationary environment, such limited history likely violates the Markov assumption and may impair learning of long-horizon dependencies.

Recommendations for Improvement  
1. Provide Code in Supplementary Material: Including the full implementation of the environment and training scripts is essential for validation and future extensions by other researchers.  
2. Add Intuitive Descriptions: Incorporate sample trajectories or illustrative scenarios that show how actions propagate through the network over time, clarify the decision horizon, and highlight inter-region coupling effects.  
3. Clarify Agent-to-Inverter Mapping: Explicitly state how many inverters each agent controls, and justify the design choice of multiple agents per region versus a single policy controlling all inverters in that region.  
4. Reevaluate Policy Granularity: If all agents in a region observe the same measurements, consider whether a single shared policy per region would reduce redundancy and simplify coordination.  
5. Enhance Observation History or Memory: To better approximate the underlying Dec-POMDP, extend the input history beyond two time steps or incorporate recurrent/memory-augmented policy architectures.

Decision: 6 – Marginally above the acceptance threshold  
This work addresses an important gap by offering a more realistic MARL benchmark for voltage control. However, the current manuscript falls short in its reproducibility and clarity of exposition. With the suggested revisions—especially the release of code, clearer problem descriptions, and stronger justification of agent design—I believe the paper could become a valuable resource for the community. I therefore recommend borderline acceptance, contingent on the authors addressing the issues outlined above.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper introduces a new open-source environment for training RL agents on controlling the voltage of a power distribution network. The problem is formulated as a multi-agent Dec-POMDP. Agents control PV inverters using only local measurements coming from sensors situated in the region they operate. Their task is to maintain the voltage within safety range. Several MARL algorithms are evaluated in three different power networks. The paper also investigates different reward functions.

Main Review:
The main contribution of this work is the introduction of a new benchmark domain for testing MARL algorithms. This consists of a simulator of a power network. The environment is built using existent network topologies and real data. I definitely agree with the authors' view on the importance of having realistic scenarios where RL methods can be tested appropriately before using them in real-world applications. It is very unfortunate that the code is not provided in the supplementary material. It is hard to assess how useful this work will be for the community without being able to test the environment.

I miss some intuitive explanation of the dynamics of the problem. Some technical details are given in the Appendix but these are hard to follow for someone lacking in knowledge of the field. In particular, I am looking for details like, how much past information about the network state is useful for making decisions. How do the actions taken in the past affect the network in the long run. I.e. what's the horizon of the problem? All this could be illustrated using some sample trajectories coming from the standard operation of the network. To what extent do the changes in the policies of the agents in one region affect other regions? I.e how strongly coupled are the different regions in the network? This information could shed some light on why some MARL algorithms seem to perform better than others.

It is not clear whether an agent controls one or multiple PV inverters. Why are there multiple agents for each region? As far as I can see, all agents in a region receive the same information. If that’s the case, wouldn’t it be more effective to have all PV inverters within a region be controlled by a single policy? 

The environment is partially observable, yet according to Section 5: MARL Algorithm Settings (line 283-287) the policies are modeled with MLPs receiving only the last two observations as input. Using only the last two observations doesn’t seem sufficient to satisfy the Markov assumption, especially considering the intrinsic non-stationarity of the problem (power generation and consumption fluctuate during the day).

Minor details:

h is first defined as a function that maps nodes to the corresponding subset of measures for that node (line 195) and then also used as the history length (line 228).

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The main contribution of the article is mention in the abstract "... establishes an open-source environment. It aims to bridge the gap between the power community and the MARL community". and In section 1: "There is no commonly accepted benchmark to provide the basis for fair comparison of different solutions. To facilitate further research on this topic, and to bridge the gap between the power community and MARL community", The authors seem to forget about L2RPN.
reviews.main_review:
- The authors say in section 1 "we present a comprehensive test-bench and open-source environment for MARL based active voltage control". The effort for standardization is not good enough because they do not seem to know what measures should be taken to this. If there has to be a standard framework for RL based learning of smart grids then it must include the following specifications
    - Definition of actions and their domains
    - Definition of states and effect of grid-users on the states
    - Definition of environment and its standard response to actions
    - Define standards for randomness and noise in states, observations, actions, and their effects. 
- The algorithms ànd networks need to be fully customizable. 


- Formulation is sketchy. The actions are only dependent on the reactive power, while other researchers have used many different types of actions. Restricting the algorithm to limited actions will definitely affect the results, but the authors do not seem to realize that. In section 5.3 they describe the difference of results between their algorithms and traditional solutions, but they seem to forget that they are not using the complete range of actions here. 

- The authors seem to forget that the network layers matter a lot with respect to the algorithm, see (https://openreview.net/forum?id=LmUJqB1Cz8)

- The authors also seem to have forgotten the effect of replay selection on the results. I conclude this by their choice reward function which they name as bowl function, which could also be a parabolic function too. Probably this is why they have doubts about their statements e.g. line 302-303, and section 5.3.

- The implementation details mentioned in the appendix do not add much to the article.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: They need to be more elaborate about the limitations of the proposed technique/framework, secondly they should clearly mention the limitations of their solution within the proposed framework.

Summary:
The main contribution of the article is mention in the abstract "... establishes an open-source environment. It aims to bridge the gap between the power community and the MARL community". and In section 1: "There is no commonly accepted benchmark to provide the basis for fair comparison of different solutions. To facilitate further research on this topic, and to bridge the gap between the power community and MARL community", The authors seem to forget about L2RPN.

Main Review:
- The authors say in section 1 "we present a comprehensive test-bench and open-source environment for MARL based active voltage control". The effort for standardization is not good enough because they do not seem to know what measures should be taken to this. If there has to be a standard framework for RL based learning of smart grids then it must include the following specifications
    - Definition of actions and their domains
    - Definition of states and effect of grid-users on the states
    - Definition of environment and its standard response to actions
    - Define standards for randomness and noise in states, observations, actions, and their effects. 
- The algorithms ànd networks need to be fully customizable. 


- Formulation is sketchy. The actions are only dependent on the reactive power, while other researchers have used many different types of actions. Restricting the algorithm to limited actions will definitely affect the results, but the authors do not seem to realize that. In section 5.3 they describe the difference of results between their algorithms and traditional solutions, but they seem to forget that they are not using the complete range of actions here. 

- The authors seem to forget that the network layers matter a lot with respect to the algorithm, see (https://openreview.net/forum?id=LmUJqB1Cz8)

- The authors also seem to have forgotten the effect of replay selection on the results. I conclude this by their choice reward function which they name as bowl function, which could also be a parabolic function too. Probably this is why they have doubts about their statements e.g. line 302-303, and section 5.3.

- The implementation details mentioned in the appendix do not add much to the article.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
They need to be more elaborate about the limitations of the proposed technique/framework, secondly they should clearly mention the limitations of their solution within the proposed framework.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper presents an open-source multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) test-bench aimed at enabling active voltage control in power networks. The authors’ goal of creating a common evaluation framework to unite the power-systems and MARL communities is commendable. However, key elements of prior work, precise environment standardization, problem formulation depth, and critical discussions of limitations are missing. As a result, I recommend a rating of 5 (marginally below the acceptance threshold).

Strengths  
- Introduces a publicly available MARL environment for active voltage control, which can foster reproducibility.  
- Explicitly seeks to bridge the gap between power-systems researchers and the MARL community.  
- Raises awareness of the need for a shared benchmark to enable fair algorithmic comparisons.

Weaknesses  
- Related work omission: existing test environments such as L2RPN are not referenced or compared, limiting context.  
- Insufficient standardization: the paper lacks precise definitions of action domains, state representations, environment response functions, and noise models.  
- Sketchy problem formulation: actions are restricted solely to reactive power adjustments, biasing the control setup.  
- Network architecture effects are not discussed, despite their known influence on MARL performance and scalability.  
- Replay buffer selection—which can drastically affect learning outcomes—is neither analyzed nor reported.  
- The choice of a “bowl-shaped” reward function is described but not justified through comparative analysis; it may reduce to a simple parabolic penalty.  
- Many implementation details are deferred to an appendix without adding substantial clarity or reproducibility.  
- The manuscript omits any discussion of framework limitations or potential societal impacts.

Detailed Suggestions  
1. Related Benchmarks and Baselines  
   - Reference and empirically compare your environment against existing platforms (e.g., L2RPN), highlighting similarities and differences in scenario design and performance metrics.  
2. Environment Specification  
   - Provide formal definitions for:  
     • Action spaces (domains and allowable ranges)  
     • State observations (voltage measurements, grid topology, loads)  
     • Environment response dynamics (power-flow equations, stochastic noise)  
   - Clearly document randomness sources and seeding procedures.  
3. Action-Space Extension  
   - Expand beyond reactive power control to include other control levers (e.g., tap changers, capacitor banks) and discuss trade-offs.  
4. Algorithm and Architecture Customization  
   - Design the test-bench to allow users to plug in arbitrary MARL algorithms and neural-network architectures; document interface points.  
5. Network Layer Discussion  
   - Analyze how different agent-network topologies or message-passing layers affect learning speed and scalability.  
6. Replay Buffer Analysis  
   - Experiment with multiple replay-buffer strategies (e.g., prioritized vs. uniform) and report their impact on convergence and policy quality.  
7. Reward Function Justification  
   - Compare your bowl-shaped barrier function against alternative reward designs (linear, quadratic penalties) to validate its chosen form.  
8. Limitations & Societal Impact  
   - Add a dedicated section discussing known shortcomings (e.g., scalability to large grids, communication delays) and broader societal implications (grid reliability, cybersecurity).

Conclusion  
The paper addresses an important need for standardized MARL environments in power systems and delivers an open-source baseline, but it falls short in situating itself among existing benchmarks, rigorously defining its framework, and critically assessing design choices. With substantial revisions along the lines above, the work could become a valuable community resource. At present, I recommend a score of 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold.

title: Differential Privacy of Dirichlet Posterior Sampling

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a method for releasing differentially private samples from Dirichlet posterior distribution. Authors present a privacy analysis for their proposed method and demonstrate that the method out performs Gaussian mechanism under certain conditions. Besides the empirical evaluation, authors also derive analytical utility guarantees for the method.
reviews.main_review:
In this paper authors propose a method that draws a single sample from Dirichlet posterior distribution. The method is rather simple and draws from Dir$(x + \alpha)$, which is the posterior of multinomial observations $x$ given a Dirichlet prior Dir$(\alpha)$ for the multinomial probabilities. The privacy of the method is analysed with respect to the prior parameter $\alpha$ using truncanted CDP. Authors consider two use cases for this method (which I think are rather similar), and derive analytical expressions for the utility of these cases. Finally authors demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms Gaussian method in a histogram release task when the number of samples is small.

The analysis of the paper seems novel to me. I appreciate the authors' efforts for deriving analytical expressions between the privacy and utility. I can also see a use case for such method (for example the histogram release demonstrated in Sec 4.2), however I think the use cases might be rather limited. Thus I think the method might not be the most striking from usage perspective, but with the analysis it is a solid privacy paper.

Pros:
- (+++) Very clearly written paper
- (++) Novel privacy analysis in terms of Dirichlet prior parameter $\alpha$
- (++) Utility analysis in terms of sample size
- (+) Emprical results showing improvement over Gaussian mech.

Cons:
- (--) Use case might be limited
- (--) I'm not sure what is the actual difference between uses in 4.1 and 4.2

Minor comments/questions:
- Introduction, lines 30-32. Are you talking about drawing from posterior predictive? In that case I think Y should also be drawn for each sample.
- Theorem 1, proof. Is there really $\alpha'$ separate from $\alpha$ or is it a typo?
- Figure 2, I guess it would make more sense to call the y-axis $\ell_2$-error instead of $\ell_2$-accuracy. For me it seems odd to say that "We see that the $\ell_2$-accuracy of the Dirichlet mechanism is smaller than that of the Gaussian mechanism for small N when d = 1000.", when you actually want to point out that your method works better in the small N domain, i.e. it's more "accurate".
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper presents a private histogram release mechanism based on a single draw from a Dirichlet posterior, namely sampling from Dirichlet(x + α). The authors provide a novel truncated concentrated differential privacy (tCDP) analysis that directly ties the privacy guarantee to the Dirichlet prior parameters α. They further derive closed-form utility bounds—expressed in terms of ℓ₂-error and sample complexity—and demonstrate empirically that their mechanism can outperform the standard Gaussian mechanism in the small-N regime. Overall, the paper is well written, theoretically solid, and provides both analytical and experimental evidence of its value. I recommend acceptance (Rating: 7: Good paper, accept).

Strengths  
1. Clarity of Presentation: The manuscript is very clearly written, with logical flow and precise notation throughout. The description of the mechanism and its analysis is easy to follow.  
2. Novel Privacy Analysis: The paper’s tCDP analysis linking the Dirichlet prior α to the privacy parameters is original and sheds new light on how Bayesian priors can be used for privacy guarantees.  
3. Analytical Utility Characterization: The authors derive explicit bounds on the expected ℓ₂-error and sample complexity for their mechanism, giving concrete guidance on how performance scales with data size and prior strength.  
4. Empirical Validation: The experiments on histogram release convincingly show that, for small to moderate N, the Dirichlet sampler yields lower error than the Gaussian mechanism under comparable privacy budgets.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Practical Use Cases: While the theoretical and synthetic experiments are strong, the paper provides only a couple of illustrative applications. It would help readers gauge real-world impact if additional concrete use cases were discussed.  
2. Unclear Distinction Between Applications: Sections 4.1 and 4.2 present two application scenarios that appear very similar in setup and outcome. The paper does not clearly explain why both are needed or how they differ substantively.

Technical Suggestions  
1. Posterior Predictive Sampling (Intro, lines 30–32): Please clarify whether “drawing from the posterior predictive” entails sampling a separate Y for each data point or a single aggregate draw. A brief remark here will help avoid confusion.  
2. Theorem 1 Proof (Typo in α): In the proof of Theorem 1, there is mention of α′ in one step—should this be the same as α throughout? Consistent notation will prevent misunderstanding.  
3. Figure 2 Axis Label: Relabel the y-axis as “ℓ₂-error” rather than “ℓ₂-accuracy” to accurately reflect the metric being plotted.  
4. Distinguish Applications: In Section 4, add a paragraph that explicitly contrasts the objectives, data assumptions, or domain contexts of the two use cases. Explaining why each application presents a unique challenge will strengthen the paper’s practical relevance.

Conclusion  
This work makes a solid privacy contribution by marrying a simple Bayesian sampling mechanism with novel tCDP analysis and clear utility guarantees, complemented by empirical evidence of improved performance over Gaussian noise in certain regimes. Addressing the above suggestions will further clarify and enhance the impact of the paper. I therefore recommend acceptance.
